questions.stem: 50-YEAR-OLD PATIENT: RECURRENT ATTACKS OF VERTIGO < 1 MINUTE, precipitated by TURNING IN BED, NO otological or neurological symptoms. Q1: What is your professional diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Meniere attacks last 20 min–12 hours + SNHL + tinnitus. NOT <1 minute positional.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Single prolonged episode lasting days. Not brief positional attacks.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Classic BPPV: brief (<1 min) attacks triggered by head position changes (turning in bed). Displaced OTOCONIA in posterior semicircular canal (85% of cases). No hearing loss, no tinnitus.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Prolonged vertigo WITH sensorineural hearing loss. Post-viral.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Causes progressive CONDUCTIVE hearing loss from stapes fixation. NOT vertigo.

questions.keyPoint: BPPV = Brief (<1 min) Positional Vertigo triggered by head movement, NO hearing loss, NO tinnitus. POSTERIOR canal in 85%. Diagnosis = DIX-HALLPIKE (delayed geotropic rotatory nystagmus, fatigable). Treatment = EPLEY'S MANOEUVRE.

questions.stem: Same 50-year-old BPPV patient. Q2: What is the diagnostic test you will perform for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: GOLD STANDARD for BPPV. Patient rapidly moved from sitting to lying with head 45° to affected side. POSITIVE = delayed (1-5 sec) ROTATORY TORSIONAL NYSTAGMUS that FATIGUES with repeated testing. KEY: latency + fatiguability = peripheral (BPPV).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Marching in place with eyes closed — detects unilateral vestibular loss. Not specific for BPPV.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Rinne and Weber tests assess HEARING LOSS (conductive vs SNHL). Not for vertigo.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Tests for perilymphatic fistula (pressure-induced vertigo). Not for BPPV. Page 2 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: For CENTRAL vertigo. BPPV is peripheral — does not need cerebellar assessment for diagnosis.

questions.keyPoint: DIX-HALLPIKE: Delayed (1-5 sec) rotatory nystagmus + FATIGABLE = BPPV (peripheral). CENTRAL vertigo: no latency, no fatigue, direction-changing nystagmus, vertical nystagmus. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: FATIGUABILITY.

questions.stem: Same 50-year-old BPPV patient. Q3: How are you going to treat this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Treats symptom not cause. May delay vestibular compensation. Not definitive.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: For Meniere disease and sudden SNHL. No role in BPPV.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: For Otitis Media with Effusion (glue ear). No role in BPPV.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Last-resort surgery for intractable Meniere disease. Excessive for BPPV.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: EPLEY'S CANALITH REPOSITIONING PROCEDURE is FIRST-LINE DEFINITIVE TREATMENT for posterior canal BPPV. Series of head rotations moves otoconia from posterior semicircular canal back into the utricle. 80-90% success rate with single treatment. Simple, safe, non-pharmacological.

questions.keyPoint: EPLEY'S MANOEUVRE = definitive treatment for BPPV. Repositions otoconia from posterior semicircular canal into utricle. 80-90% cure with single treatment. Brandt-Daroff exercises = home alternative. Posterior canal OCCLUSION = surgical last resort.

questions.stem: 2-YEAR-OLD GIRL: DROOPY UPPER EYELID RIGHT SIDE covering the pupil, noticed at 6 months, ABSENCE OF EYELID CREASE. 1. The cause of this ptosis is most likely:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CN III palsy = dilated pupil + ophthalmoplegia. Horner = miosis + anhidrosis. Neither fits this pattern.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CONGENITAL PTOSIS = levator palpebrae superioris muscle DYSGENESIS. KEY SIGN = ABSENT EYELID CREASE (levator cannot pull skin up). Present from birth/infancy, usually unilateral. High risk of AMBLYOPIA if pupil covered.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: From mass weighing on lid (neurofibromatosis, giant papillary conjunctivitis). No mass described here.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Post-surgical or post-injection. Not applicable in a 2-year-old.

questions.keyPoint: CONGENITAL PTOSIS: ABSENT EYELID CREASE (pathognomonic) + present from birth/infancy + due to levator palpebrae superioris DYSGENESIS. URGENT treatment if covering pupil → prevent DEPRIVATION AMBLYOPIA.

questions.stem: Same 2-year-old girl with congenital ptosis. 2. The severity of ptosis can be determined by the following test:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Detects manifest ocular deviation (tropia). Tests ALIGNMENT, not ptosis severity.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Detects latent deviation (phoria + tropia). Tests ALIGNMENT, not ptosis severity.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MRD1 = distance from corneal light reflex to UPPER eyelid margin in primary gaze. NORMAL = 4-4.5 mm. Mild ptosis = 2-3 mm; Moderate = 1-2 mm; Severe ≤0 mm (covering pupil). This is the STANDARD GRADING METHOD for ptosis severity.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Measures levator muscle EXCURSION (downgaze to upgaze). Normal >12 mm. Used to choose surgical technique, NOT to grade severity. MRD = severity; LF = surgical planning. Page 4 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: MRD1 (MARGIN REFLEX DISTANCE) = distance from corneal reflex to upper lid margin. Normal = 4-4.5 mm. Used to GRADE SEVERITY. Levator Function (LF) guides SURGICAL TECHNIQUE choice. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: MRD = how bad; LF = what surgery.

questions.stem: Same 2-year-old girl with congenital ptosis. 3. The treatment of choice is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Corrects hypotropia (downward deviation). No role in ptosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Corrects vertical strabismus. No role in ptosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LEVATOR RESECTION (levator advancement) = DEFINITIVE TREATMENT for ptosis with ADEQUATE LEVATOR FUNCTION (>4 mm). Shortens levator muscle → raises upper lid. If LF <4 mm → FRONTALIS SLING instead (connects lid to frontalis muscle for brow-assisted lid elevation). URGENT if covering pupil to prevent amblyopia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Orbicularis = eyelid CLOSING muscle (CN VII). Weakening it would worsen lagophthalmos, not lift the lid.

questions.keyPoint: LEVATOR RESECTION = treatment for congenital ptosis when LF >4 mm. FRONTALIS SLING when LF <4 mm (poor levator). URGENT surgery if pupil covered (deprivation amblyopia risk in <8 years). Frontalis sling uses silicone rod or fascia lata to connect lid to brow.

questions.stem: Same 2-year-old girl with congenital ptosis. If left untreated: 4. If not treated, this child will likely suffer from:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Children SUPPRESS the deviating/occluded eye image (neuroplasticity). They do NOT develop diplopia — adults do. Children develop amblyopia instead.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Myopia is a refractive error, not a consequence of ptosis. Lid pressure can cause astigmatism, but myopia is not the primary concern.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ptosis can press on the cornea causing astigmatism — a secondary effect. However, the PRIMARY and most serious untreated consequence is AMBLYOPIA.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: DEPRIVATION AMBLYOPIA = most critical consequence. The ptotic lid occludes the visual axis during the CRITICAL PERIOD (birth-8 years). Without visual stimulation, the visual cortex of the affected eye fails to develop normal connections → permanently reduced visual acuity that CANNOT be corrected by glasses alone. Must treat URGENTLY.

questions.keyPoint: Untreated ptosis covering the pupil → DEPRIVATION AMBLYOPIA. Critical period: birth to 8 years (most sensitive in first 3 years). After 8 years = irreversible. Treatment: surgery + then patching good eye + optical correction. Time is everything in paediatric ophthalmology.

questions.stem: 38-YEAR-OLD MALE: MUCOPURULENT DISCHARGE for 6 months, HEARING LOSS, LARGE CENTRAL TYMPANIC MEMBRANE PERFORATION. 1) What is the cause of this pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: External canal infection. NO TM perforation. Pain on pinna movement. No middle ear involvement.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CSOM TUBOTYMPANIC (MUCOSAL/SAFE) TYPE: Chronic middle ear infection → CENTRAL (pars tensa) perforation + MUCOPURULENT (non-offensive) discharge + conductive hearing loss. "Safe" = fewer intracranial complications than squamous type. Page 6 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: QUAMOUS/UNSAFE type: CHOLESTEATOMA + ATTIC/PERIPHERAL perforation + OUL-SMELLING (offensive) discharge. More dangerous.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: auses SNHL and/or vertigo. Does NOT cause TM perforation or middle ear discharge.

questions.keyPoint: CSOM TUBOTYMPANIC: CENTRAL perforation + NON-OFFENSIVE mucopurulent discharge + CONDUCTIVE HL. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: Central (safe) vs Attic/Marginal (unsafe = cholesteatoma). Non-offensive (safe) vs Offensive/foul (unsafe).

questions.stem: Same 38-year-old with CSOM (tubotympanic type). 2) What is the cause of his hearing loss?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fungal infection of middle ear sac
choices.explanation: Otomycosis affects external canal. Not causing this pattern of HL with central TM perforation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Otitis externa affects canal. HL from canal oedema, not TM perforation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic infection & inflammation of the middle ear
choices.explanation: CONDUCTIVE HL in CSOM: TM perforation reduces sound-transmitting surface area + inflamed middle ear mucosa impairs ossicular function. The cochlea (inner ear) is INTACT. Air-bone gap present on audiogram.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Inner ear infection = SNHL (sensorineural). CSOM tubotympanic = CONDUCTIVE HL.

questions.keyPoint: CSOM tubotympanic = CONDUCTIVE HEARING LOSS. TM perforation + inflamed mucosa = reduced sound transmission. Cochlea INTACT = bone conduction preserved. Air-bone gap on audiogram. SNHL only if labyrinthine fistula develops (complication).

questions.stem: Same 38-year-old with CSOM (tubotympanic type). 3) The definitive treatment approach to restore the hearing & prevent complications?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Controls infection temporarily but does NOT repair the perforation or restore hearing. NOT definitive.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Aural toilet removes debris but does not repair structural defect. Supportive only.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MEDICAL management to control active infection and make ear DRY before surgery. NOT definitive treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical repair of TM & ossicles (tympanoplasty/ ossiculoplasty)
choices.explanation: TYMPANOPLASTY (TM repair using temporalis fascia graft) + OSSICULOPLASTY (ossicular chain reconstruction) = DEFINITIVE SURGICAL TREATMENT. Closes perforation → reduces infection risk + restores hearing. Ear must be DRY for at least 6 weeks before surgery.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Grommets ventilate middle ear in OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION (glue ear). In CSOM there is already a perforation — grommets are inappropriate.

questions.keyPoint: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT of CSOM Tubotympanic = TYMPANOPLASTY + OSSICULOPLASTY. Medical (aural toilet + topical ciprofloxacin) used FIRST to make ear dry for surgery. Grommet = glue ear (OME). NEVER for CSOM.

questions.stem: Same 38-year-old with CSOM. Infection spread beyond middle ear and mastoid. 4) What is the most common complication still remaining INTRA-TEMPORAL?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Intra-temporal complication (CN VII in fallopian canal), but LESS COMMON than coalescent mastoiditis as the first complication.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: INTRACRANIAL complication (not intra-temporal) from lateral sinus thrombosis. Page 8 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: OST COMMON INTRA-TEMPORAL complication. Infection spreads to mastoid air cells breakdown of bony septa between cells (coalescence) → mastoid abscess. Signs: ost-auricular swelling + erythema + AURICLE DISPLACED ANTEROINFERIORLY + loss f post-auricular crease. Treat: IV antibiotics + cortical mastoidectomy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: NTRACRANIAL complication — infection crosses the dura.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: NTRACRANIAL complication (most common INTRACRANIAL complication of CSOM).

questions.keyPoint: INTRA-TEMPORAL complications: Coalescent Mastoiditis (most common), Facial Palsy, Labyrinthitis, Petrositis. INTRACRANIAL: Meningitis (most common), Extradural abscess, Brain abscess, Lateral sinus thrombosis, Subdural abscess, Otitic hydrocephalus.

questions.stem: Same 38-year-old with CSOM. 5) What is the most frequent INTRA-CRANIAL complication of this type of ear infection?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Rare — from lateral sinus thrombosis causing raised ICP. Not the most frequent.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Serious but less common. Causes septicaemia, picket-fence fever.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Serious — between dura and skull. Less common than meningitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MENINGITIS = MOST COMMON INTRACRANIAL COMPLICATION of CSOM. Infection spreads through tegmen (roof of mastoid) or bony erosion. Signs: fever, severe headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, Kernig, Brudzinski. Treatment: IV ceftriaxone + neurosurgical review.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Second most serious intracranial complication. Temporal lobe (from middle ear) or cerebellar (from mastoid). Less common than meningitis.

questions.keyPoint: MENINGITIS = MOST COMMON INTRACRANIAL COMPLICATION of CSOM. Frequency: Meningitis > Extradural abscess > Brain abscess > Lateral sinus thrombosis > Subdural abscess. BRAIN ABSCESS: temporal lobe from middle ear; cerebellar from mastoid.

questions.stem: 6-YEAR-OLD MALE: LEFT EYE TURNING INWARD since age 2. 1) What is the abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: LATENT inward deviation only seen on dissociation (cover test). Not visible in binocular vision.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: OUTWARD (divergent) manifest deviation. This eye turns INWARD — opposite direction.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ESOTROPIA = MANIFEST INWARD (convergent) deviation of the eye. Most common childhood strabismus. 'Eso' = inward/nasal. 'Tropia' = manifest (visible without cover test).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LATENT outward deviation only revealed by dissociation. Not visible without cover testing.

questions.keyPoint: ESO = inward; EXO = outward; HYPER = upward; HYPO = downward. TROPIA = manifest (always visible); PHORIA = latent (only with dissociation). This case = LEFT ESOTROPIA (manifest inward deviation since age 2).

questions.stem: Same child. OD 6/6 & OS 6/18 on examination. 2) On examination: OD 6/6 & OS 6/18, what is the abnormality? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 10 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced visual acuity of left eye
choices.explanation: OD (Oculus Dexter = RIGHT) = 6/6 = NORMAL. OS (Oculus Sinister = LEFT) = 6/18 = REDUCED. 6/6: sees at 6m what normal sees at 6m. 6/18: needs to be at 6m to see what normal sees at 18m. REDUCED LEFT EYE VA = amblyopia from esotropia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduced visual acuity of right eye
choices.explanation: OD = 6/6 = NORMAL. Right eye is fine.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Myopia = negative refraction (concave lenses). This child has hypermetropia (see Q3). Reduced VA here = amblyopia, not myopia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Astigmatism has cylindrical correction. The reduced VA here is from amblyopia, not astigmatism primarily.

questions.keyPoint: OD = Oculus Dexter = RIGHT eye. OS = Oculus Sinister = LEFT eye. Snellen 6/X: normal 6/6; legal blindness 6/60. LEFT EYE 6/18 = amblyopia (the deviating eye suppressed during critical period).

questions.stem: Same child. OD = +1 & OS = +4. 3) OD = +1 & OS = +4, what is the abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Myopia = NEGATIVE refractive error. OS = +4 is POSITIVE = hypermetropia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: OD = +1 is also POSITIVE = mild hypermetropia, not myopia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: POSITIVE refractive error = HYPERMETROPIA (far-sightedness). OS = +4 dioptres = significant left eye hypermetropia. Anisometropia (OD +1 vs OS +4 = 3D difference). More hypermetropic eye accommodates more → excess convergence → esotropia + amblyopia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Astigmatism has cylindrical correction (e.g., -1.00 × 90). These are purely spherical positive values = hypermetropia.

questions.keyPoint: POSITIVE (+) = HYPERMETROPIA. NEGATIVE (-) = MYOPIA. Anisometropia >1.5 D difference = significant. Here OD +1 vs OS +4 = 3D difference = ANISOMETROPIC AMBLYOPIA in the more hypermetropic left eye. Full optical correction + patching good eye = treatment.

questions.stem: Same child. Condition corrected by spectacles. 4) The patient condition is corrected by spectacles — what is the type of abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: FULLY ACCOMMODATIVE ESOTROPIA: Hypermetropia → need to ACCOMMODATE (increase lens power) to see clearly → accommodation triggers CONVERGENCE reflex → excess convergence → ESOTROPIA. Plus lenses (glasses) reduce accommodation demand → esotropia resolves. If FULLY corrected by glasses = FULLY ACCOMMODATIVE. Most common type of childhood esotropia (onset 2-3 years).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Myopia does NOT cause accommodative esotropia. Myopes do not need to accommodate for near vision.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Paralytic (non-comitant) strabismus: CN III/IV/VI palsy, angle varies with gaze direction, does NOT respond to optical correction.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Latent = phoria (only under dissociation). This child has MANIFEST esotropia = tropia.

questions.keyPoint: ACCOMMODATIVE ESOTROPIA: Hypermetropia → accommodation → AC/A ratio convergence → esotropia. FULLY corrected by glasses (plus lenses) = FULLY ACCOMMODATIVE. Partially corrected = PARTIALLY ACCOMMODATIVE. Gold standard treatment: CYCLOPLEGIC REFRACTION (under atropine) to reveal full hypermetropia + full correction glasses.

questions.stem: Same child. If left untreated. 5) If the patient condition is left untreated, what could lead to? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 12 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Astigmatism is a refractive error, not a consequence of untreated strabismus. Not caused by esotropia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Adults with NEW strabismus get diplopia (brain cannot suppress). Children in CRITICAL PERIOD → SUPPRESS instead → no diplopia but develops AMBLYOPIA.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Myopia is separate from strabismus development. Not caused by untreated esotropia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: STRABISMIC AMBLYOPIA: Child's brain SUPPRESSES the deviating eye to avoid confusion → visual cortex of deviated eye does not develop properly → REDUCED VA that cannot be corrected by glasses alone. Untreated during critical period = PERMANENT. Treatment: glasses + PATCHING GOOD EYE forces deviating eye to work.

questions.keyPoint: Childhood strabismus → AMBLYOPIA (NOT diplopia). Brain suppresses deviating eye during critical period. After 8 years: permanent and irreversible. Treatment: optical correction + patching good eye (occlusion therapy). Earlier treatment = better outcome.

questions.stem: 27-YEAR-OLD FEMALE: SUDDEN SEVERE UNILATERAL PAIN right eye, PHOTOPHOBIA, NAUSEA, HEADACHE, ABDOMINAL DISCOMFORT. Exam: CORNEAL OEDEMA, MID-DILATED OVAL PUPIL WITH SLUGGISH LIGHT RESPONSE, MARKED CONJUNCTIVAL HYPEREMIA. 1. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Scleritis: boring/aching eye pain, violaceous hue, tender globe, associated with RA/SLE. No corneal oedema or mid-dilated fixed pupil.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: AACG CLASSIC TRIAD: Sudden severe eye pain + N&V; + headache (mimics acute abdomen). On exam: CORNEAL OEDEMA (steamy), MID-DILATED OVAL FIXED PUPIL (iris ischaemia), ciliary injection, RAISED IOP (50-70 mmHg). Precipitated by darkness, mydriatics, or emotional stress.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ION: Sudden PAINLESS visual loss + RAPD + optic disc oedema or normal disc. No fixed pupil, no corneal oedema, no N&V.;

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CL keratitis: pain + photophobia + discharge in CL wearer. No mid-dilated fixed pupil.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: AAU: SMALL (MIOTIC) pupil from ciliary spasm + cells and flare in AC + perilimbal injection. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: AACG = MID-DILATED pupil; AAU = CONSTRICTED pupil. Page 13 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: AACG PATHOGNOMONIC: Mid-DILATED OVAL FIXED PUPIL + STEAMY CORNEA + severe pain + N&V.; KEY DIFFERENTIATOR from anterior uveitis: DILATED vs CONSTRICTED pupil. NEVER give ATROPINE in AACG (worsens angle closure). OPHTHALMOLOGICAL EMERGENCY.

questions.stem: Same 27-year-old with AACG. 2. What is the first line treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: For inflammatory/autoimmune eye conditions. Does NOT lower IOP rapidly.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: For inflammatory and neoplastic conditions. No role in AACG.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IV mannitol with topical IOP-lowering agents
choices.explanation: AACG EMERGENCY: IV ACETAZOLAMIDE (or IV MANNITOL to draw fluid from vitreous) + TOPICAL TIMOLOL (beta-blocker) + TOPICAL PILOCARPINE (miotic — pulls iris from angle; works better once IOP partially lowered). Goal: rapidly lower IOP to save optic nerve before definitive treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ATROPINE IS ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED — it DILATES the pupil → WORSENS angle closure → DANGEROUS. This is the most dangerous wrong answer in AACG.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Surgical implants for refractory chronic glaucoma. Not acute emergency first-line.

questions.keyPoint: AACG MEDICAL EMERGENCY: IV Acetazolamide OR IV Mannitol + Topical Timolol + Topical Pilocarpine. ATROPINE IS ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED (dilates pupil → worsens closure). Lie patient supine. Then definitive: LASER PERIPHERAL IRIDOTOMY.

questions.stem: Same 27-year-old with AACG, now IOP controlled medically. 3. What is the definite treatment for this case?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: For diabetic retinopathy, retinal vein occlusion, retinal tears. Not for angle-closure glaucoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: For neovascular AMD, diabetic macular oedema. Not for glaucoma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: For macular oedema. Can RAISE IOP — dangerous in glaucoma.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LASER PERIPHERAL IRIDOTOMY (LPI) = DEFINITIVE TREATMENT for AACG. YAG or argon laser creates a small hole in the PERIPHERAL IRIS → aqueous bypasses pupil block → flows freely → angle opens → IOP normalised permanently. ALSO performed on FELLOW EYE prophylactically (bilateral risk).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: For OPEN-ANGLE glaucoma (improves aqueous outflow through open trabecular meshwork). In ANGLE-CLOSURE: physical block by iris not addressed by trabeculoplasty.

questions.keyPoint: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT of AACG = LASER PERIPHERAL IRIDOTOMY (LPI). Creates iris hole → bypasses pupil block → angle opens. Also treat FELLOW EYE prophylactically. Trabeculoplasty = OPEN-ANGLE (different mechanism entirely).

questions.stem: 20-YEAR-OLD MALE: BILATERAL NASAL OBSTRUCTION for 2 days following FACIAL TRAUMA. Exam: BILATERAL COMPRESSIBLE SWELLING on nasal septum. 1. What is your professional diagnosis? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 15 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Causes UNILATERAL obstruction; NOT compressible/fluctuant; not acute-onset bilateral after trauma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: SEPTAL HAEMATOMA = blood between PERICHONDRIUM and SEPTAL CARTILAGE after trauma. Key features: (1) History of nasal trauma, (2) BILATERAL compressible swelling, (3) BILATERAL nasal obstruction. EMERGENCY: perichondrium provides blood supply to avascular cartilage → untreated → AVASCULAR NECROSIS → SADDLE-NOSE DEFORMITY. Must drain within 24 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Localised staphylococcal boil at nasal entrance. Not bilateral compressible septal swelling.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Infected septal haematoma — with fever + severe pain + systemic infection. Complication of UNTREATED haematoma, not the initial diagnosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Grey glistening insensate masses from middle meatus. Not from acute trauma.

questions.keyPoint: SEPTAL HAEMATOMA = BILATERAL COMPRESSIBLE swelling after nasal trauma = SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Must drain within 24 hours to prevent AVASCULAR NECROSIS of septal cartilage → SADDLE-NOSE. If infected = SEPTAL ABSCESS (→ risk cavernous sinus thrombosis).

questions.stem: Same 20-year-old with confirmed septal haematoma. 2. What is the main line of treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Incision and drainage of the swelling
choices.explanation: I&D; is the DEFINITIVE TREATMENT. Hemitransfixion incision → evacuate haematoma → suture flap → BILATERAL NASAL PACKING (to prevent re-accumulation and support cartilage) + antibiotics (anti-staphylococcal: co-amoxiclav/flucloxacillin) to prevent secondary infection. Must be done within 24 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Treats pain only. Does not drain haematoma or prevent cartilage necrosis. Supportive only.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Given ALONGSIDE I&D; to prevent abscess formation. Not PRIMARY treatment alone.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: For allergic rhinitis and polyps. No role in acute haematoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Decongestants reduce mucosal congestion. Cannot drain a haematoma. Page 16 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: SEPTAL HAEMATOMA TREATMENT = I&D; + BILATERAL NASAL PACKING + ANTIBIOTICS. Time-sensitive: must drain within 24 hours to prevent avascular necrosis and saddle-nose deformity. SURGICAL EMERGENCY.

questions.stem: 50-YEAR-OLD FEMALE: PROGRESSIVE DYSPHAGIA for 3 months, SMOKING HISTORY, ANAEMIA for 5 years, BILATERAL HARD LYMPH NODE ENLARGEMENT. What's the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: NPC: nasal symptoms + unilateral serous OM + cervical LN + CN involvement. Common in South-East Asian males, EBV-associated. NOT primarily dysphagia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Laryngeal Ca: HOARSENESS as primary symptom. Dysphagia only in advanced disease.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Benign HPV-associated lesion in the oropharynx. Not associated with progressive dysphagia + hard LN.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: POST-CRICOID CARCINOMA: Classic in FEMALE with PLUMMER-VINSON SYNDROME (iron deficiency anaemia + post-cricoid web). Risk: smoking + anaemia + female sex. Progressive dysphagia (solids → liquids) + hard cervical LN (metastatic disease) + anaemia = classic presentation. SCC of posterior cricoid region.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Incomplete option in original file.

questions.keyPoint: POST-CRICOID Ca: FEMALE + Iron deficiency anaemia + Progressive dysphagia + Smoking = Plummer-Vinson syndrome predisposes. HARD cervical LN = metastatic disease. Most common hypopharyngeal Ca in FEMALES. Absent laryngeal crepitus = pre-vertebral invasion = INOPERABLE.

questions.stem: Same 50-year-old with post-cricoid mass. What's the initial clinical workup?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Nasopharyngoscopy visualises nasopharynx only. Does not reach hypopharynx/post-cricoid region.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: FLEXIBLE FIBREOPTIC LARYNGOSCOPY (nasendoscopy) = INITIAL INVESTIGATION for suspected hypopharyngeal pathology. Visualises nasopharynx → oropharynx → hypopharynx → larynx under LA in outpatient setting. Allows direct visualisation of post-cricoid mass and assessment of vocal cord mobility.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Otoscopy assesses ear canal and TM. No role in hypopharyngeal assessment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anterior rhinoscopy assesses nasal cavity. Not applicable to post-cricoid mass.

questions.keyPoint: Initial investigation for hypopharyngeal/post-cricoid pathology = FLEXIBLE FIBREOPTIC LARYNGOSCOPY. Definitive diagnosis requires BIOPSY UNDER GA. Absent laryngeal crepitus = pre-vertebral invasion. CT for staging.

questions.stem: Same 50-year-old with post-cricoid mass. Neck exam findings besides LN. What would you find when you examine the neck (besides enlarged lymph nodes)?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Subcutaneous air from penetrating trauma or oesophageal perforation. Not a feature of hypopharyngeal carcinoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Previous surgery finding. Not applicable here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Thyroid pathology. Not the expected finding in hypopharyngeal carcinoma.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ABSENT LARYNGEAL CREPITUS (Jackson sign): Normal larynx moves side-to-side over the pre-vertebral spine (crunching sensation = laryngeal crepitus). When hypopharyngeal tumour INVADES the pre-vertebral fascia, the larynx is FIXED → crepitus ABSENT. Absent crepitus = INOPERABLE DISEASE (T4b — invasion of pre-vertebral fascia). Page 18 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: uggests fistula or TB lymphadenitis (collar-stud abscess). Not characteristic of ypopharyngeal carcinoma.

questions.keyPoint: ABSENT LARYNGEAL CREPITUS (Jackson sign) = tumour has invaded pre-vertebral fascia = INOPERABLE (T4b). This is a critically important clinical finding before planning surgery — if larynx cannot rock over spine, surgical resection is not possible.

questions.stem: Same 50-year-old with post-cricoid mass. How to reach the definite diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hypopharynx inaccessible under LA. GA required for controlled airway and direct visualisation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: DEFINITIVE DIAGNOSIS: GA + direct laryngoscopy/hypopharyngoscopy + EXCISIONAL/INCISIONAL BIOPSY for histological confirmation. Under GA: direct visualisation + extent assessment + multiple biopsies + vocal cord mobility assessment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Incisional biopsy for neck lymph nodes
choices.explanation: LN biopsy provides metastatic disease info but does NOT confirm primary tumour histology. Primary lesion must be biopsied.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Fossa of Rosenmüller is in NASOPHARYNX — for NPC biopsy. Not for post-cricoid carcinoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: For tonsillar malignancy. Not applicable to post-cricoid carcinoma.

questions.keyPoint: Definitive diagnosis of post-cricoid carcinoma = DIRECT LARYNGOSCOPY UNDER GA + BIOPSY. Histology = SCC (>95%). FNAC of neck node useful for staging but does not confirm primary diagnosis.

questions.stem: Same 50-year-old with post-cricoid mass. How to determine disease extension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Plain X-ray for neck soft tissue
choices.explanation: Limited soft tissue info. Insufficient for staging. Cannot show depth of invasion, LN involvement, or distant mets.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Shows filling defect and level of obstruction. Outdated for staging — no depth of invasion, LN or distant mets info.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PET-CT useful for distant mets and recurrence detection. CT is the STANDARD FIRST staging tool.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CONTRAST-ENHANCED CT NECK/CHEST/ABDOMEN = STANDARD STAGING INVESTIGATION. Provides: (1) Local tumour extent + depth of invasion, (2) Lymph node status (size, number, necrosis), (3) Distant metastases (lung, liver). MRI adjunctive for soft tissue detail. PET-CT for equivocal cases.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Incomplete option.

questions.keyPoint: STAGING of hypopharyngeal carcinoma = CONTRAST-ENHANCED CT NECK/CHEST/ABDOMEN (TNM staging). MRI for soft tissue detail. PET-CT for equivocal distant metastases. CT provides: T (local extent) + N (LN) + M (distant).

questions.stem: YOUNG PATIENT: ball hit the eye during tennis (orbital blowout fracture). What's the most likely wall to be affected in orbital blow out?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ORBITAL FLOOR = MOST COMMONLY FRACTURED wall. Thinnest orbital wall (forms roof of maxillary sinus). Sudden intraorbital pressure → floor blows downward into maxillary sinus → inferior rectus/fat herniation/entrapment.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Orbital roof is thick and strong. Rarely fractured in isolated blowout.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Orbital apex is deep and rarely fractured in blowout injuries. Page 20 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lamina papyracea (ethmoid) = SECOND most common. Fractures into ethmoid sinus. Medial rectus can be entrapped.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Thickest orbital wall (zygoma + sphenoid). Resists isolated blowout forces.

questions.keyPoint: ORBITAL FLOOR = most commonly fractured in blowout fractures (thinnest wall, over maxillary sinus). Second = medial wall (lamina papyracea). Complication = INFERIOR RECTUS ENTRAPMENT → vertical diplopia + restricted upgaze.

questions.stem: Same blowout fracture patient. What is the best imaging modality?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CT ORBIT with HIGH-RESOLUTION CORONAL RECONSTRUCTIONS = GOLD STANDARD. Shows: fracture location/extent, muscle entrapment, sinus opacification, orbital volume loss. Coronal views essential for floor assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Superior soft tissue contrast but SLOWER and POORER bone detail than CT. MRI for soft tissue complications (not primary fracture assessment).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: X-ray may show TEARDROP SIGN (fat/muscle herniation into maxillary sinus) but lacks detail for surgical planning.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: B-scan: intraocular pathology. Cannot assess orbital bony walls.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Ocular ultrasound for retina/vitreous. Not for orbital fractures.

questions.keyPoint: CT ORBIT (coronal reformats) = GOLD STANDARD for orbital blowout fractures. Shows fracture + muscle entrapment + sinus changes. TEARDROP SIGN on plain X-ray (fat herniation into maxillary sinus) = indirect sign. CT confirms.

questions.stem: Same patient. Has VERTICAL DIPLOPIA. The patient has vertical diplopia; what's the most likely muscle to be affected?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Medial rectus entrapment = HORIZONTAL diplopia (restricted abduction). From MEDIAL WALL fractures.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Superior rectus is above the globe. Cannot be trapped in an ORBITAL FLOOR fracture.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Lateral rectus is on the temporal side. Cannot be trapped in the orbital floor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inferior rectus trapped in the fracture line
choices.explanation: INFERIOR RECTUS ENTRAPMENT = classic complication of ORBITAL FLOOR fractures. Floor fractures → inferior rectus/inferior oblique trapped in fracture → tethering → (1) VERTICAL DIPLOPIA, (2) RESTRICTED UPGAZE (cannot stretch trapped inferior rectus), (3) HYPOGLOBUS (downward displacement).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Placeholder.

questions.keyPoint: INFERIOR RECTUS ENTRAPMENT (floor fracture) → VERTICAL DIPLOPIA + RESTRICTED UPGAZE. MEDIAL RECTUS entrapment (medial wall fracture) → HORIZONTAL diplopia. FLOOR = vertical; MEDIAL = horizontal. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR.

questions.stem: Related to blowout fracture scenario. Definition question. Q: Hyphema mean?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: WBCs in posterior chamber = posterior uveitis/inflammation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pus in posterior chamber = severe endophthalmitis. Page 22 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cs in POSTERIOR chamber = not hyphema. Hyphema is ANTERIOR chamber.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cs in anterior chamber = HYPOPYON (inflammatory cells layering inferiorly in AC). en in severe uveitis/keratitis/endophthalmitis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PHEMA = BLOOD (RBCs) IN THE ANTERIOR CHAMBER (between cornea and iris). used by blunt ocular trauma (iris vessel rupture) or rubeosis iridis. Appears as red fluid yering inferiorly. Risk: raised IOP + corneal blood staining + secondary haemorrhage.

questions.keyPoint: HYPHEMA = RBCs in ANTERIOR chamber (blood between cornea and iris). HYPOPYON = WBCs/pus in anterior chamber (white layering = uveitis/keratitis). KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: RED layering = hyphema (blood); WHITE layering = hypopyon (pus).

questions.stem: Same blowout fracture patient. True statement regarding treatment. What's the true statement regarding treatment if the patient had a blowout fracture?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: NOT all blowout fractures require surgery. Small fractures with minimal symptoms → conservative. Surgery indicated for: persistent diplopia, enophthalmos >2mm, large defect, CT-confirmed entrapment.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NOT routinely. Typically performed at 10-14 days (allow oedema to resolve). EXCEPTION: paediatric "white-eyed blowout" with oculocardiac reflex = URGENT (<24h).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient is told to not blow up with his nose
choices.explanation: NASAL BLOWING IS CONTRAINDICATED after orbital blowout. Positive pressure forces air through the fracture into the orbit → ORBITAL EMPHYSEMA → further displacement + infection risk. Advise: no nose-blowing for 2 weeks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wire and screws are used in the surgery
choices.explanation: Orbital floor repair uses IMPLANTS (titanium mesh, silastic, porous polyethylene). Wires/screws = mandibular/other facial fracture repairs.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Persistent diplopia is the only indication for surgery
choices.explanation: Surgery also indicated for: enophthalmos >2mm, large floor defect (>50%), significant entrapment on CT, "white-eyed blowout" in children.

questions.keyPoint: AFTER ORBITAL BLOWOUT: NO NOSE-BLOWING (2 weeks). Prevents orbital emphysema. Surgery NOT always needed — only for: persistent diplopia, enophthalmos >2mm, large defect, entrapment. Paediatric white-eyed blowout = URGENT (<24h).

questions.stem: 3-YEAR-OLD BABY: RIGHT PUPIL LOOKS WHITE (leukocoria). FATHER HAS AN ENUCLEATED EYE since childhood. Fundus: MULTIPLE MIDPERIPHERAL GREY COTTAGE CHEESE RETINAL MASSES with PROMINENT TORTUOUS FEEDING VESSELS. Q1: The clinical term for his pupil is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Miosis = constricted pupil. Caused by opioids, CN III spasm, Horner. Not the white appearance.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Mydriasis = dilated pupil. The abnormality here is the WHITE COLOUR, not size.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LEUKOCORIA = WHITE PUPILLARY REFLEX. Most important RED FLAG for RETINOBLASTOMA. Normally pupil reflex is red (vascularised retina). White reflex = mass behind lens. MOST COMMON PRESENTATION of retinoblastoma. Causes (RETRO): Retinoblastoma, ROP, Total retinal detachment, Retinoschisis, Ocular toxocara, Persistent foetal vasculature, Cataract, Coats disease.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Hyphaema = blood in anterior chamber = red layering, not white pupil.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Iris adhesions causing irregular pupil shape, not leukocoria.

questions.keyPoint: LEUKOCORIA in a child = RETINOBLASTOMA UNTIL PROVEN OTHERWISE. Most common presentation of retinoblastoma. Second most common feature = STRABISMUS. Positive family history + bilateral disease = HEREDITARY (germline) RB1 mutation.

questions.stem: Same 3-year-old with retinoblastoma. Q2: A useful and safe investigation to aid in the diagnosis would be:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Corneal topography = contact lens fitting / keratoconus. No role in diagnosing posterior intraocular mass.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: May show anaemia in advanced disease but not a useful diagnostic imaging investigation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: May show calcification but not sensitive/specific. CT has replaced it.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: B-SCAN ULTRASONOGRAPHY = FIRST-LINE SAFE INVESTIGATION. Detects: intraocular mass + HIGH INTERNAL REFLECTIVITY (calcification — hallmark of retinoblastoma) + retinal detachment + vitreous seeding. CT avoided when possible in children (radiation risk in Rb1 gene carriers → secondary cancers). BIOPSY CONTRAINDICATED.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Assesses retinal vasculature. Not primary diagnostic tool for suspected retinoblastoma. Biopsy also contraindicated.

questions.keyPoint: B-SCAN USG = safe initial investigation (no radiation). HIGH INTERNAL REFLECTIVITY on B-scan = calcification = retinoblastoma hallmark. CT for staging (optic nerve/orbital extension). MRI preferred over CT (no radiation for Rb1 carriers). BIOPSY ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED.

questions.stem: Same 3-year-old. Father had enucleated eye since childhood. Q3: The pattern of inheritance is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Standard teaching: familial retinoblastoma is inherited as AD (germline Rb1 mutation). However, file marks Autosomal Recessive as correct — see FLAG below. Page 25 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: FILE ANSWER. See flag below for full explanation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma is not X-linked.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma is not X-linked.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma is well characterised genetically.

questions.keyPoint: RETINOBLASTOMA GENETICS: Knudson's TWO-HIT HYPOTHESIS. RB1 gene (chr 13q14) = tumour suppressor. Both copies must be lost for tumour development. HEREDITARY (40%): 1st hit = germline, 2nd hit = somatic → bilateral/multifocal. SPORADIC (60%): both hits somatic → unilateral/unifocal.

questions.stem: Same 3-year-old with retinoblastoma. Q4: Treatment options for such condition include:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systemic steroids and im munomodulator s
choices.explanation: For inflammatory orbital disease. No role in retinoblastoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: For neovascular AMD/DME. Not primary retinoblastoma treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma is a tumour, not an infection.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SYSTEMIC CHEMOTHERAPY (chemoreduction) = cornerstone of bilateral retinoblastoma treatment. Common regimen: CARBOPLATIN + VINCRISTINE + ETOPOSIDE (CVE). Reduces tumour for subsequent focal treatments (laser, cryo, brachytherapy). Enucleation for advanced unilateral disease. AVOID external beam radiation in Rb1 carriers (secondary malignancy risk — osteosarcoma). Page 26 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma is MALIGNANT with metastatic potential. Active treatment always required.

questions.keyPoint: RETINOBLASTOMA TX: Systemic chemo (carboplatin + vincristine + etoposide) → focal treatments (laser, cryo, brachytherapy). Enucleation for advanced unilateral disease. AVOID external beam radiation in Rb1 carriers (osteosarcoma risk). Excellent prognosis (>95% survival) with early treatment.

questions.stem: Same 3-year-old with retinoblastoma. Q5: The most likely cause of this white pupil is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Most common cause of leukocoria overall but: detected at birth, no retinal masses or feeding vessels, no family history of enucleation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Buphthalmos: enlarged eye, photophobia, epiphora, corneal haziness. Does NOT cause leukocoria or retinal masses.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ROP: premature infants <32 weeks or <1500g. Not applicable in a 3-year-old without prematurity history.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: (1) Leukocoria, (2) POSITIVE FAMILY HISTORY (father enucleated childhood = almost certainly retinoblastoma), (3) Cottage cheese masses with feeding vessels on fundoscopy, (4) Age 3 years = TEXTBOOK RETINOBLASTOMA. Father with bilateral Rb1 = germline mutation → 50% inheritance risk.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Ocular toxocariasis: single inflammatory granuloma (not multiple masses), associated with dog/cat contact, ELISA positive.

questions.keyPoint: RETINOBLASTOMA confirmed by: White pupil + family history of enucleation + cottage cheese masses with prominent tortuous feeding vessels. Family history of enucleation in early childhood = germline RB1 mutation until proven otherwise. Second commonest paediatric intraocular malignancy (most common = uveal melanoma in adults).

questions.stem: Meniere disease mechanism question. Q4: Meniere disease mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Not clearly visible in original file.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Not clearly visible in original file.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ENDOLYMPHATIC HYDROPS = excess endolymph in membranous labyrinth (cochlea/saccule). Distension → rupture of Reissner's membrane → mixing of endo/perilymph → hair cell depolarisation → Meniere's tetrad: EPISODIC VERTIGO (20min-12h) + low-frequency SNHL + TINNITUS + AURAL FULLNESS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Not clearly visible in original file.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Not clearly visible in original file.

questions.keyPoint: MENIERE = ENDOLYMPHATIC HYDROPS. Tetrad: Episodic vertigo (20min-12h) + low-frequency SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness (Lermoyez's). Treatment: low-salt diet + betahistine + diuretics. Surgery: endolymphatic sac decompression or gentamicin ablation for refractory.

questions.stem: Peripheral vs Central vertigo differentiation. Q5: Peripheral vertigo is described as: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 28 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Non-specific. Central vertigo often presents as vague dizziness/imbalance without true spinning.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PERIPHERAL VERTIGO = TRUE ROTARY SPINNING SENSATION (environment or self appears to spin). From vestibular apparatus dysfunction (BPPV, vestibular neuritis, Meniere). Nystagmus: horizontal-rotatory, FATIGABLE, NEVER purely vertical.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Numbness = sensory nerve pathology. Not characteristic of peripheral vestibular vertigo.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Not visible in original file.

questions.keyPoint: PERIPHERAL VERTIGO = TRUE SPINNING SENSATION. CENTRAL = vague dizziness/imbalance. Peripheral nystagmus: horizontal-rotatory, FATIGABLE, unidirectional. Central: direction-changing, VERTICAL, non-fatigable. Red flags for CENTRAL: headache, diplopia, dysphagia, new SNHL, ataxia.

questions.stem: 4-MONTH-OLD BABY: Failed neonatal hearing screening (REFERRED). Non-remarkable perinatal history. POSITIVE FAMILY HISTORY of congenital hearing loss. What will your next step be?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: At 4 months with positive family history, repeat screening is no longer adequate. Formal audiological evaluation required.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ABR (AUDITORY BRAINSTEM RESPONSE) = GOLD STANDARD diagnostic test for hearing loss in infants. Records electrical responses from auditory nerve/brainstem to click stimuli. Can be done under natural sleep. Provides threshold information, distinguishes cochlear vs retrocochlear. EHDI: Screen by 1m, Diagnose by 3m, Intervene by 6m.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Requires patient cooperation (raising hand to tones). NOT possible in a 4-month-old. Used from age ~4-5 years.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Requesting CT scan of temporal bones
choices.explanation: CT useful for cochlear anatomy assessment but NOT first-line. Audiological evaluation (ABR) must confirm diagnosis first before imaging.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MRI brain for retrocochlear pathology in adults (acoustic neuroma). Not appropriate first step in a 4-month-old.

questions.keyPoint: ABR = GOLD STANDARD for infants. Can be done during natural sleep or sedation. EHDI rule: Screen by 1 MONTH, Diagnose by 3 MONTHS, Intervene (hearing aid/CI) by 6 MONTHS. Earlier intervention = better language outcomes.

questions.stem: Same 4-month-old. On evaluation: RIGHT SIDE MODERATE SNHL + LEFT SIDE PROFOUND SNHL. What is the best treatment in this case?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HA suitable for mild-moderate SNHL. Profound SNHL (left side) does NOT benefit adequately from HAs — cochlear hair cells too damaged.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Right side has MODERATE loss — HA sufficient. Cochlear implant for right would be over-treatment at this stage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right side hearing aid with left side Cochlear implant
choices.explanation: TAILORED TO EACH EAR: RIGHT (moderate) = HEARING AID. LEFT (profound) = COCHLEAR IMPLANT (electrically stimulates auditory nerve, bypassing damaged cochlear hair cells). Bilateral intervention critical for speech development + sound localisation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Untreated profound left SNHL denies binaural hearing. Critical for language development.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: HA ineffective for profound SNHL. Cochlear implant needed for left side.

questions.keyPoint: TREATMENT tailored to DEGREE of HL in each ear: Mild-Moderate = HEARING AID. Severe-Profound = COCHLEAR IMPLANT. Cochlear implant criteria: severe-profound bilateral SNHL + failed HA trial + no medical contraindications. Best before age 2 (critical language period).

questions.stem: 65-YEAR-OLD MALE: 40 PACK-YEAR SMOKING HISTORY. PERSISTENT HOARSENESS for 3 MONTHS. Chronic cough + occasional throat pain. POSSIBLE MASS on left vocal cord on examination. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DANGEROUS: Hoarseness >3 weeks in a smoker = MALIGNANT until proven otherwise. Antibiotics delay cancer diagnosis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recommend voice rest and follow-up 6 weeks
choices.explanation: Voice rest only for benign conditions without risk factors. Watchful waiting in this high-risk case = unacceptable delay.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: STANDARD OF CARE: Direct laryngoscopy + biopsy under GA. Provides: (1) Direct visualisation of mass, (2) Vocal cord mobility assessment (fixed cord = T3), (3) Tissue biopsy for histology. URGENT in this context.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Order chest X-ray to rule out pulmonary causes
choices.explanation: CXR has role in staging (pulmonary mets) but does NOT provide primary diagnosis. Laryngoscopy + biopsy is the URGENT first step.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Steroids suppress inflammation but do not diagnose or treat malignancy.

questions.keyPoint: HOARSENESS >3 WEEKS in a SMOKER = direct laryngoscopy + biopsy URGENTLY. NEVER prescribe antibiotics or watch and wait. 2-week urgent referral rule applies. Laryngeal SCC = most common laryngeal malignancy.

questions.stem: Laryngeal SCC risk factor question. Which of the following is the STRONGEST risk factor for SCC of the larynx?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: LPR from GERD = minor risk factor for laryngeal Ca. Not the strongest.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: HPV-16/18 strongly associated with OROPHARYNGEAL SCC (base of tongue, tonsil). Less prominent role in GLOTTIC laryngeal SCC.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Can cause candidal laryngitis/hoarseness. NOT a significant risk factor for laryngeal carcinoma.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heavy alcohol consumption combined with tobacco use
choices.explanation: TOBACCO + ALCOHOL = SYNERGISTIC (multiplicative) risk for laryngeal SCC. Combined = up to 30x risk. Tobacco = most important SINGLE factor (nitrosamines, benzpyrene directly damage DNA). Synergism: alcohol dissolves mucus barrier + tobacco delivers carcinogens directly.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Causes allergic laryngitis/oedema. Not a risk factor for laryngeal carcinoma.

questions.keyPoint: TOBACCO + ALCOHOL = SYNERGISTIC (multiplicative, not additive) risk for laryngeal SCC. Combined ~30x risk vs neither. TOBACCO = most important SINGLE risk factor. HPV more important for OROPHARYNGEAL SCC. Asbestos = additional minor risk for laryngeal Ca.

questions.stem: Patient with advanced laryngeal carcinoma with referred otalgia. Referred otalgia in patient with advanced CA larynx indicates involvement of which nerve?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CN X carries laryngeal sensation (recurrent + superior laryngeal nerves) and via Arnold's nerve refers ear pain from the larynx/subglottis. Also a correct answer in broader context but file answer is CN IX. Page 32 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN IX (GLOSSOPHARYNGEAL) mediates referred otalgia from the PHARYNX via Jacobson's nerve (tympanic branch) which carries sensation to the middle ear. Pharyngeal/hypopharyngeal tumours refer pain to ear via CN IX. PEARL: Any patient with EAR PAIN + NORMAL OTOSCOPY must be examined for a PHARYNGEAL/LARYNGEAL TUMOUR.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CN V: referred otalgia from oral cavity/anterior tongue (auriculotemporal nerve V3). Not the primary nerve for pharyngeal/laryngeal referred otalgia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CN XI = purely MOTOR (SCM, trapezius). No sensory fibres. Cannot mediate referred pain.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CN XII = purely MOTOR (tongue muscles). No sensory fibres.

questions.keyPoint: REFERRED OTALGIA from pharynx/larynx: CN IX (Glossopharyngeal) via Jacobson's nerve. CN X (Vagus) via Arnold's nerve (from larynx). KEY PEARL: EAR PAIN + NORMAL OTOSCOPY = examine the pharynx/larynx for CANCER. This is an important diagnostic trap.

questions.stem: Laryngeal carcinoma causing stridor. Stridor in patient with CA larynx most likely associated with:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Supraglottic: dysphagia + referred otalgia + muffled voice. Stridor only LATE (when glottis compromised).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Glottic: HOARSENESS as EARLIEST symptom (even tiny lesions). Stridor occurs late (when cord fixed/occluded).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SUBGLOTTIC CARCINOMA → STRIDOR as EARLY/PRESENTING FEATURE. Subglottis is a NARROW FIXED COMPARTMENT — even a small mass causes significant airway narrowing → biphasic stridor. WORST PROGNOSIS (rich lymphatic drainage + late presentation due to no early specific symptom like hoarseness in glottic Ca).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pharyngeal extension → dysphagia. Stridor from glottic/subglottic airway compromise.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Extralaryngeal → cervical mass. Stridor from intrinsic airway obstruction.

questions.keyPoint: SUBGLOTTIC Ca → STRIDOR (narrow subglottic space). GLOTTIC Ca → HOARSENESS (vocal cord involved early). SUPRAGLOTTIC Ca → DYSPHAGIA + referred otalgia. PROGNOSIS: Glottic BEST (early hoarseness, least LN drainage). Supraglottic WORST (most LN drainage, late presentation).

questions.stem: 58-YEAR-OLD LAWYER: T1N0M0 GLOTTIC CARCINOMA. Treatment options discussion. Which of the following treatment modalities is most likely to PRESERVE HIS VOICE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Removes entire larynx → PERMANENT TRACHEOSTOMY → COMPLETE LOSS OF NATURAL VOICE. Not voice-preserving.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Used for organ-preserving treatment in Stages III-IV. Not preferred for early T1 disease (higher side effects, not needed for T1).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Partially voice-preserving but more invasive than necessary for T1 glottic. Radiotherapy preferred.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: RADIOTHERAPY = PREFERRED for T1 GLOTTIC Ca to preserve voice. >90% local control for T1a. Vocal cords remain in place → voice maintained. Alternative: endoscopic CO2 LASER resection (also voice-preserving). Both are equally effective for T1 disease.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: T1N0M0 = NO nodal disease → neck dissection not indicated. Chemo reserved for advanced disease.

questions.keyPoint: T1 GLOTTIC Ca: Voice-preserving curative options = (1) EXTERNAL BEAM RADIOTHERAPY (preferred) or (2) ENDOSCOPIC CO2 LASER RESECTION. >90% cure rate for T1a. Total laryngectomy = VOICE LOSS (salvage for recurrence). T1N0M0 = no need for neck dissection.

questions.stem: 16-YEAR-OLD BOY: nasal trauma during football 4 days ago. Temperature 39°C, increased nasal swelling, tender nose (anterior rhinoscopy = nasal haematoma). LONG-TERM SEQUELA if untreated? The most likely long-term sequela if this condition is left untreated is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cauliflower ear = auricular haematoma complication. The nasal equivalent is saddle-nose deformity.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: SADDLE-NOSE DEFORMITY = classic complication of UNTREATED nasal septal haematoma. Haematoma separates perichondrium from cartilage → avascular necrosis → cartilage resorption → collapse of nasal dorsum. File note confirms: NASAL HAEMATOMA = SADDLE NOSE.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Septal deviation from cartilage displacement ≠ saddle-nose from avascular necrosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CSF rhinorrhoea = anterior skull base fracture (cribriform plate). Not from septal haematoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Risk if haematoma gets infected → abscess → intracranial spread. Less common than cosmetic saddle-nose deformity as long-term sequela.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL HAEMATOMA untreated → SADDLE-NOSE (avascular necrosis of cartilage). Other causes of saddle-nose: Wegener's granulomatosis, relapsing polychondritis, congenital syphilis, cocaine. Must drain within 24h to prevent this.

questions.stem: 25-YEAR-OLD MAN: PAINFUL LEFT EAR + humming sounds after a FIGHT. Otoscopy shows a dry, irregular perforation without purulent discharge. The diagnosis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ASOM: ear pain + PURULENT discharge + FEVER + bulging TM before perforation. This patient has NO fever, NO pus, and the perforation is from DIRECT TRAUMA. Page 35 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: TRAUMATIC TM PERFORATION: RAGGED/IRREGULAR edges (vs clean central = CSOM) + history of trauma (slap/blast/fight) + NO purulent discharge + NO fever. Management: keep DRY, no swimming, heals spontaneously in ~3 months in most cases.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CSOM = months-years of mucopurulent discharge. Acute trauma makes CSOM impossible.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Otitis externa = external canal infection. TM is INTACT. Pain on pinna/tragus movement.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: OME = serous/mucous fluid in middle ear with INTACT, dull/amber TM. Not from acute trauma.

questions.keyPoint: TRAUMATIC TM PERFORATION: Irregular ragged edges + trauma history + NO fever + NO pus. Heals spontaneously in 3 months (most cases). NO swimming (keep dry). Antibiotics only if contamination suspected. Myringoplasty if not healed in 3 months.

questions.stem: 70-YEAR-OLD MAN: BILATERAL HEARING LOSS + TINNITUS for 3 years. NORMAL OTOSCOPY. Audiogram shows bilateral symmetrical high-frequency loss with air and bone conduction closely parallel. What is your diagnosis based on his audiogram?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Normal: all thresholds within 25 dB at all frequencies. Clearly elevated thresholds here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Conductive: AC reduced, BC normal → AIR-BONE GAP. Here both AC and BC are reduced equally = NO gap = not conductive.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Otosclerosis = CONDUCTIVE HL (stapes fixation) with Carhart's notch at 2kHz on BC. Air-bone gap present. This audiogram has no air-bone gap = no conductive component.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SNHL: Both AC and BC thresholds equally reduced (NO air-bone gap). Bilateral symmetrical HIGH-FREQUENCY loss in elderly = PRESBYCUSIS (age-related SNHL). Normal otoscopy (no visible cause = inner ear/cochlear problem). Most common cause of HL in >60 year olds.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Mixed = BOTH conductive and SNHL components → air-bone gap present BUT BC also reduced. Not the pattern here. Page 36 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: SNHL AUDIOGRAM: AC = BC (NO air-bone gap), both reduced. Bilateral symmetrical HF loss in elderly = PRESBYCUSIS. CONDUCTIVE: AC reduced, BC normal, gap >10 dB. MIXED: both reduced but gap still present. KEY RULE: if BC is normal and AC reduced = conductive.

questions.stem: 59-YEAR-OLD MAN: LEFT-SIDED NASAL BLOCKAGE + HEARING LOSS + DEEP NECK PAIN + FACIAL PAIN. Otoscopy shows a mass. What is your diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Parapharyngeal paraganglioma: pulsatile neck mass, pulsatile tinnitus. Not this presentation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hypopharyngeal Ca: primarily DYSPHAGIA. Nasal symptoms and serous OM not typical.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: NPC TRIAD: (1) NASAL symptoms (obstruction, epistaxis), (2) OTOLOGICAL: unilateral SEROUS OTITIS MEDIA/conductive HL (Eustachian tube blocked by NPC in fossa of Rosenmüller), (3) CERVICAL LYMPHADENOPATHY (often presenting feature). Also: CN V (facial pain/numbness), CN VI palsy. Associated with EBV + South-East Asian males.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Oropharyngeal Ca: dysphagia, odynophagia, sore throat, HPV-associated. Not unilateral nasal obstruction + serous OM.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Arises from cribriform plate: epistaxis, anosmia, visual disturbance. Not this presentation.

questions.keyPoint: NPC CLASSIC TRIAD: Nasal symptoms + Unilateral serous OM (conductive HL from ET obstruction) + Cervical LN (often first feature). EBV-associated. South-East Asian males most common. Biopsy site: Fossa of Rosenmüller. CN involvement: V (facial pain), VI (diplopia) in advanced disease.

questions.stem: 33-YEAR-OLD MAN: severe sore throat + DIFFICULTY OPENING MOUTH + LEFT-SIDED EAR PAIN. Exam: tender enlarged jugulodigastric LN. Throat image shows peritonsillar bulge. What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Retropharyngeal abscess: posterior midline neck swelling, dysphagia, neck stiffness, usually in children. Uvula not deviated.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Dental abscess: localised dental pain, cheek swelling. Trismus may occur but peritonsillar bulge absent.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Parapharyngeal: lateral pharyngeal wall displacement, medial pterygoid involvement → trismus. Less common.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: QUINSY (PERITONSILLAR ABSCESS) CLASSIC FEATURES: (1) Unilateral peritonsillar bulge with uvular deviation to OPPOSITE side, (2) TRISMUS (difficulty opening mouth = pterygoid spasm), (3) HOT POTATO VOICE (muffled voice), (4) REFERRED OTALGIA (CN IX), (5) Drooling, odynophagia, dysphagia, (6) Jugulodigastric (tonsillar) lymph node. Treatment: INCISION AND DRAINAGE + IV antibiotics. Absolute indication for tonsillectomy = 2nd quinsy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Ludwig's angina (submandibular): floor of mouth swelling, bilateral, risk of airway compromise.

questions.keyPoint: QUINSY (PERITONSILLAR ABSCESS): Unilateral peritonsillar bulge + UVULAR DEVIATION to OPPOSITE side + TRISMUS + HOT POTATO VOICE + referred otalgia. Treatment: I&D; + IV antibiotics. Tonsillectomy after 2nd quinsy. First quinsy = NOT an indication for immediate tonsillectomy.

questions.stem: Question about the predominant symptom of acute sinusitis. Predominant symptom of acute sinusitis:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: FACIAL PAIN (pressure/ache over affected sinus) is the PREDOMINANT SYMPTOM of acute sinusitis. Maxillary sinusitis = cheek pain; Frontal = forehead pain; Ethmoid = periorbital pain; Sphenoid = vertex/deep pain. Pain worsens with bending forward. Accompanied by nasal discharge (purulent), nasal obstruction, reduced smell, fever.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Foul-smelling discharge suggests dental infection or foreign body causing sinusitis. Not the predominant symptom of typical acute sinusitis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Facial pressure is closely related to facial pain but "facial pain" is the PREDOMINANT distinguishing symptom for sinusitis (as opposed to other rhinological conditions).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anosmia/hyposmia can occur in sinusitis (from mucosal inflammation) but is not the PREDOMINANT symptom.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Epistaxis (nosebleed) is not a typical or predominant feature of acute sinusitis.

questions.keyPoint: ACUTE SINUSITIS: FACIAL PAIN = predominant symptom. Diagnostic criteria (EPOS): ≥2 symptoms (nasal obstruction, discharge, facial pain, reduced smell) + objective evidence. Maxillary most commonly affected (poor drainage dependent on mucociliary clearance, gravity not helpful).

questions.stem: Which is an ABSOLUTE indication of tonsillectomy? Which of the followings is an absolute indication of tonsillectomy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Chronic tonsillitis is a RELATIVE indication (usually requires ≥7 episodes/year, or ≥5/year for 2 years, or ≥3/year for 3 years — Paradise criteria). NOT absolute.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After first attack of peritonsillar abscess
choices.explanation: FIRST quinsy alone is NOT an absolute indication. Second episode of quinsy IS an indication. Some surgeons recommend tonsillectomy after any quinsy — but first episode alone is not universally absolute.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Foreign body in tonsil can usually be removed without tonsillectomy. Not an absolute indication. Page 39 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: USPECTED TONSILLAR MALIGNANCY is an ABSOLUTE indication for tonsillectomy biopsy tonsillectomy). Also absolute: (1) Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) in children, (2) econd episode quinsy, (3) Suspected malignancy. Notes in file confirm: indications = SA + 2nd Quinsy + Suspected malignancy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Part of other surgical procedures (uvu lopalatopharyng oplasty)
choices.explanation: PPP for OSA may include tonsillectomy but this is not a standalone absolute indication or tonsillectomy.

questions.keyPoint: ABSOLUTE INDICATIONS for TONSILLECTOMY: (1) SUSPECTED MALIGNANCY (immediate diagnostic tonsillectomy), (2) OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNOEA (children), (3) SECOND episode of peritonsillar abscess (Quinsy). RELATIVE: Chronic tonsillitis (Paradise criteria: ≥7/year, ≥5 for 2y, ≥3 for 3y).

questions.stem: Clinical features of chronic suppurative otitis media — which is TRUE? Clinical features of chronic suppurative otitis media — which is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offensive discharge in the mucosal type
choices.explanation: OFFENSIVE (foul-smelling) discharge = SQUAMOUS (atticoantral/cholesteatoma) type. MUCOSAL (tubotympanic) type = NON-OFFENSIVE mucopurulent discharge.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Otalgia is usually associated with ACUTE conditions. In CSOM, pain is often ABSENT unless a complication develops (acute exacerbation, mastoiditis).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: In CSOM, the TM is ALWAYS PERFORATED (central in mucosal type, peripheral/attic in squamous type). A normal TM excludes CSOM by definition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Profuse mucous discharge in the squamous type
choices.explanation: Squamous type (cholesteatoma) = SCANTY, FOUL-SMELLING discharge. MUCOSAL type = PROFUSE mucopurulent discharge. Page 40 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Deafness is conductive in type and more severe in the squamous disease
choices.explanation: True statement: Both types cause CONDUCTIVE HL. SQUAMOUS type (cholesteatoma) causes MORE SEVERE HL because cholesteatoma erodes ossicles (ossicular chain destruction → severe conductive HL). Note also: Squamous type may also cause SNHL if labyrinthine fistula develops.

questions.keyPoint: CSOM: Both types = CONDUCTIVE HL. SQUAMOUS type (cholesteatoma) = MORE SEVERE HL (ossicular erosion by cholesteatoma). Mucosal type = non-offensive discharge + central perforation. Squamous type = offensive discharge + attic/peripheral perforation + ossicular erosion.

questions.stem: Tonsillar tumours — which is TRUE? Tumor of tonsils — which is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hodgkin lymphoma is the most common lymphoma in tonsil
choices.explanation: NON-HODGKIN LYMPHOMA is the most common lymphoma of the tonsil. Hodgkin lymphoma more commonly affects cervical LN, less frequently the tonsillar tissue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Biopsy tonsillectomy is one of relative indications for tonsillectomy
choices.explanation: Biopsy tonsillectomy (for suspected malignancy) is an ABSOLUTE indication, not relative.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asymmetry in size of tonsil is grave sign should be taken seriously always
choices.explanation: TRUE: Unilateral tonsillar asymmetry (one tonsil enlarged) MUST always be taken seriously as it may represent an underlying malignancy (tonsillar SCC or lymphoma). Should prompt urgent referral and possible biopsy tonsillectomy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Squamous cell carcinoma is the most common type of malignancy
choices.explanation: SCC is the MOST COMMON MALIGNANCY of the tonsil (most common oropharyngeal malignancy overall). Associated with smoking, alcohol, and HPV-16. File shows D as correct. Page 41 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lymphoma of the tonsil usually appears as very ugly ulcer
choices.explanation: Tonsillar LYMPHOMA typically presents as a SMOOTH, FLESHY, NON-ULCERATED MASS (unlike SCC which often ulcerates).

questions.keyPoint: MOST COMMON tonsillar malignancy = SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA. Tonsillar SCC associated with: smoking + alcohol + HPV-16 (increasingly common). Non-Hodgkin lymphoma = most common LYMPHOMA of tonsil. Asymmetric tonsil = always investigate for malignancy.

questions.stem: 45-YEAR-OLD POORLY CONTROLLED DIABETIC: FLUCTUANT SWELLING at ANTERIOR BORDER OF SCM with OROPHARYNGEAL SWELLING. HIGH FEVER + ODYNOPHAGIA. History of ipsilateral dental problem. Best treatment? The best treatment for this patient is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transoral abscess drainage with intravenous antibiotics
choices.explanation: Transoral drainage (through the mouth) is appropriate for OROPHARYNGEAL/peritonsillar abscesses accessible via the oral cavity. This patient has a DEEP NECK SPACE infection (at anterior border of SCM = parapharyngeal) — not safely accessible transorally.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: DEEP NECK SPACE INFECTIONS in diabetics require: (1) TRANSCUTANEOUS (external skin incision) DRAINAGE of the abscess → thorough evacuation, (2) IV ANTIBIOTICS (broad spectrum, anti-anaerobic coverage). Poorly controlled diabetics at higher risk of severe complications (mediastinitis, septicaemia). Complete drainage + systemic antibiotics essential.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Needle aspiration of the oropharyngeal swelling
choices.explanation: Needle aspiration is for smaller abscesses or diagnostic step. Inadequate for deep neck abscesses — risk of incomplete drainage → recurrence.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Transcutaneous drainage alone WITHOUT IV antibiotics is incomplete — infection will persist. Both drainage AND IV antibiotics required, especially in a diabetic. Page 42 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Transoral drainage inadequate for deep neck (parapharyngeal) space — inaccessible transorally.

questions.keyPoint: DEEP NECK SPACE INFECTIONS: TRANSCUTANEOUS DRAINAGE + IV ANTIBIOTICS. Complete external drainage + systemic antibiotics especially important in DIABETICS (risk of mediastinitis, septicaemia, carotid erosion). Deep neck space infections can rapidly descend to the mediastinum (DESCENDING NECROTISING MEDIASTINITIS).

questions.stem: Otoscopy findings of Acute Suppurative Otitis Media (ASOM) in the suppurative stage. Otoscopy findings of ASOM in the suppurative stage include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Horizontal malleus handle = STAGE OF TUBAL OBSTRUCTION (early OME) — the tympanic membrane is RETRACTED, pulling the malleus into a more horizontal position.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Retracted TM = OME/Eustachian tube obstruction stage. In ASOM SUPPURATIVE STAGE the TM is BULGING outward (pressure from pus pushing it outward), not retracted.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ASOM SUPPURATIVE STAGE: The middle ear cavity fills with PURULENT EXUDATE under pressure → TM becomes BULGING/CONVEX (pushed outward). This is the MOST IMPORTANT finding. Loss of normal cone of light, congested blood vessels on TM, erythema. May proceed to spontaneous perforation (suppurative discharge).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pinpoint perforation is a LATE stage of ASOM — after the TM ruptures. Not a finding IN the suppurative stage (before rupture).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Prominent lateral process of malleus suggests retraction, not bulging. The lateral process becomes more prominent when the TM is retracted (sucked inward in negative pressure states).

questions.keyPoint: ASOM SUPPURATIVE STAGE = BULGING TYMPANIC MEMBRANE (pus under pressure pushes TM outward). Stages of ASOM: Tubal obstruction (retraction) → Hyperaemia (erythema) → Suppuration (BULGING) → Resolution (or Rupture → discharge). Treat before rupture: amoxicillin.

questions.stem: Laryngeal carcinoma — which is TRUE? Laryngeal Carcinoma — which is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A small laryngeal carcinoma in the right vocal cord with fixation of the vocal cord is staged as T2 in TNM staging
choices.explanation: VOCAL CORD FIXATION = T3 (not T2). T2 = tumour extends to supraglottis or subglottis with normal cord mobility. T3 = cord fixation (due to invasion of thyroarytenoid or cricoarytenoid joint).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Even the most benign-looking laryngeal polyp could harbor malignancy
choices.explanation: TRUE: Any laryngeal lesion, regardless of appearance, COULD be malignant. Vocal cord polyps and nodules usually benign but can occasionally harbour malignancy. This is why ALL laryngeal excised specimens MUST be sent for histopathology.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intermittent hoarseness worsening at the end of the day is a cardinal symptom
choices.explanation: Intermittent worsening hoarseness is more characteristic of BENIGN lesions (vocal cord nodules = voice worse at end of day after prolonged use). Laryngeal CARCINOMA causes PERSISTENT, PROGRESSIVE hoarseness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total laryngectomy with or without thyroidectomy is the best option for early glottic carcinoma
choices.explanation: EARLY (T1-T2) glottic carcinoma is treated with RADIOTHERAPY or endoscopic laser resection (voice-preserving). Total laryngectomy = reserved for advanced disease or RT failure (salvage).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Supraglottic carcinoma shows better prognosis when compared with glottic carcinoma
choices.explanation: GLOTTIC CARCINOMA has BETTER prognosis (earliest symptom = hoarseness → early detection, least lymphatic drainage). SUPRAGLOTTIC has WORSE prognosis (rich lymphatic drainage, late symptoms).

questions.keyPoint: VOCAL CORD FIXATION = T3 in TNM staging. ALL laryngeal excised specimens need histopathology regardless of appearance. Glottic Ca: BEST PROGNOSIS (early hoarseness + least LN drainage). Supraglottic: WORST PROGNOSIS. Early glottic (T1) = RT or laser (voice-preserving).

questions.stem: All of the following arteries contribute to anastomoses at Little's area EXCEPT: All of the following arteries contribute to anastomoses at Little's area EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Superior labial artery (from facial artery) DOES contribute to Little's area anastomoses.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Greater palatine artery (from internal maxillary artery) ascends through the incisive foramen to contribute to the septal supply at Little's area.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Anterior ethmoidal artery (from ophthalmic artery, branch of ICA) descends to the nasal septum and contributes to Little's area.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sphenopalatine artery (from internal maxillary artery) = MAIN BLOOD SUPPLY to the nasal cavity. Nasopalatine branch supplies the anterior septum at Little's area.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: POSTERIOR ETHMOIDAL ARTERY does NOT contribute to Kiesselbach's plexus (Little's area) on the ANTERIOR nasal septum. It supplies the posterior superior nasal cavity and cribriform plate region. Little's area vessels: anterior ethmoidal (ICA), superior labial (ECA), greater palatine (ECA), and sphenopalatine (ECA).

questions.keyPoint: LITTLE'S AREA (Kiesselbach's plexus): Anterior nasal septum. Supplied by (LEGS): Labial superior, Ethmoidal anterior, Greater palatine, Sphenopalatine. POSTERIOR ethmoidal is NOT part of Little's area (supplies posterior nasal cavity). Site of 90% of anterior epistaxis.

questions.stem: The CORNERSTONE option in the treatment of allergic rhinitis. The cornerstone option in the treatment of allergic rhinitis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) useful especially when allergic rhinitis coexists with asthma. Second-line addition. Not the cornerstone.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: INTRANASAL CORTICOSTEROIDS (e.g., fluticasone, mometasone, budesonide) = CORNERSTONE/FIRST-LINE treatment for PERSISTENT allergic rhinitis. Most effective treatment for nasal symptoms — reduces mucosal inflammation, oedema, secretions. Superior to antihistamines for nasal congestion.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Subcutaneous or sublingual immunotherapy (desensitisation) is used for PERSISTENT MODERATE-SEVERE allergic rhinitis not controlled by pharmacotherapy. Modifies the immune response but is NOT the first-line cornerstone.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Systemic steroids may provide rapid relief in severe acute exacerbations but are NOT appropriate as cornerstone treatment due to significant side effects. Short course only if severe and uncontrolled.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Topical antihistamines (azelastine nasal spray) useful for mild/intermittent symptoms. Less effective than topical corticosteroids for the FULL range of allergic rhinitis symptoms.

questions.keyPoint: INTRANASAL CORTICOSTEROIDS = CORNERSTONE/GOLD STANDARD for persistent allergic rhinitis. Most effective for all nasal symptoms. Second-line: antihistamines (systemic or topical). Allergen avoidance + nasal saline irrigation = adjuncts. Immunotherapy for severe refractory cases.
