questions.stem: Single best answer questions — Davidson's Chapter 15. 1. Regarding hearing tests — which is TRUE? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 46 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: The optimum tuning fork to use is 128 Hz in frequency
choices.explanation: 128 Hz tuning fork is used to test VIBRATION sense (peripheral neuropathy assessment). For hearing testing, the standard tuning fork is 512 Hz (C512) — provides good balance between vibration and auditory sensation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Weber's test involves placing the tuning fork on the mastoid process
choices.explanation: Mastoid placement = RINNE test (bone conduction). WEBER test = tuning fork placed on the VERTEX (midline of forehead or top of head).

choices.label: C
choices.text: A normal ear should be Rinne negative
choices.explanation: RINNE POSITIVE = NORMAL (air conduction > bone conduction, AC>BC). RINNE NEGATIVE = ABNORMAL = conductive hearing loss (BC>AC). A normal ear is Rinne POSITIVE.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rinne's and Weber's tests do not differentiate between conductive and sensorineural deafness
choices.explanation: These tests ARE used to differentiate conductive vs SNHL. Weber: conductive HL → lateralises to AFFECTED ear (louder); SNHL → lateralises to UNAFFECTED ear. Rinne: conductive → negative; SNHL → positive.

choices.label: E
choices.text: In Weber's test, sound is heard louder in an ear with conductive deafness
choices.explanation: WEBER TEST: sound lateralises to the WORSE hearing ear in CONDUCTIVE HL (bone conduction transmitted through skull bypasses the blocked external/middle ear), and to the BETTER hearing ear in SNHL. Conductive HL ear: bone conduction relatively better because background noise doesn't mask it.

questions.keyPoint: WEBER lateralises to AFFECTED ear in CONDUCTIVE HL (bone conduction to the blocked side is relatively enhanced). Lateralises to UNAFFECTED ear in SNHL (inner ear/nerve damage on one side). RINNE POSITIVE = normal (AC>BC). RINNE NEGATIVE = conductive HL (BC>AC).

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 2. Causes of conductive deafness include: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 47 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: OTOSCLEROSIS causes PROGRESSIVE CONDUCTIVE HEARING LOSS from abnormal bone remodelling → stapes footplate fixation → impaired sound transmission. Affects females more, bilateral, AD inheritance, worse during pregnancy. Audiogram: conductive HL with CARHART'S NOTCH at 2 kHz on bone conduction. Treatment: hearing aid or STAPEDECTOMY.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Loud noise causes SENSORINEURAL hearing loss (NIHL = noise-induced HL). Acoustic trauma damages outer hair cells in the cochlea at the 4 kHz region (noise notch).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) causes UNILATERAL SNHL + tinnitus + vertigo. Sensorineural, not conductive.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Congenital rubella causes SNHL (part of rubella embryopathy: SNHL + cataracts + cardiac defects + microcephaly). Not conductive.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vestibular neuronitis causes VERTIGO (not hearing loss). It is a viral infection of the vestibular nerve — cochlear (hearing) function is preserved.

questions.keyPoint: CONDUCTIVE HL causes: OAMS — Otosclerosis, OME (glue ear), ASOM, Middle ear effusion, Wax (cerumen), external canal atresia, Otitis externa, TM perforation. SNHL causes: noise, age (presbycusis), Meniere, ototoxic drugs, genetic.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 3. Causes of sensorineural deafness include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Beta-blockers are not ototoxic. They do not cause SNHL.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Otitis media causes CONDUCTIVE hearing loss (middle ear effusion/TM perforation). Only causes SNHL if labyrinthine fistula or labyrinthitis develops (complication).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE: Abnormal bone remodelling of the temporal bone → compression of CN VIII (vestibulocochlear nerve) in the internal auditory meatus OR involvement of the cochlea → SNHL. Paget's commonly affects the skull base and temporal bone. Also causes conductive HL from ossicular involvement (less common). Lab: markedly raised ALP with normal calcium/phosphate.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia affects CN V → lancinating facial pain. No relationship to hearing loss.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Bell's palsy = idiopathic CN VII palsy → facial weakness. Not associated with hearing loss (though the facial nerve travels close to CN VIII, Bell's palsy does NOT cause hearing loss). Page 48 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: PAGET'S DISEASE OF BONE → SNHL (cochlear/CN VIII involvement). OTOTOXIC drugs causing SNHL: Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin), Loop diuretics (furosemide), Cisplatin (chemotherapy), Quinine, Aspirin (high dose), Vancomycin (GAACQV mnemonic).

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 4. Causes of ear discharge include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Meniere's causes: episodic vertigo + SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. NO ear discharge — it is an inner ear (endolymphatic) condition without external manifestation of discharge.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Presbycusis = age-related SNHL. No ear discharge.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CN VIII palsy causes SNHL and/or vertigo. No ear discharge.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CHOLESTEATOMA (CSOM squamous type) = one of the most important causes of PERSISTENT, FOUL-SMELLING (offensive/foetid) ear discharge. The keratin debris in the cholesteatoma sac becomes infected by Pseudomonas/anaerobes → distinctive foul-smelling otorrhoea. Also causes: conductive HL + tympanic membrane attic/peripheral perforation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Tinnitus = ringing/buzzing in the ear (auditory perception without external sound). Not associated with ear discharge.

questions.keyPoint: CHOLESTEATOMA = FOUL-SMELLING (offensive/foetid) ear discharge + attic/peripheral TM perforation + conductive HL. Caused by keratin debris + infection (Pseudomonas, anaerobes). LOCALLY DESTRUCTIVE — erodes ossicles, facial nerve canal, labyrinth. Treatment: MASTOIDECTOMY.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 5. Causes of sensorineural deafness include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Beta-blockers not ototoxic.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Causes CONDUCTIVE HL, not SNHL (unless complication develops).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: As discussed in Q3 — Paget's disease involves temporal bone → SNHL from cochlear or CN VIII involvement.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CN V facial pain. No hearing loss.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CN VII motor palsy. No hearing loss.

questions.keyPoint: REPEATED TOPIC: Paget's disease of bone is an important cause of SNHL due to temporal bone involvement. Key feature: markedly elevated ALP with normal calcium and phosphate. Other SNHL causes to memorise: Presbycusis, NIHL, Meniere's, ototoxic drugs, acoustic neuroma, viral labyrinthitis.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 6. Causes of ear pain include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Presbycusis = progressive bilateral SNHL in elderly. No otalgia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: OTITIS MEDIA (acute suppurative or chronic exacerbation) is a major cause of ear pain (otalgia). ASOM: severe ear pain + fever + TM bulging → pain relieves after spontaneous perforation. Also causes otalgia: otitis externa, foreign body, furunculosis, referred otalgia (dental/pharyngeal/laryngeal).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Tinnitus is a sound phenomenon (ringing/buzzing). Not a cause of otalgia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Meniere's: episodic vertigo + SNHL + tinnitus + AURAL FULLNESS. Not primarily ear pain.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: BPPV: positional vertigo without ear pain.

questions.keyPoint: CAUSES OF OTALGIA: DIRECT: AOM, otitis externa, herpes zoster oticus (Ramsay Hunt), furuncle, TM perforation, cholesteatoma. REFERRED: dental (V3), TMJ, pharyngeal/tonsillar (CN IX), laryngeal (CN X). Key: otalgia + NORMAL otoscopy = look for referred source.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 7. Which of the following measures is NOT used to treat epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Anterior nasal packing (BIPP or Merocel sponge) IS used for anterior epistaxis not controlled by first aid. Posterior packing for posterior epistaxis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Arterial ligation (sphenopalatine artery, external carotid artery) IS used for refractory posterior epistaxis. Endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation (ESAL) is now preferred.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Foley catheter (used as posterior nasal pack) IS used to tamponade posterior epistaxis. The balloon provides tamponade to the choana.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Chemical cautery (silver nitrate) or electrocautery IS used for anterior epistaxis (Little's area). Must not cauterise both sides simultaneously (risk of septal perforation).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: SENGSTAKEN-BLAKEMORE TUBE is used for OESOPHAGEAL VARICES (not epistaxis). It has oesophageal and gastric balloons for tamponade of variceal bleeding. It has NO ROLE in epistaxis management.

questions.keyPoint: EPISTAXIS TREATMENT: First aid (pinch nose, lean forward, ice pack) → Cautery (silver nitrate or electrocautery) → Anterior packing (Merocel/BIPP) → Posterior packing (Foley catheter/balloon) → Arterial ligation/embolisation. SENGSTAKEN-BLAKEMORE TUBE = for oesophageal varices, NOT epistaxis.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 8. Which of the following is NOT a cause of chronic nasal obstruction? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 51 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DNS IS a cause of chronic unilateral nasal obstruction.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Choanal atresia (bony/membranous obstruction of posterior nasal aperture) IS a cause of nasal obstruction (bilateral → neonatal emergency; unilateral → diagnosed later in childhood).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: KALLMANN'S SYNDROME (hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism + ANOSMIA) causes ANOSMIA (loss of smell) NOT nasal obstruction. The anosmia is from failure of olfactory nerve development (olfactory bulb aplasia). No structural nasal obstruction occurs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Enlarged adenoids IS a major cause of chronic nasal obstruction + snoring + recurrent OM in children.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Sinusitis with nasal polyposis IS a cause of chronic nasal obstruction (from mucosal oedema + polyps blocking the nasal airway).

questions.keyPoint: KALLMANN'S SYNDROME: Hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism + ANOSMIA (not obstruction). The ONLY nasal symptom = anosmia (from failed olfactory bulb development). Associated with: colour blindness, cleft lip/palate, cryptorchidism, synkinesis. X-linked or autosomal. No nasal OBSTRUCTION.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 9. Causes of a hoarse voice include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Graves' disease (hyperthyroidism) can cause laryngeal oedema in severe cases but is NOT a primary cause of hoarse voice. The associated enlarged thyroid can rarely compress the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BRONCHIAL CARCINOMA can cause HOARSE VOICE by involving the LEFT RECURRENT LARYNGEAL NERVE (RLN). The left RLN hooks around the aortic arch (ligamentum arteriosum) → vulnerable to compression/invasion by left hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy or left lung cancer → LEFT VOCAL CORD PALSY → hoarseness.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Oesophagitis (GERD) causes heartburn, dysphagia, odynophagia. Laryngopharyngeal reflux (LPR) can cause hoarseness but not by the mechanism of bronchial carcinoma.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Choanal atresia causes nasal obstruction in neonates. No effect on the larynx/voice.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pleural effusion does not cause hoarseness. Large effusions may rarely compress mediastinal structures.

questions.keyPoint: BRONCHIAL CARCINOMA → HOARSE VOICE via LEFT RECURRENT LARYNGEAL NERVE palsy. Left RLN loops under aortic arch → vulnerable to left hilar/mediastinal disease. Left vocal cord palsy = bovine cough + hoarseness. Other causes of RLN palsy: thyroid surgery, thyroid Ca, mediastinal LN, aortic aneurysm.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch 15 SBA questions. 10. Ménière's disease:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Most cases of Meniere's disease are SPORADIC. Familial cases do exist (5-15%) with autosomal dominant pattern in some families. NOT consistently autosomal recessive.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Meniere's disease has no relationship to epistaxis (nosebleed). It is an inner ear condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Is triggered by a viral illness
choices.explanation: Vestibular NEURITIS is triggered by viral illness. Meniere's disease is caused by endolymphatic hydrops — triggers include high sodium diet, stress, and caffeine — not primarily viral.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Gabapentin is used for neuropathic pain and epilepsy. Meniere's treatment: betahistine + diuretics + low-salt diet (medical). Intratympanic gentamicin (chemical ablation) or endolymphatic sac decompression (surgical) for refractory cases.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MENIERE'S DISEASE CAUSES NYSTAGMUS during an acute vertiginous attack. The nystagmus is HORIZONTAL (with rotatory component), beating AWAY from the affected side (fast phase toward unaffected side). It is peripheral nystagmus: fatigable + direction toward affected side when acute (inhibition of the hydropic labyrinth).

questions.keyPoint: MENIERE'S DISEASE: Endolymphatic hydrops → tetrad: Episodic VERTIGO (20min-12h) + SNHL (low-frequency) + TINNITUS + AURAL FULLNESS. NYSTAGMUS present during acute attack (horizontal, peripheral type). Treatment: low-salt diet + betahistine + thiazide diuretics.

questions.topic: Which sinus does NOT open on lateral wall
questions.stem: Bailey & Love Chapter 43 — Multiple choice questions. 1. Which of the following sinuses do NOT open on the lateral wall of the nose?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Opens into the hiatus semilunaris in the MIDDLE MEATUS (lateral wall). Unfortunately its ostium is positioned HIGH on the medial wall — poor gravitational drainage.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Anterior ethmoid opens into MIDDLE MEATUS; Posterior ethmoid into SUPERIOR MEATUS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Opens into MIDDLE MEATUS (via frontonasal recess/duct) = lateral wall.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SPHENOID SINUS opens into the SPHENOETHMOIDAL RECESS (above the superior turbinate, medially/posteriorly) — NOT directly on the lateral wall of the nose (unlike the other sinuses).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The nasal septum is not a paranasal sinus. This is a distractor.

questions.keyPoint: Sinus drainage: Maxillary → Middle meatus. Frontal → Middle meatus. Anterior ethmoid → Middle meatus. Posterior ethmoid → Superior meatus. SPHENOID → SPHENOETHMOIDAL RECESS (not lateral wall proper). Inferior meatus: only nasolacrimal duct drains here.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 2. Via which route does the greater palatine artery supply the nasal septum?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Foramen rotundum = V2 (maxillary division of trigeminal nerve). Not for vascular supply. Page 54 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Vidian canal transmits the vidian nerve (greater + deep petrosal nerves). Not the greater palatine artery.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The GREATER PALATINE ARTERY ascends through the INCISIVE CANAL (incisive foramen) from the hard palate into the nasal cavity to supply the NASAL SEPTUM anteriorly (contributing to Little's area/Kiesselbach's plexus).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sphenopalatine artery (main nasal blood supply) passes through the sphenopalatine foramen — different artery. The greater palatine uses the incisive canal.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cribriform plate transmits olfactory nerve fibres + anterior ethmoidal nerve/artery into the nasal cavity from above.

questions.keyPoint: GREATER PALATINE ARTERY → INCISIVE CANAL → nasal septum (contributes to Little's area). SPHENOPALATINE ARTERY → sphenopalatine foramen → main nasal supply. Both are branches of the internal maxillary artery (from ECA).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 3. Intracranial drainage of venous blood from nose and sinuses passes to cavernous sinus via:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The OPHTHALMIC VEINS (superior and inferior) drain NASAL and FACIAL venous blood to the CAVERNOUS SINUS. This creates a pathway for spread of infection from the danger triangle of the face → cavernous sinus → CAVERNOUS SINUS THROMBOSIS (a feared complication of nasal/facial infections).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pharyngeal plexus drains blood from the pharynx. Not the route from nose to cavernous sinus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Pterygoid plexus drains deep face/infratemporal fossa. Can communicate with cavernous sinus but not the primary route from the nose.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Facial vein drains to the external jugular. However, it communicates with the cavernous sinus VIA the ophthalmic veins (not directly).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Drains the forehead. Not the primary route from nasal/sinus blood to cavernous sinus.

questions.keyPoint: OPHTHALMIC VEINS → CAVERNOUS SINUS. This is the route by which infection from the DANGER TRIANGLE of the face (nose + upper lip + cheeks) can spread to cause CAVERNOUS SINUS THROMBOSIS (CST). CST signs: proptosis, chemosis, CN III/IV/VI/V1 palsies, fever, meningism.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 4. Which view(s) are necessary on CT for detailed assessment of the sinuses?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Coronal alone misses other planes of assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Axial alone misses important coronal information about sinus drainage.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Two planes — closer but incomplete.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Two planes — still incomplete.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: HIGH-RESOLUTION CT OF SINUSES: All THREE PLANES needed for complete assessment — (1) CORONAL: shows ostia drainage, sinonasal anatomy, OMC; (2) AXIAL: shows posterior sinuses, sphenoid, lateral relationships; (3) SAGITTAL: shows frontal sinus drainage, anterior-posterior depth. Modern CBCT or multiplanar reconstruction allows all three.

questions.keyPoint: CT SINUSES: All three planes (CORONAL + AXIAL + SAGITTAL) needed for complete paranasal sinus assessment. Coronal most important for OSTIOMEATAL COMPLEX (OMC) assessment. MRI better for soft tissue/malignancy assessment. Plain X-ray now largely obsolete for sinus assessment.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 5. Injury to which blood vessel is associated with severe post-traumatic epistaxis? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 56 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: External carotid branches (sphenopalatine, internal maxillary) supply the nose. But the SPECIFIC vessel in severe post-traumatic epistaxis (especially from skull base fracture) is the anterior ethmoid artery.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Facial artery supplies the superior labial artery (contributes to Little's area) but is not the vessel associated with SEVERE post-traumatic epistaxis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Main nasal blood supply — causes severe POSTERIOR epistaxis (non-traumatic). The SPECIFIC post-traumatic association is anterior ethmoidal.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ANTERIOR ETHMOIDAL ARTERY (branch of ophthalmic artery, from ICA) runs along the roof of the ethmoid and can be injured in naso-orbito-ethmoid fractures → SEVERE POST-TRAUMATIC EPISTAXIS that is difficult to control with packing (because it is in the superior nasal cavity, above the level of nasal packing).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Not a standard anatomical term.

questions.keyPoint: ANTERIOR ETHMOIDAL ARTERY (from ICA via ophthalmic artery) = severe post-traumatic epistaxis, especially with NOE (naso-orbito-ethmoid) fractures. Bleeds from above the packing level — may need ENDOSCOPIC LIGATION or EMBOLISATION. Not controllable with simple anterior packing.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 6. How can clear nasal discharge be confirmed as CSF rather than nasal mucus?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CT scan can identify the SITE of CSF leak (bone defect in skull base) but does NOT confirm that the discharge IS CSF vs mucus.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CSF contains GLUCOSE (as blood does). Nasal mucus contains little to no glucose. Bedside GLUCOSE STICK TEST (dipstick test for glucose) is a quick screening test: POSITIVE glucose = suggests CSF. However, the DEFINITIVE test is BETA-2 TRANSFERRIN (a protein found ONLY in CSF, perilymph, and aqueous humour — not in nasal secretions, blood, or serum).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MRI can show CSF leak site and cisternal connections but does not confirm the DISCHARGE composition biochemically.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Endoscopy can identify the site of CSF leak (clear fluid dripping from cribriform plate area) but does not biochemically confirm it is CSF vs mucus.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased production with dependent head position
choices.explanation: Increased discharge with head down (Valsalva) suggests CSF leak clinically but is not a definitive confirmatory test. Page 57 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: CSF RHINORRHOEA CONFIRMATION: Bedside = GLUCOSE STICK TEST (CSF has glucose). Definitive = BETA-2 TRANSFERRIN assay (CSF-specific protein — not found in blood or nasal secretions). "Halo sign" on filter paper (blood/CSF mixture) is unreliable. CT cisternography or MRI cisternography can localise the leak.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 7. How should suspected nasal bone fracture be managed?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Nasal bone X-rays have poor sensitivity and specificity. Most nasal bone fractures are clinical diagnoses — X-rays rarely change management.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Immediate manipulation is NOT recommended — the nose is swollen and its true shape cannot be assessed until oedema subsides (5-10 days). Immediate manipulation risks re-fracturing or worsening the fracture.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: NASAL BONE FRACTURE MANAGEMENT: Review at 5-7 days (after oedema subsides) to assess whether deformity requires MANIPULATION UNDER ANAESTHESIA (MUA). MUA must be performed within 2-3 weeks of the injury (while cartilage is still mobile — before union). If the patient waits >3 weeks = rhinoplasty required.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Rhinoplasty is elective cosmetic surgery — performed if fracture untreated >3 weeks or for longstanding deformity. Not the immediate management of a fresh fracture.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: EUA without manipulation is not indicated for a simple nasal fracture.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL FRACTURE: Review at 5-7 days (after oedema resolves). If deformity present → MUA (manipulation under anaesthesia) within 2-3 weeks of injury (before union). Plain X-ray NOT routinely indicated (clinical diagnosis). >3 weeks = rhinoplasty.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 8. Which of the following is NOT a complication of nasal septal haematoma?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Meningitis IS a complication — if septal abscess develops and infection spreads intracranially through the cribriform plate or via cavernous sinus thrombosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Septal abscess IS a direct complication of an infected septal haematoma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Saddle-nose deformity IS a complication of untreated haematoma → avascular necrosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CST IS a complication — septal abscess → spread via ophthalmic veins → cavernous sinus thrombosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: ISCHAEMIA OF THE SOFT PALATE is NOT a complication of septal haematoma. The soft palate has a completely separate blood supply (lesser palatine artery, ascending palatine artery) independent of the nasal septum.

questions.keyPoint: Complications of SEPTAL HAEMATOMA: Saddle-nose (most common), Septal abscess, Meningitis (via abscess spread), Cavernous sinus thrombosis. ISCHAEMIA OF SOFT PALATE is NOT a complication — different anatomical territory with separate blood supply.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 9. Which of the following procedures involve correction of a deviated nasal septum with PRESERVATION OF CARTILAGE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SMR (Killlian's) removes cartilage and bone of the septum. NOT cartilage-preserving.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: SEPTOPLASTY (Cottle's operation) is the MODERN procedure for DNS correction. It involves precise mobilisation, reshaping, and repositioning of deviated cartilage with PRESERVATION of the structural cartilage. Only the deviated portions are removed/reshaped. Preferred over SMR because it preserves the nasal dorsal and caudal struts → prevents saddle-nose. Page 59 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Turbinectomy removes the inferior turbinate (for turbinate hypertrophy). Not for septal deviation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Submucous diathermy reduces turbinate size by destroying submucosal vascular tissue. For turbinate hypertrophy, not septal deviation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Septodermoplasty is used for HEREDITARY HAEMORRHAGIC TELANGIECTASIA (HHT) — replaces the telangiectatic nasal mucosa with a skin graft to prevent epistaxis.

questions.keyPoint: SEPTOPLASTY (Cottle's) = DNS correction with CARTILAGE PRESERVATION. Mobilise, reshape, reposition deviated cartilage. Modern standard over SMR. SUBMUCOUS RESECTION (SMR) = older, removes cartilage (risk of saddle-nose). Remember: septodermoplasty = for HHT/epistaxis.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 10. Which of the following is NOT included in the aetiology of nasal septal perforation?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ALLERGY is NOT a recognised cause of nasal septal perforation. Allergy causes rhinitis, polyps, and turbinate hypertrophy — but does not cause septal perforations.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Infections (syphilis, TB, leprosy, kala-azar, rhinoscleroma, and septal ABSCESS) DO cause septal perforation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Vasculitis (Wegener's granulomatosis/GPA, sarcoidosis) DOES cause septal perforation via granulomatous destruction.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Malignant tumours of the nasal septum (SCC, lymphoma) CAN cause septal perforation by erosion.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Toxic causes (cocaine abuse, chromium/nickel industrial exposure, intranasal cocaine) DO cause septal perforation by vasoconstriction → ischaemic necrosis.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL SEPTAL PERFORATION causes: TOXIC (cocaine, chromium), INFECTIVE (syphilis, TB, septal abscess), VASCULITIS (Wegener's/GPA, sarcoidosis), IATROGENIC (post-septoplasty/SMR), TRAUMA, MALIGNANCY. ALLERGY does NOT cause septal perforation.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 11. Which of the following are NOT complications of nasal septal perforation?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Epistaxis IS a complication of septal perforation — exposed dry cartilage/bone at perforation margins bleeds easily.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Whistling/whistling sound on breathing IS a complication — turbulent airflow through the perforation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Nasal obstruction IS a complication — altered airflow dynamics.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: NASAL POLYPS are NOT a complication of septal perforation. Polyps are caused by allergic/eosinophilic inflammation in the sinonasal mucosa. They are a separate pathological entity.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cosmetic deformity (saddle-nose) IS a complication — large perforations destabilise the nasal dorsum → saddle-nose collapse.

questions.keyPoint: SEPTAL PERFORATION COMPLICATIONS: Epistaxis, Whistling (turbulent airflow), Nasal obstruction (altered dynamics), Crusting, Saddle-nose deformity (large perforations). NASAL POLYPS are NOT a complication of septal perforation.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 12. Which of the following regarding Wegener's granulomatosis are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Wegener's (GPA) is a SYSTEMIC VASCULITIS affecting multiple organs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is best diagnosed by CT scanning
choices.explanation: CT can show extent. Best DIAGNOSED by BIOPSY (nasal/lung/kidney) + c-ANCA titre. Page 61 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.text: It can affect the nose, lungs and kidneys
choices.explanation: WEGENER'S GRANULOMATOSIS (GPA = Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis) = CLASSIC TRIAD: (1) UPPER RESPIRATORY TRACT (saddle-nose, septal perforation, sinusitis, epistaxis), (2) LOWER RESPIRATORY TRACT (lungs: nodules, cavities, haemoptysis), (3) KIDNEYS (glomerulonephritis, haematuria → renal failure). c-ANCA (PR3-ANCA) highly specific.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Wegener's treatment = IMMUNOSUPPRESSION: cyclophosphamide + prednisolone (remission induction) → maintenance with methotrexate or azathioprine. Surgery only for complications.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It tends to spare nasal septal cartilage
choices.explanation: Wegener's DESTROYS nasal septal cartilage → saddle-nose deformity + septal perforation.

questions.keyPoint: WEGENER'S (GPA) = UPPER airway (saddle-nose, sinusitis, epistaxis) + LUNGS (nodules, cavities) + KIDNEYS (glomerulonephritis). c-ANCA (PR3) highly specific. Treatment: cyclophosphamide + prednisolone. NOT managed surgically.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 13. Which of the following is NOT a cause of epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Trauma (digital picking, nasal fractures, facial trauma) IS the most common cause of anterior epistaxis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Nasal foreign body in children IS a cause of unilateral foul-smelling bloody discharge.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Upper respiratory tract infections cause mucosal inflammation and fragility → epistaxis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Granulomatous diseases (Wegener's, sarcoidosis, TB) DO cause epistaxis via mucosal destruction.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: OLD AGE per se is NOT a cause of epistaxis. However, elderly patients more commonly have POSTERIOR EPISTAXIS from hypertension and arteriosclerosis — the age itself is not the cause, the associated conditions are. The file marks old age as the answer (the exception), which aligns with the concept that age alone is not an aetiological factor.

questions.keyPoint: EPISTAXIS CAUSES: Local (trauma, FB, infection, polyps, angiofibroma, malignancy) + Systemic (hypertension, coagulopathy, anticoagulants, HHT, Wegener's). Old age per se is not a cause — associated conditions in elderly (hypertension, arteriosclerosis, anticoagulants) are the actual causes.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 14. What gender/age group does juvenile angiofibroma tend to affect?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Lower age range. Juvenile angiofibroma typically presents from mid-teenage years.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Juvenile angiofibroma occurs almost exclusively in MALES.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: JUVENILE ANGIOFIBROMA (Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma/JNA): occurs almost EXCLUSIVELY in ADOLESCENT MALES aged 14-18 years. Highly vascular, locally invasive tumour arising from the superior margin of the sphenopalatine foramen. Androgen-dependent. Presents with: recurrent profuse epistaxis + nasal obstruction.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: JNA is almost exclusively in MALES. If found in a female, biopsy to confirm diagnosis is essential (diagnose before assuming).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Juvenile angiofibroma predominantly occurs in adolescent MALES aged 14-18.

questions.keyPoint: JUVENILE NASOPHARYNGEAL ANGIOFIBROMA (JNA): ADOLESCENT MALES (14-18 years), recurrent profuse EPISTAXIS + unilateral nasal obstruction + cheek fullness (Holman-Miller sign on X-ray: anterior bowing of posterior wall of maxillary sinus). Androgen-sensitive. Pre-op embolisation then surgical excision.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 15. Which of the following statements about juvenile angiofibroma are TRUE? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 63 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BIOPSY IS CONTRAINDICATED due to risk of MASSIVE HAEMORRHAGE (highly vascular tumour). Diagnosis is made by imaging (CT + MRI) + angiography.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It should initially be treated medically
choices.explanation: Medical treatment alone (e.g., hormone manipulation) is not the standard primary treatment. Definitive treatment = surgical excision after pre-op embolisation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is associated with vascular malformations of the meninges
choices.explanation: JNA is a benign vascular tumour. Not associated with meningeal vascular malformations.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: JNA is DIAGNOSED BY IMAGING: CT (shows extent of bony erosion + Holman-Miller sign), MRI (shows soft tissue extent + intracranial extension), ANGIOGRAPHY (shows feeding vessels for embolisation). BIOPSY CONTRAINDICATED.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It may be cured by embolisation
choices.explanation: Embolisation ALONE does NOT cure JNA — it devascularises the tumour to REDUCE INTRAOPERATIVE BLEEDING before definitive surgical excision. Embolisation = pre-operative adjunct, not standalone treatment.

questions.keyPoint: JNA DIAGNOSIS = IMAGING (CT + MRI + angiography). BIOPSY CONTRAINDICATED (massive haemorrhage risk). Treatment: pre-op EMBOLISATION (reduces bleeding) → SURGICAL EXCISION (endoscopic preferred). Embolisation alone does NOT cure — surgical removal required.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 16. What may be involved in the management of LIFE-THREATENING epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Facial artery ligation is used for oral/facial bleeding, not nasal epistaxis specifically.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lingual artery supplies the tongue. Not appropriate for epistaxis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ligation of the external carotid artery ABOVE the origin of the lingual artery
choices.explanation: In life-threatening posterior epistaxis uncontrolled by packing and embolisation: EXTERNAL CAROTID ARTERY LIGATION above the lingual artery origin ligates branches (including facial, internal maxillary) supplying the nose while preserving the lingual branch below (to avoid tongue ischaemia). Modern preference: ENDOSCOPIC SPHENOPALATINE ARTERY LIGATION (ESAL). Page 64 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ligation of the W external carotid artery BELOW origin of lingual artery
choices.explanation: Ligating below the lingual origin would interrupt lingual supply → tongue ischaemia. Must be above.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ligation of the W internal carotid artery
choices.explanation: Ligation of the ICA = catastrophic (contralateral stroke in 50%). ABSOLUTELY NOT used for epistaxis.

questions.keyPoint: Life-threatening epistaxis: ECA ligation ABOVE lingual artery origin (preserves tongue blood supply). Modern preferred: ENDOSCOPIC SPHENOPALATINE ARTERY LIGATION (ESAL) — more targeted, less morbidity. NEVER ligate the ICA for epistaxis.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 17. What should be included in the management of hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT)-related epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Nasal packing is relatively contraindicated in HHT — repeated packing damages the fragile telangiectatic mucosa → more bleeding.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Repeated cautery can worsen telangiectasias and cause more damage to the fragile mucosa. Focal laser ablation is preferred over cautery.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: OESTROGEN CREAMS (topical oestrogen/progestogen) are used in HHT to induce squamous metaplasia of the nasal mucosa → reduces telangiectasia fragility and bleeding. Other treatments: LASER ABLATION (KTP/Nd:YAG), SEPTODERMOPLASTY (skin graft), BEVACIZUMAB (anti-VEGF), and EMBOLISATION for severe cases.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anterior ethmoidal artery ligation is used for specific cases but is not specifically listed as management for HHT epistaxis in this context.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Platelet transfusion is for thrombocytopaenia. HHT epistaxis is from vascular fragility, not platelet deficiency.

questions.keyPoint: HHT (Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome): AD inheritance, mucocutaneous telangiectasia, recurrent epistaxis, GI bleeding, pulmonary/cerebral AVMs. EPISTAXIS MANAGEMENT: oestrogen creams (squamous metaplasia) + laser ablation + septodermoplasty (skin graft) + bevacizumab.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 18. Where do the majority of nasal polyps arise from?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Antrochoanal polyps (Killian's) arise from the MAXILLARY sinus (antrum) and are solitary/unilateral. These are a SPECIAL TYPE, not the majority.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The MAJORITY of nasal polyps (ethmoidal polyps — the common bilateral type) arise from the ETHMOID SINUSES, specifically from the mucosa around the ethmoid bullae and middle meatus/OMC area. They are bilateral, grey, glistening, insensate, and associated with allergy/eosinophilia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Frontal sinuses are rarely a source of polyps.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sphenoid sinus polyps are very rare.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Septal lesions are usually not polyps — they are more likely tumours or haematomas.

questions.keyPoint: ETHMOID sinuses = majority source of nasal polyps (bilateral, eosinophilic). MAXILLARY sinus = antrochoanal polyp (Killian's polyp) = special type, unilateral, solitary, passes through choana. Unilateral polyp = investigate for malignancy. Bilateral = allergic/ASA sensitivity/CF.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 19. Which of the following is associated with nasal polyps? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 66 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Penicillin allergy causes drug hypersensitivity reactions (urticaria, anaphylaxis). Not associated with nasal polyps.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Chronic bronchitis (COPD) is not specifically associated with nasal polyps. Aspirin sensitivity is the asthma link.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ASPIRIN SENSITIVITY (ASA exacerbated respiratory disease) is strongly associated with nasal polyps. SAMTER'S TRIAD: (1) Nasal polyps, (2) Asthma, (3) Aspirin sensitivity. NSAIDs trigger aspirin-sensitive asthma and worsen nasal polyps. Pathogenesis: shunting of arachidonic acid to leukotriene pathway (COX-1 inhibition).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Gastric polyps are GI lesions. Not associated with sinonasal polyps.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Medullary thyroid cancer (MEN 2 syndrome) is not associated with nasal polyps.

questions.keyPoint: SAMTER'S TRIAD (Aspirin exacerbated respiratory disease/AERD): Nasal polyps + ASTHMA + ASPIRIN SENSITIVITY. Always ask about aspirin/NSAID sensitivity in patients with nasal polyps and asthma. Treatment: leukotriene antagonists (montelukast) + intranasal steroids + aspirin desensitisation.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 20. Which of the following is NOT commonly associated with nasal polyps?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: EPISTAXIS is NOT commonly associated with BENIGN NASAL POLYPS. Polyps are avascular, insensate grey masses — they do not bleed easily. Epistaxis with a nasal mass suggests MALIGNANCY (most important differential) or angiofibroma (in young males).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Nasal obstruction IS the primary symptom of nasal polyps (bilateral, progressive).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Anosmia IS associated with nasal polyps (polyps obstruct the olfactory cleft → olfactory loss).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Not applicable.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Not applicable.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL POLYPS: Bilateral nasal obstruction + anosmia + clear nasal discharge + reduced smell. EPISTAXIS is NOT a feature of benign polyps. Any nasal mass with EPISTAXIS or UNILATERAL PRESENTATION must be investigated for MALIGNANCY. Angiofibroma = epistaxis in adolescent males.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 21. What should be suspected in unilateral nasal polyps?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CF causes BILATERAL polyps in children. Unilateral polyp does not specifically suggest CF.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: UNILATERAL NASAL POLYP = suspect MALIGNANCY until proven otherwise. Any asymmetric/unilateral sinonasal mass may represent: inverted papilloma (malignant transformation risk 10-15%), SCC, lymphoma, rhabdomyosarcoma (children), or metastatic disease. Imaging + biopsy mandatory.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Allergy causes BILATERAL polyps (bilateral eosinophilic inflammation). Unilateral = not typical allergy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Fungal (allergic fungal) sinusitis can cause unilateral disease but is less common than malignancy as the primary concern for unilateral polyp.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cocaine causes septal perforation and mucosal damage. Not specifically associated with unilateral polyp.

questions.keyPoint: UNILATERAL NASAL POLYP = MALIGNANCY UNTIL PROVEN OTHERWISE. Must biopsy. Differential: inverted papilloma, SCC, lymphoma, angiofibroma (young males), rhabdomyosarcoma (children). Bilateral polyps = allergy, aspirin sensitivity, CF (children).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 22. Which of the following statements regarding surgery for nasal polyps are TRUE? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 68 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is recommended in all patients fit for surgery
choices.explanation: Surgery is NOT first-line. Medical treatment (intranasal steroids ± oral steroids) is tried first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is recommended as a long-term cure
choices.explanation: Polyps frequently RECUR after surgery. Surgery is NOT a cure — it provides symptom relief and facilitates steroid delivery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is recommended after failure of steroid treatments
choices.explanation: ENDOSCOPIC SINUS SURGERY (ESS/FESS) is recommended for polyps that FAIL medical treatment (intranasal steroids ± oral steroids course). ESS opens the OMC, removes polyps, and improves post-operative steroid delivery to sinonasal mucosa.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is recommended prior to CT scanning of the sinuses
choices.explanation: CT MUST be performed BEFORE surgery to delineate anatomy, identify anatomical variants, and assess extent of disease. Surgery without pre-op CT is dangerous (risk of orbital/skull base complications).

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is performed through an external incision to allow excision with clear margins
choices.explanation: Nasal polyposis is treated by ENDOSCOPIC (transnasal) surgery, not external incision. External approaches are for malignancy.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL POLYP SURGERY: ESS (FESS) recommended after FAILURE OF MEDICAL TREATMENT (intranasal steroids ± short oral steroids course). CT MUST be done BEFORE surgery. Surgery is NOT curative (polyps recur) — it improves quality of life and post-op steroid delivery.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 23. What is the most likely organism in maxillary sinusitis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: S. aureus causes chronic/recurrent sinusitis and is the organism in septal furuncles/abscesses. Not the most common in acute maxillary sinusitis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pseudomonas is common in chronic sinusitis (especially in CF and immunocompromised patients). Not the most common in acute community-acquired sinusitis. Page 69 iversity of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: EPTOCOCCUS PNEUMONIAE = MOST COMMON organism in ACUTE MAXILLARY USITIS (~40% of cases). Second most common: Haemophilus influenzae (~30%). ether (S. pneumoniae + H. influenzae) account for ~70% of acute bacterial sinusitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: trachomatis causes genital and ocular infections. Not a cause of sinusitis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: epidermidis = coagulase-negative staphylococcus. Normal commensal on skin/mucosa. the primary cause of maxillary sinusitis.

questions.keyPoint: MOST COMMON organism in ACUTE MAXILLARY SINUSITIS = STREPTOCOCCUS PNEUMONIAE (~40%). Second = Haemophilus influenzae (~30%). Third = Moraxella catarrhalis (~20%). Remember: SPHMM = Strep pneumo, H.influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis + Mixed anaerobes in chronic cases.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 24. Where is a trochar usually inserted in antral lavage?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The canine fossa approach is used for more complex endoscopic approaches (Caldwell-Luc operation). Not for simple trochar insertion in antral lavage.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ANTRAL LAVAGE (Proetz displacement, sinus washout): the trochar (cannula) is inserted through the INFERIOR MEATUS (beneath the inferior turbinate) into the MAXILLARY SINUS. The lateral nasal wall is thin here, allowing easy puncture into the maxillary antrum for irrigation and aspiration.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Middle meatus is the natural ostium of the maxillary sinus. Trochar access is through the INFERIOR meatus (thinner bone).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Percutaneous (through cheek/fossa canina) access is used in Caldwell-Luc procedure, not standard antral lavage.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Through the natural maxillary sinus ostium
choices.explanation: The natural ostium is accessed endoscopically in functional endoscopic sinus surgery, not by trochar during simple lavage.

questions.keyPoint: ANTRAL LAVAGE: Trochar inserted through INFERIOR MEATUS (beneath inferior turbinate) → maxillary sinus. Thin bone here allows easy penetration. Irrigation of the sinus washes out secretions and pus. Largely replaced by FESS (endoscopic sinus surgery) in modern practice.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 25. In acute sinusitis, pus may be seen draining from which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Inferior meatus receives drainage from the nasolacrimal duct only. Maxillary sinus drains into MIDDLE meatus, not inferior.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PUS IN MIDDLE MEATUS is the classic endoscopic finding in ACUTE MAXILLARY SINUSITIS. The maxillary, frontal, and anterior ethmoid sinuses all drain into the OSTIOMEATAL COMPLEX (OMC) in the MIDDLE MEATUS. Pus seen in the middle meatus on endoscopy = acute bacterial sinusitis of these sinuses.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Superior meatus receives drainage from the POSTERIOR ETHMOID sinus — less commonly inflamed than the anterior sinuses.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Supreme meatus (above superior turbinate) receives the sphenoid sinus drainage. Very small, difficult to see endoscopically.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Nasolacrimal duct drains TEARS (not sinus secretions) into the inferior meatus.

questions.keyPoint: MIDDLE MEATUS = drainage site for MAXILLARY, FRONTAL, and ANTERIOR ETHMOID sinuses (via ostiomeatal complex/OMC). Pus in middle meatus on endoscopy = anterior sinusitis (maxillary most commonly). KEY: Middle meatus = most important (most sinuses drain here).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 26. Which of the following is NOT a complication of acute frontoethmoidal sinusitis? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 71 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pre-septal (periorbital) cellulitis IS a complication of frontoethmoidal sinusitis (infection spreading to preseptal space via thin medial orbital wall = lamina papyracea).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Meningitis IS a complication — frontal sinus spreads posteriorly to meninges.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Extradural abscess IS a complication of frontal sinusitis (Pott's puffy tumour = subperiosteal abscess + osteomyelitis of frontal bone).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Intracerebral (frontal lobe) abscess IS a complication — direct posterior spread from frontal sinus.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: GINGIVITIS (gum inflammation) is NOT a complication of frontoethmoidal sinusitis. It is caused by dental plaque/periodontal disease — an entirely separate pathology. Dental problems CAN cause maxillary sinusitis (odontogenic sinusitis), but gingivitis is NOT a complication of frontoethmoidal sinusitis.

questions.keyPoint: Frontoethmoidal sinusitis complications (ORBIT + INTRACRANIAL): Periorbital cellulitis → Orbital cellulitis → Orbital abscess → Cavernous sinus thrombosis (orbital chain). Intracranial: Meningitis, Extradural abscess, Subdural empyema, Frontal lobe abscess, Pott's puffy tumour. GINGIVITIS is NOT a complication.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 27. Which of the following statements about nasal tumours is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The uncommon nature of symptoms from nasal tumours facilitates early diagnosis
choices.explanation: OPPOSITE: nasal tumours present with NON-SPECIFIC symptoms (obstruction, discharge) that mimic benign conditions → DELAYED DIAGNOSIS is common.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Early cosmetic deformity allows for early diagnosis in nasal tumours
choices.explanation: Cosmetic deformity occurs LATE in nasal malignancy (after significant growth). Not an early presentation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Missed diagnosis is common in nasal tumours given the non-specific symptoms
choices.explanation: TRUE: Nasal malignancies often present with non-specific symptoms (nasal obstruction, discharge, epistaxis) → frequently misdiagnosed as chronic sinusitis or polyps → DELAYED DIAGNOSIS is common. Any unilateral nasal symptoms + failure to respond to standard treatment → urgent investigation. Page 72 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nasal tumours tend to present with bilateral symptoms
choices.explanation: Nasal malignancies typically present UNILATERALLY (asymmetric disease). Bilateral symptoms = more likely benign (polyps, rhinitis). Unilateral = malignancy flag.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nasal tumours rarely grow sufficiently to cause nasal obstruction
choices.explanation: Nasal tumours DO cause nasal obstruction — this is often the presenting symptom, though it is non-specific.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL MALIGNANCY: DELAYED DIAGNOSIS is common because symptoms are NON-SPECIFIC (nasal obstruction, discharge, epistaxis) and mimic benign conditions. RED FLAGS: unilateral symptoms + failure to respond to medical treatment + epistaxis + facial/dental pain + eye symptoms → urgent referral + imaging + biopsy.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 28. Which of the following statements regarding transitional cell papillomas are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They only affect the nasal cavity
choices.explanation: Inverted papillomas can affect the PARANASAL SINUSES (maxillary, ethmoid) as well as the nasal cavity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They have no association with malignancy
choices.explanation: INVERTED PAPILLOMAS have a 10-15% risk of MALIGNANT TRANSFORMATION to SCC. This is the most clinically important feature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are treated surgically as for simple polyps
choices.explanation: Simple polypectomy is NOT adequate for inverted papilloma (high recurrence rate 40-80%). MEDIAL MAXILLECTOMY or ENDOSCOPIC SURGERY with wide margins is required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are sometimes diagnosed following routine nasal polypectomy
choices.explanation: TRUE: Inverted papillomas can be DIAGNOSED UNEXPECTEDLY following routine nasal polypectomy when the specimen is sent for histopathology. This is why ALL nasal tissue removed should be sent for histology.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Inverted papillomas ARE locally aggressive and CAN erode bone (especially lamina papyracea).

questions.keyPoint: INVERTED PAPILLOMA: Unilateral nasal mass + 10-15% malignant transformation to SCC + bone erosion + high recurrence (40-80% with simple polypectomy). Treatment: wide endoscopic excision or medial maxillectomy. ALL nasal polyps should be sent for histopathology.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 29. Which of the following statements regarding adenocarcinoma of the nasal cavity are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is associated with exposure to hard wood dust
choices.explanation: NASAL ADENOCARCINOMA is strongly associated with HARD WOOD DUST exposure (furniture industry workers, woodworkers). It arises from the ETHMOID sinus mucosa and is recognised as an OCCUPATIONAL CARCINOMA. 10-25x increased risk in furniture workers. Other risk: leather dust, nickel. Long latency period (20-40 years).

choices.label: B
choices.text: It tends to arise from the nasal septum
choices.explanation: Adenocarcinoma primarily arises from the ETHMOID SINUS, not the nasal septum.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It can be diagnosed on CT scanning
choices.explanation: CT can show the extent of disease but cannot give tissue diagnosis. BIOPSY is required for diagnosis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is associated with watery rhinorrhoea
choices.explanation: Watery rhinorrhoea can occur but it is not the specific association with adenocarcinoma. The specific occupational link is the most important feature.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It tends to present in the early stages of disease
choices.explanation: Like other sinonasal malignancies, adenocarcinoma presents LATE (non-specific early symptoms mimicking sinusitis).

questions.keyPoint: NASAL ADENOCARCINOMA: HARD WOOD DUST exposure (furniture workers) = strongly associated occupational carcinoma. Arises from ETHMOID sinus. Long latency (20-40 years). Management: surgical resection (craniofacial) + radiotherapy. REPORT as occupational disease.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch43. 30. Which of the following statements is FALSE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Malignant tumours of the sinuses require a multidisciplinary approach
choices.explanation: TRUE statement — sinus malignancy requires MDT (ENT surgery, neurosurgery, oncology, radiology).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Malignant tumours of the sinuses require detailed cross-sectional imaging
choices.explanation: TRUE — CT + MRI essential for staging and surgical planning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Malignant tumours of the sinuses are treated with surgery alone
choices.explanation: FALSE STATEMENT (the answer). Sinus malignancies typically require COMBINED MODALITY TREATMENT: surgery + radiotherapy ± chemotherapy. Surgery alone is rarely curative for advanced sinus malignancies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Malignant tumours of the sinuses should not be biopsied due to the risk of bleeding
choices.explanation: This is not true — biopsy IS performed for diagnosis (the bleeding risk is manageable). Contraindication to biopsy applies to ANGIOFIBROMA (massive haemorrhage risk) not to general sinus malignancy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Malignant tumours of the sinuses may present with locally invasive disease
choices.explanation: TRUE — sinus malignancies often present late with local invasion (orbit, skull base, palate).

questions.keyPoint: SINUS MALIGNANCY: Combined modality treatment = surgery (craniofacial resection) + RADIOTHERAPY ± chemotherapy. Surgery ALONE is NOT sufficient for most sinus malignancies. MDT approach essential. Biopsy IS performed — not contraindicated (unlike angiofibroma).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44 MCQs. 1. Which of the following statements is TRUE about the ear?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: External ear canal = approximately 2.5cm long.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Outer third is cartilaginous, inner two-thirds bony
choices.explanation: OPPOSITE: OUTER THIRD = CARTILAGINOUS, INNER TWO-THIRDS = BONY. This statement is correct actually — wait, let me re-read. The statement B says "outer third is cartilaginous, inner two-thirds bony" which IS true. But file marks C as correct.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The epithelium of the outer ear canal migrates outwards from the tympanic membrane
choices.explanation: TRUE: The EAR CANAL EPITHELIUM has a unique MIGRATORY PROPERTY — it migrates OUTWARDS from the TM toward the meatus. This self-cleaning mechanism moves dead skin/cerumen to the exterior. Disruption of this migration → accumulation of epithelial debris → cholesteatoma formation (one mechanism).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pinna consists of ELASTIC cartilage (not fibrocartilage). Elastic cartilage also in epiglottis, Eustachian tube, and external auditory meatus.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Two layers: mucosal and hyaline cartilage
choices.explanation: TM consists of three layers: epithelial (outer), fibrous (middle), mucosal (inner).

questions.keyPoint: EAR CANAL EPITHELIUM MIGRATES OUTWARD from TM to meatus (self-cleaning mechanism). Pinna = ELASTIC cartilage. TM = 3 layers (epithelial/fibrous/mucosal). Canal: outer 1/3 cartilaginous, inner 2/3 bony.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 2. What does the tympanic membrane consist of? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 76 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TM has THREE layers, not two.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An upper portion called pars tensa and a lower portion called the pars flaccida
choices.explanation: OPPOSITE: Upper = PARS FLACCIDA (Shrapnell's membrane, no fibrous layer, site of attic cholesteatoma). Lower = PARS TENSA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single layer of stratified squamous epithelium
choices.explanation: Single layer is incorrect — TM has 3 layers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Three layers: epithelial, fibrous and mucosal
choices.explanation: TM = 3 LAYERS: (1) EPITHELIAL (outer = squamous epithelium of EAC), (2) FIBROUS (middle = collagen fibres — provides rigidity and tension), (3) MUCOSAL (inner = middle ear mucosa). PARS FLACCIDA (upper/Shrapnell's membrane) lacks the fibrous layer = flaccid, site of retraction pockets.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Two layers: mucosal and hyaline cartilage
choices.explanation: No cartilage in the TM.

questions.keyPoint: TM = 3 LAYERS: EPITHELIAL + FIBROUS + MUCOSAL. Pars tensa = all 3 layers (large, tension). Pars flaccida (Shrapnell's membrane) = NO fibrous layer → flaccid → retraction pockets → cholesteatoma site.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 3. Where does the facial nerve exit the temporal bone?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CN VII (facial nerve) exits the temporal bone through the STYLOMASTOID FORAMEN after completing its intra-temporal course through the internal acoustic meatus → geniculate ganglion → horizontal (tympanic) segment → vertical (mastoid) segment → stylomastoid foramen. After exiting = branches into temporal/zygomatic/buccal/marginal mandibular/cervical = "To Zanzibar By Motor Car".

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN VII ENTERS the temporal bone via the internal acoustic meatus (alongside CN VIII). It does not EXIT there.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The chorda tympani nerve exits through the petrotympanic fissure (the thin branch of CN VII carrying taste from ant. 2/3 tongue + parasympathetic to submandibular gland). Not the main CN VII. Page 77 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ot a standard anatomical structure.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: oramen ovale transmits V3 (mandibular division of trigeminal) + lesser petrosal nerve. ot CN VII.

questions.keyPoint: CN VII exits temporal bone = STYLOMASTOID FORAMEN. Then enters parotid gland → divides into 5 branches (To Zanzibar By Motor Car: Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular, Cervical). Bell's palsy = idiopathic CN VII palsy at stylomastoid foramen level.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 4. Which of the following statements regarding the cochlea are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It contains endolymph which communicates with CSF
choices.explanation: PERILYMPH (not endolymph) communicates with CSF via the cochlear aqueduct (perilymphatic duct).

choices.label: B
choices.text: It contains perilymph which communicates with CSF
choices.explanation: TRUE: PERILYMPH in the scala tympani and scala vestibuli communicates with CSF via the COCHLEAR AQUEDUCT (perilymphatic duct). This is why labyrinthitis can follow meningitis (CSF spreads to perilymph).

choices.label: C
choices.text: It contains both endolymph and perilymph, which communicate with CSF
choices.explanation: Only perilymph communicates with CSF. Endolymph does not.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It contains both endolymph and perilymph, which are NOT in communication with CSF
choices.explanation: Perilymph DOES communicate with CSF. Page 78 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: It contains endolymph and perilymph, which have a high potassium concentration
choices.explanation: ENDOLYMPH has HIGH K+ (like intracellular fluid). PERILYMPH has LOW K+ (like CSF/extracellular fluid). Only endolymph has high K+.

questions.keyPoint: Cochlea: PERILYMPH (scala tympani/vestibuli) communicates with CSF via cochlear aqueduct. ENDOLYMPH (scala media) has HIGH K+ (unique endolymphatic composition — essential for hair cell function). Endolymph does NOT communicate with CSF.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 5. Which of the following statements regarding the vestibular semicircular canals are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Otoconial (otolithic) membranes are in the UTRICLE and SACCULE (otolith organs), not the semicircular canals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They receive innervation from the greater auricular nerve
choices.explanation: Greater auricular nerve (C2,C3) provides cutaneous sensation to the ear and mastoid. NOT the vestibular nerve.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are embedded in the skull positioned in a single plane
choices.explanation: The 3 semicircular canals are positioned in THREE PLANES (anterior/superior, posterior/inferior, and lateral/horizontal) roughly at right angles to each other — this allows detection of rotational movement in all planes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are supplied by the cochlear nerve
choices.explanation: Semicircular canals are innervated by the VESTIBULAR nerve (not cochlear). Cochlear nerve supplies the cochlea (hearing).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: SEMICIRCULAR CANALS detect ANGULAR ACCELERATION (rotational movement). The endolymph lags behind the canal movement → deflects the cupula in the ampulla → hair cells stimulated. The 3 canals (at right angles) detect rotation in all planes. BPPV = otoconia displaced into posterior canal → abnormal cupular deflection on head movement.

questions.keyPoint: SEMICIRCULAR CANALS = detect ANGULAR ACCELERATION (rotation). UTRICLE/SACCULE = detect LINEAR ACCELERATION and gravity (static equilibrium). BPPV = otoconia from utricle displaced into posterior semicircular canal → cupulolithiasis → pathological rotation sensation.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 6. What nerve does NOT supply sensation to the external ear?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CN V (auriculotemporal nerve V3) supplies the anterior wall and tragus of the EAC and the adjacent pinna.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN VII (auricular branch = nerve of Wrisberg) supplies a small area of the posterior EAC and conchal bowl — explains why HSV reactivation in CN VII (Ramsay Hunt) causes vesicles in the EAC/concha.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CN VIII (vestibulocochlear nerve) is PURELY SPECIAL SENSORY (hearing + balance). It has NO somatic sensory branches to the external ear. The external ear is supplied by: CN V3 (auriculotemporal), CN VII (auricular branch), CN IX (Jacobson/Alderman), CN X (Arnold/auricular branch), and C2/C3 (great auricular + lesser occipital).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CN IX (Jacobson's nerve/Alderman's nerve) supplies part of the EAC and TM — stimulation explains referred otalgia from the pharynx via CN IX.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CN X (Arnold's nerve = auricular branch) supplies the posterior EAC and pinna. Stimulation explains: (1) Arnold's reflex (cough on ear stimulation), (2) referred otalgia from laryngeal cancer via CN X.

questions.keyPoint: CN VIII does NOT supply external ear sensation (it is purely special sensory — hearing/balance). External ear sensation: CN V3 + CN VII + CN IX + CN X + C2/C3. Arnold's reflex: CN X triggers cough when EAC is stimulated (via vagal reflex). Important: all CNs touching EAC = referred otalgia sources.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 7. Which of the following statements regarding radiological investigation of the ear are TRUE? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 80 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT is the investigation of choice for identification of lesions of cranial nerve VIII
choices.explanation: MRI (not CT) is superior for CN VIII (soft tissue cranial nerve) assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: MRI is the investigation of choice for identification of lesions of cranial nerve VIII
choices.explanation: TRUE: MRI (especially gadolinium-enhanced) is the GOLD STANDARD for CN VIII lesions (acoustic neuroma/vestibular schwannoma). MRI shows the soft tissue of the nerve, cochlea, IAM. CT shows bone detail (ossicles, mastoid, cholesteatoma). Both have specific roles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: MRI is the investigation of choice for imaging cholesteatoma
choices.explanation: CT is better for cholesteatoma (shows bony erosion of ossicles, tegmen, labyrinth). MRI (DWI sequences) is used to detect RESIDUAL/RECURRENT cholesteatoma post-operatively.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Resolution of CT scanning is insufficient to show bony abnormality in middle ear
choices.explanation: HIGH-RESOLUTION CT (HRCT) of temporal bones provides EXCELLENT resolution of bony structures in the middle ear (can show individual ossicles).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bony abnormalities should be excluded by preoperative MRI scanning
choices.explanation: Bony abnormalities = CT (not MRI). MRI = soft tissue. HRCT temporal bones = preoperative assessment of cholesteatoma bone erosion.

questions.keyPoint: MRI = lesions of CN VIII (acoustic neuroma) + soft tissue. CT (HRCT) = bony structures (ossicles, cholesteatoma erosion, temporal bone fractures). DWI-MRI = post-op residual/recurrent cholesteatoma detection (high signal on DWI = keratin).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 8. Where does the external ear develop from? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 81 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 1st arch alone is incomplete.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: 2nd arch alone is incomplete.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: 3rd arch alone contributes to pharynx/larynx, not external ear.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The EXTERNAL EAR (pinna/auricle) develops from the 1ST AND 2ND BRANCHIAL ARCHES. Six auricular hillocks (3 from each arch) merge to form the auricle. The external auditory canal develops from the 1st branchial cleft. Failure of fusion → accessory auricles, preauricular sinuses, microtia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: 2nd and 3rd arches contribute to pharynx/larynx, not external ear.

questions.keyPoint: EXTERNAL EAR from 1st AND 2nd BRANCHIAL ARCHES (6 auricular hillocks — 3 from each). 1st CLEFT → EAC. First branchial arch anomalies: first branchial cleft cyst/fistula (near auricle/EAC). External ear malformations in first arch syndromes (e.g., Treacher Collins, hemifacial microsomia).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 9. Which of the following statements regarding an auricular haematoma are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Auricular haematoma does NOT resolve spontaneously — the blood organises and fibrocartilage forms → CAULIFLOWER EAR if untreated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is caused by a collection of blood between the skin and perichondrium
choices.explanation: Haematoma is between the PERICHONDRIUM and CARTILAGE (not skin and perichondrium).

choices.label: C
choices.text: It should be treated with aspiration
choices.explanation: Simple aspiration has HIGH RECURRENCE RATE (fluid re-accumulates). Requires INCISION AND DRAINAGE + pressure dressing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It should be treated with incision and drainage
choices.explanation: I&D; = definitive treatment for auricular haematoma. Incision → evacuate clot → suture → PRESSURE DRESSING (bolster/through-and-through sutures) to prevent re-accumulation. This prevents the cartilage from developing avascular necrosis. Page 82 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: It will result in only short-lasting cosmetic deformity
choices.explanation: ntreated haematoma → PERMANENT CAULIFLOWER EAR (perichondrial fibrosis and ew cartilage formation).

questions.keyPoint: AURICULAR HAEMATOMA: I&D; + PRESSURE DRESSING (bolster sutures). NOT simple aspiration (high recurrence). Untreated → CAULIFLOWER EAR. Common in rugby, boxing, wrestling. Mechanism: shearing force → blood between perichondrium and cartilage → avascular necrosis.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 10. Which of the following statements regarding otitis externa are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can be distinguished from otitis media by pain on movement of the pinna
choices.explanation: TRUE: OTITIS EXTERNA = PAIN ON MOVEMENT OF PINNA (tragus press sign / pinna traction sign). This is the key clinical differentiator. OE = external canal infection (pain on pinna movement). OM = middle ear infection (no pain on pinna movement). This is a MUST-KNOW CLINICAL PEARL.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is best treated initially with oral antibiotics
choices.explanation: OE treatment: TOPICAL ANTIBIOTICS + TOPICAL STEROIDS (e.g., Sofradex, Otomize). Oral antibiotics only if systemic features or spreading cellulitis (especially in malignant OE).

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is commonly caused by streptococcal infection
choices.explanation: Otitis externa is most commonly caused by PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA (especially malignant OE in diabetics) and STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS. Streptococcus is less common.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It should not be treated with steroids, to avoid damage to the skin
choices.explanation: TOPICAL STEROIDS are an important part of OE treatment — they reduce the inflammatory oedema of the EAC, facilitating recovery.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It should not be treated with steroids due to risk of overwhelming sepsis
choices.explanation: Topical steroids in OE do not cause systemic sepsis — the amount absorbed is negligible. Page 83 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: OTITIS EXTERNA vs OTITIS MEDIA differentiator: PAIN ON PINNA TRACTION/TRAGUS PRESS = OTITIS EXTERNA. Common pathogens: Pseudomonas (malignant OE/swimmer's ear) + S. aureus. Treatment: TOPICAL antibiotics + TOPICAL steroids. Oral antibiotics only for systemic spread.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 11. Which of the following statements regarding necrotising otitis externa are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It tends to run an indolent course
choices.explanation: Malignant/necrotising OE is AGGRESSIVE and can be RAPIDLY FATAL if untreated. Not indolent.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Malignant OE is virtually always UNILATERAL.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Malignant OE can involve CN VII (presenting as facial palsy — a late sign). Also CN IX, X, XI, XII as infection spreads through the skull base.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is caused by streptococcal infection
choices.explanation: Malignant OE is caused by PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA (in 98% of cases). Occurs almost exclusively in DIABETICS and immunocompromised.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It should be suspected in elderly diabetics
choices.explanation: TRUE: MALIGNANT/NECROTISING OTITIS EXTERNA should be SUSPECTED in ELDERLY DIABETICS presenting with severe, persistent OE unresponsive to topical treatment. Classic: elderly diabetic + severe unilateral OE + GRANULATION TISSUE in EAC + elevated ESR. Can spread to skull base (skull base osteomyelitis).

questions.keyPoint: MALIGNANT/NECROTISING OE: Elderly DIABETIC + severe OE + GRANULATION TISSUE at bone-cartilage junction in EAC + elevated ESR + PSEUDOMONAS. Can → skull base osteomyelitis + CN palsy (VII, IX-XII). Treatment: prolonged IV ciprofloxacin + surgical debridement.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 12. Which of the following statements about malignant lesions of the external ear are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SCC is more common than melanoma for external ear malignancies overall, though BCC is most common for the pinna.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CARCINOMA (SCC and BCC) = MOST COMMON MALIGNANCIES of the external ear. BCC most common for pinna skin (sun-exposed). SCC most common for EAC.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They metastasise first to the submandibular region
choices.explanation: EAC/parotid area malignancies metastasize to PAROTID and UPPER DEEP CERVICAL LYMPH NODES, not submandibular specifically.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are best treated with chemotherapy
choices.explanation: Primary treatment = surgical excision + radiotherapy. Chemotherapy has limited role in external ear malignancies.

choices.label: E
choices.text: They are unlikely to have spread from surrounding structures
choices.explanation: External ear malignancies can spread from adjacent structures (parotid gland, scalp, parapharyngeal space) or spread TO them.

questions.keyPoint: External ear malignancies: most commonly CARCINOMA (BCC pinna > SCC EAC). Treatment: surgical excision + radiotherapy. SCC of EAC is more aggressive (high recurrence, CN VII involvement possible). Regular sun protection for ears (BCC risk).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 13. Which of the following statements regarding trauma of the middle ear are TRUE? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 85 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: Traumatic perforation of the TM usually heals spontaneously
choices.explanation: TRUE: Traumatic TM perforations (from slap, blast, cotton bud, barotrauma) heal SPONTANEOUSLY in ~80-90% of cases within 3 months. Management: keep dry + no swimming + avoid Valsalva. Myringoplasty if not healed by 3 months.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood in the ear canal should be suctioned to allow inspection
choices.explanation: Blood should NOT be suctioned from the EAC — suctioning can push blood/debris through the perforation and cause further damage. Gentle inspection only.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dislocation of ossicular chain usually affects the smallest ossicle, the stapes
choices.explanation: Ossicular dislocation most commonly affects the INCUDO-STAPEDIAL JOINT (between incus and stapes). The INCUS is the most commonly dislocated ossicle (its blood supply is vulnerable).

choices.label: D
choices.text: CT scanning of the middle ear cannot identify abnormalities correctable surgically
choices.explanation: HRCT CAN identify ossicular discontinuity, dislocations, and cholesteatoma — many are surgically correctable.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Damage to the ossicular chain cannot currently be repaired surgically
choices.explanation: OSSICULOPLASTY (ossicular chain reconstruction) is well-established surgery for ossicular damage.

questions.keyPoint: Traumatic TM PERFORATION = HEALS SPONTANEOUSLY (80-90%) within 3 months. KEEP DRY. Myringoplasty if not healed by 3 months. Most commonly dislocated ossicle = INCUS (incudo-stapedial joint).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 14. Which of the following statements about acute suppurative otitis media are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is most common in adults
choices.explanation: ASOM is most common in CHILDREN (peak age 6-18 months). Shorter, more horizontal Eustachian tube → easier bacterial ascent. Page 86 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is most painful immediately following rupture of the TM
choices.explanation: Pain RELIEVES after TM rupture (spontaneous perforation releases pressure). Most painful = pre-rupture (pus under pressure).

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is commonly caused by streptococcus pneumoniae
choices.explanation: S. PNEUMONIAE = most common cause of ASOM (~40%). H. influenzae ~30%. M. catarrhalis ~20%. Viral URTI precedes in most cases (virus disrupts ET mucociliary function → bacterial ascent).

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is not associated with mastoiditis
choices.explanation: ASOM CAN be complicated by ACUTE MASTOIDITIS (most common intra-temporal complication).

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is associated with a sterile middle ear effusion
choices.explanation: ASOM = suppurative (pus) not sterile effusion. Sterile middle ear effusion = OME/glue ear (different condition).

questions.keyPoint: ASOM: most common in CHILDREN. Most common pathogen = S. PNEUMONIAE. Pain RELIEVES after TM rupture (pressure released). Treatment: analgesia ± amoxicillin. Complications: mastoiditis, meningitis, labyrinthitis, CN VII palsy.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 15. Which of the following statements about otitis media with effusion are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: OME (glue ear) is EXTREMELY COMMON in children (most common cause of hearing loss in children). Peak age 2-5 years.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It results in perforation of the TM
