choices.explanation: OME has an INTACT but DULL/AMBER TM (with loss of light reflex and air-fluid level sometimes visible). No perforation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It causes systemic upset in the child
choices.explanation: OME is usually PAINLESS with NO systemic upset (unlike ASOM which causes fever and pain).

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is due to Eustachian tube dysfunction
choices.explanation: TRUE: OME (GLUE EAR) is caused by EUSTACHIAN TUBE DYSFUNCTION → negative middle ear pressure → serous/mucous effusion accumulates. ET dysfunction: adenoid hypertrophy (most common in children), allergy, cleft palate (most severe ET dysfunction). Causes conductive HL. Page 87 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: OME is characteristically PAINLESS. Otalgia suggests ASOM.

questions.keyPoint: OME (GLUE EAR): Eustachian tube dysfunction → middle ear negative pressure → effusion. INTACT dull/amber TM. PAINLESS. Most common cause of hearing loss in CHILDREN. Treatment: watchful waiting (50% resolve in 6-8 weeks) → grommets (if persists >3 months and bilateral HL).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 16. Which of the following statements about treatment of otitis media with effusion in children are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urgent treatment once diagnosed improves outcome
choices.explanation: OME is not a surgical emergency. 50% resolve spontaneously within 3 months — watchful waiting is the first-line approach.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medical treatment of OME has been proven effective
choices.explanation: ANTIBIOTICS, antihistamines, and nasal steroids have NOT been proven to definitively treat OME in clinical trials.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Grommet insertion requires later grommet removal in most children
choices.explanation: Most grommets EXTRUDE SPONTANEOUSLY within 6-12 months (no removal required in most cases).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Following grommet insertion, children should not swim for around 1 year
choices.explanation: Swimming RESTRICTIONS after grommet insertion are DEBATED. Most evidence suggests SURFACE SWIMMING is acceptable. Diving/submersion is avoided. Page 88 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: A watch-and-wait policy results in improvement in 50 per cent over 6 weeks
choices.explanation: RUE: WATCHFUL WAITING leads to spontaneous resolution in ~50% of OME within 6-8 eeks. Therefore, initial management = watchful waiting (3 months) before considering rommets.

questions.keyPoint: OME MANAGEMENT: WATCHFUL WAITING first (50% resolve in 6-8 weeks). Grommets after 3 months if HL persists (bilateral ≥25-30 dB). Grommets EXTRUDE SPONTANEOUSLY in most cases (no planned removal). Hearing aids alternative to grommets. Adenoidectomy if adenoids contributing.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 17. Which of the following statements regarding middle ear effusion in adults are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Middle ear effusion is LESS COMMON in adults than in children (Eustachian tube is longer and more angled in adults).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Adult OME does not always persist — may resolve spontaneously though often slower than in children.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If persistent, it should be treated medically
choices.explanation: Persistent adult OME unresponsive to medical measures requires grommets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It may be the presenting feature of nasopharyngeal carcinoma
choices.explanation: UNILATERAL MIDDLE EAR EFFUSION IN AN ADULT = EXCLUDE NPC. NPC can obstruct the Eustachian tube orifice in the nasopharynx (fossa of Rosenmüller) → unilateral serous OME. Any adult with unilateral OME needs nasopharyngoscopy to EXCLUDE NPC.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Adult OME does not respond to adenoidectomy (adenoids typically atrophied in adults). Grommets ± nasopharyngoscopy (exclude NPC) is the approach.

questions.keyPoint: ADULT UNILATERAL OME = EXCLUDE NPC. NPC blocks ET orifice in nasopharynx → conductive HL. ALWAYS perform nasopharyngoscopy in adult with unilateral OME. NPC: EBV-associated, South-East Asian, may also present with epistaxis + cranial nerve involvement.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 18. What are the typical presenting symptoms associated with chronic otitis media?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TYPICAL PRESENTING SYMPTOMS of CSOM: (1) OTORRHOEA (persistent or recurrent ear discharge), (2) HEARING LOSS (conductive in mucosal type; can be mixed/severe in squamous type with ossicular erosion). OTALGIA is usually ABSENT (acute pain suggests complication). These two symptoms define the presentation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pain is unusual in CSOM unless complication develops. The typical symptoms are discharge + HL.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Dizziness suggests labyrinthine involvement (complication). Not typical presenting symptoms.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Not the typical presenting complaint. Tinnitus can occur but is not a defining feature.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pain is not typical of CSOM (suggests acute exacerbation or complication).

questions.keyPoint: CSOM = OTORRHOEA (persistent discharge) + HEARING LOSS (conductive). OTALGIA usually ABSENT (its presence suggests complication). The chronicity (>3 months) + recurrence + absence of pain are the defining features of CSOM.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 19. What is cholesteatoma? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 90 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: A benign condition which is self-limiting
choices.explanation: Cholesteatoma is NOT self-limiting — it is progressive and locally destructive.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A benign condition which can be locally destructive
choices.explanation: CHOLESTEATOMA = A BENIGN (non-malignant histologically) condition. It is an accumulation of KERATINISING SQUAMOUS EPITHELIUM in the middle ear/mastoid. Despite being benign histologically, it is LOCALLY DESTRUCTIVE — it erodes bone through a combination of enzymatic action (collagenase, proteases from matrix) + pressure necrosis → ossicles, facial nerve, labyrinth, tegmen, sigmoid sinus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A malignant ear condition without metastasis
choices.explanation: Cholesteatoma is BENIGN histologically. Despite being locally destructive (which can mislead), it does not metastasise.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Not malignant and does not metastasise.

choices.label: E
choices.text: An infection more common in immunocomp romised patients
choices.explanation: Cholesteatoma is not an infection per se — it is a squamous epithelial growth. It becomes infected secondarily (causing offensive discharge).

questions.keyPoint: CHOLESTEATOMA = BENIGN (histologically) but LOCALLY DESTRUCTIVE squamous epithelial accumulation in middle ear/mastoid. Erodes bone via enzymes + pressure. Destroys: ossicles (conductive HL), facial nerve (CN VII palsy), labyrinth (SNHL, vertigo), tegmen (meningitis), sigmoid sinus (lateral sinus thrombosis).

questions.topic: NOT a complication of chronic OM
questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 20. Which of the following is NOT a recognised complication of chronic otitis media?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Meningitis IS a recognised intracranial complication of CSOM.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Brain abscess IS an intracranial complication of CSOM.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PERIORBITAL CELLULITIS is a complication of SINUSITIS (frontoethmoidal sinusitis spreading to the periorbital space). It is NOT a complication of CSOM/chronic otitis media.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Extradural abscess IS an intracranial complication of CSOM. Page 91 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Subdural empyema IS a complication of CSOM (rare but serious).

questions.keyPoint: PERIORBITAL CELLULITIS = complication of SINUSITIS (not CSOM). CSOM complications: mastoiditis, labyrinthitis, petrositis, CN VII palsy, meningitis, brain abscess, lateral sinus thrombosis, extradural abscess. The orbit is adjacent to the ethmoid sinuses (not to the mastoid/middle ear directly).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 21. Which of the following statements regarding otosclerosis are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An abnormal ear drum suggests the diagnosis
choices.explanation: In otosclerosis, the TYMPANIC MEMBRANE IS NORMAL (abnormality is within the bony labyrinth/stapes footplate, not the TM). Schwartze sign: flamingo pink blush through TM from active foci.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Otosclerosis is more common in WOMEN (female:male ratio 2:1). Also worsens during PREGNANCY (oestrogen effect on bone).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The malleus is the ossicle most commonly affected
choices.explanation: The STAPES FOOTPLATE is most commonly affected (fixation at annular ligament → conductive HL). Malleus and incus involvement is rare.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical treatment is unlikely to be effective
choices.explanation: STAPEDECTOMY (or stapedotomy) is highly effective — 90% success rate for improving hearing.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TRUE: Otosclerosis is BILATERAL in 70-80% of cases (though one side usually more severely affected). AD inheritance (25-40% penetrance). Bilateral progressive conductive hearing loss in young adults (20-30s).

questions.keyPoint: OTOSCLEROSIS: Bilateral (70-80%) + females > males + young adults (20-30s) + AD inheritance + STAPES footplate fixation → CONDUCTIVE HL + Carhart notch at 2kHz on BC. Treatment: hearing aid or STAPEDECTOMY (highly effective, 90%). Worsens in pregnancy.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 22. Which of the following statements about middle ear tumours are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The most common middle ear tumour is squamous cell carcinoma
choices.explanation: GLOMUS TUMOUR (paraganglioma) is the MOST COMMON MIDDLE EAR TUMOUR.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The most common middle ear tumour is a glomus tumour
choices.explanation: TRUE: GLOMUS TUMOUR (glomus jugulare/tympanicum = paraganglioma) is the MOST COMMON MIDDLE EAR TUMOUR. Presents with PULSATILE TINNITUS + conductive HL. Red pulsating mass behind an intact TM. Highly vascular. Biopsy can cause haemorrhage. Diagnosis: MRI (flow voids).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vascular middle ear tumours tend to present with a constant tinnitus
choices.explanation: Glomus tumours cause PULSATILE (not constant) tinnitus — the patient hears their own heartbeat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Squamous cell carcinoma of middle ear tends to spare the facial nerve
choices.explanation: SCC of middle ear is locally destructive and often involves CN VII.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Because of bony anatomy, palsies of CNs in the jugular foramen do not occur with middle ear tumours
choices.explanation: Glomus jugulare tumours CAN cause jugular foramen CN palsies (CN IX, X, XI = jugular foramen syndrome).

questions.keyPoint: GLOMUS TUMOUR (paraganglioma) = most common middle ear tumour. Pulsatile tinnitus + red pulsating mass behind intact TM + conductive HL. Highly vascular — DO NOT BIOPSY (haemorrhage). Diagnosis: MRI + angiography. Treatment: surgery ± radiotherapy.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 23. Which of the following statements regarding congenital anomalies of the inner ear are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Children cannot be formally assessed with hearing tests, so treatment should be based on clinical suspicion
choices.explanation: ABR and OAE testing CAN formally assess hearing in infants — they do not require cooperation. Newborn screening uses OAE.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Children with profound hearing loss should not be offered hearing aids due to social stigma
choices.explanation: Hearing aids and cochlear implants should be offered. Social stigma is NOT a reason to withhold treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: In correctly selected cases, cochlear implantation offers significant benefits
choices.explanation: TRUE: COCHLEAR IMPLANTATION offers significant benefits in correctly selected children with congenital profound SNHL. Selection criteria: severe-profound bilateral SNHL + failed HA trial + appropriate cochlear anatomy (CT/MRI) + family motivation + no medical contraindications. Best outcomes when implanted before age 2 (before critical language period ends).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Most cases of profound SNHL are due to maldevelopment of the cochlear nerve
choices.explanation: Most congenital profound SNHL = COCHLEAR HAIR CELL APLASIA/MALDEVELOPMENT (not cochlear nerve maldevelopment). Most have intact cochlear nerves (essential for CI candidacy).

choices.label: E
choices.text: A child with congenital hearing loss will also have an abnormal middle ear
choices.explanation: Congenital SNHL is predominantly an INNER EAR problem (cochlea/hair cells). The middle ear is usually NORMAL.

questions.keyPoint: COCHLEAR IMPLANTATION: Benefits congenitally deaf children if: bilateral profound SNHL + failed HA + intact CN VIII + appropriate anatomy + early intervention (before age 2 for best language outcomes). Most congenital SNHL = cochlear hair cell problem (inner ear). Middle ear usually NORMAL.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 24. Which of the following statements about presbycusis are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A condition which tends to affect adolescents
choices.explanation: Presbycusis = AGE-RELATED hearing loss. Affects ELDERLY, not adolescents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A condition which tends to affect the elderly
choices.explanation: TRUE: PRESBYCUSIS = age-related progressive bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Most common cause of hearing loss in the elderly (>60 years). Due to progressive degeneration of cochlear hair cells (especially at the base = high-frequency loss first). Audiogram: bilateral symmetric DOWNSLOPING high-frequency SNHL (no air-bone gap).

choices.label: C
choices.text: A condition which results in a low-frequency hearing loss
choices.explanation: Presbycusis causes HIGH-FREQUENCY hearing loss (basal turn of cochlea, which processes high frequencies, is affected first by aging).

choices.label: D
choices.text: A condition caused by loss of cochlear hair cells at the apex
choices.explanation: HIGH-frequency hair cells at the BASE are affected first. APEX processes LOW frequencies.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A condition which is unlikely to be helped by hearing aids
choices.explanation: Hearing aids ARE beneficial for presbycusis — amplifying sound, especially high frequencies. Modern digital HAs with frequency shaping are very effective.

questions.keyPoint: PRESBYCUSIS = age-related SNHL (bilateral symmetric high-frequency). Most common HL in elderly. Basal cochlear hair cells affected first (process high frequencies). Audiogram: bilateral symmetric DOWNSLOPING (high-freq) SNHL. Treatment: hearing aids (effective). Social isolation, depression risk if untreated.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 25. Following temporal bone fracture, which of the following are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is important to assess and document facial nerve function
choices.explanation: TRUE: CN VII ASSESSMENT is essential after temporal bone fracture. The facial nerve runs through the Fallopian canal in the temporal bone. Immediate CN VII palsy = suggests severed nerve (surgical exploration). Delayed palsy = neuropraxia/oedema (conservative, steroid, good prognosis). Document the timing and severity carefully.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hearing loss IS COMMON after temporal bone fracture: transverse fractures → severe SNHL + vertigo; longitudinal fractures → conductive HL (ossicular damage/TM perforation).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sensorineural hearing loss will tend to recover given time
choices.explanation: SNHL from temporal bone fracture (cochlear/labyrinthine injury) usually DOES NOT RECOVER fully. Conductive HL from ossicular dislocation may be surgically correctable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plain X-rays allow preoperative assessment of temporal bone anatomy
choices.explanation: CT (HRCT) is needed for preoperative assessment. Plain X-rays of temporal bones have very poor resolution.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Examination of the ear canal will add nothing to the examination
choices.explanation: EAC examination may reveal: blood, TM perforation, CSF, or avulsion of the canal skin — all important findings post-temporal bone fracture.

questions.keyPoint: After TEMPORAL BONE FRACTURE: (1) ASSESS CN VII IMMEDIATELY (immediate palsy = surgical emergency; delayed = conservative). (2) Types: LONGITUDINAL (80%, along EAC axis, conductive HL, TM perforated) vs TRANSVERSE (rare, across EAC, severe SNHL, vertigo, CN VII palsy). (3) HRCT for imaging.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 26. Which of the following statements regarding ototoxic drugs is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aminoglycoside- containing ear drops must be avoided in perforated TM
choices.explanation: This is NOT absolutely true — TOPICAL aminoglycosides (gentamicin ear drops) in perforated ears carry risk of cochlear toxicity though the actual incidence is debated. However, UK guidelines state they should be used with CAUTION, not absolutely avoided, as the benefit (treating CSOM infection) may outweigh the risk in controlled settings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ototoxic drugs selectively impair cochlear over vestibular function
choices.explanation: Different aminoglycosides have different cochlear vs vestibular toxicity: GENTAMICIN = predominantly VESTIBULOTOXIC (used intentionally for vestibular ablation in Meniere's). TOBRAMYCIN/AMIKACIN = cochleotoxic (cause permanent SNHL). NOT a general rule.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ototoxicity can manifest DURING or AFTER treatment. Cisplatin causes progressive HL that may worsen even after stopping treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Renal function may be an important factor in ototoxicity
choices.explanation: TRUE: RENAL IMPAIRMENT increases the risk of ototoxicity from aminoglycosides because they are renally excreted. Renal failure → drug accumulation → higher blood levels → greater cochlear/vestibular toxicity. MONITOR renal function AND DRUG LEVELS during aminoglycoside therapy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loop diuretics should be used when risk of ototoxicity is high
choices.explanation: Loop diuretics (furosemide) ARE THEMSELVES OTOTOXIC (cause SNHL, usually reversible). They should be AVOIDED or used with CAUTION in combination with aminoglycosides (synergistic ototoxicity).

questions.keyPoint: RENAL FUNCTION + OTOTOXICITY: Aminoglycosides are renally excreted. Renal failure → accumulation → higher levels → ototoxicity. Monitor: renal function + drug trough/peak levels. Ototoxic drugs (GAACQV): Gentamicin, Aspirin, Amphotericin, Cisplatin, Quinine, Vancomycin + LOOP DIURETICS.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 27. Which of the following statements regarding BPPV are TRUE? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 97 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BPPV episodes last SECONDS TO MINUTES (<1 minute per episode). Hours = Meniere's or vestibular neuritis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is associated with intermittent tinnitus
choices.explanation: BPPV does NOT cause tinnitus. Tinnitus + vertigo = Meniere's disease.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It can only be diagnosed in the absence of recent head injury
choices.explanation: BPPV CAN follow head injury (traumatic BPPV) — trauma displaces otoconia. Dix-Hallpike confirms regardless of history.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It causes vertigo in the absence of nystagmus
choices.explanation: BPPV causes characteristic ROTATORY NYSTAGMUS on Dix-Hallpike (delayed, geotropic, fatigable). Nystagmus IS present.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It may be helped by a repositioning manoeuvre
choices.explanation: TRUE: BPPV = treated with CANALITH REPOSITIONING MANOEUVRE (Epley's = 80-90% success rate). The manoeuvre repositions displaced otoconia from the posterior semicircular canal back into the utricle, resolving the condition.

questions.keyPoint: BPPV: Brief (<1 min) positional vertigo + fatigable rotatory nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike. NO hearing loss, NO tinnitus. Treated with Epley's manoeuvre (80-90% success). Can be traumatic (head injury). KEY DIFFERENTIATOR from Meniere's: no HL/tinnitus, <1 min, positional, Dix-Hallpike positive.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 28. Which of the following statements regarding the vertigo associated with Ménière's disease are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It lasts for days to weeks
choices.explanation: Meniere's vertigo lasts MINUTES TO HOURS (20 minutes to 12 hours typically). NOT days to weeks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It lasts for minutes to hours
choices.explanation: TRUE: MENIERE'S VERTIGO = lasts 20 MINUTES TO 12 HOURS (per attack). This is a defining diagnostic criterion. Distinguished from: BPPV (<1 minute) and vestibular neuritis (days).

choices.label: C
choices.text: It lasts for seconds to minutes
choices.explanation: Seconds to minutes = BPPV. Meniere's = minutes to hours. Page 98 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Meniere's vertigo occurs WITH the tetrad: SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. Not isolated.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is caused by recurrent infection
choices.explanation: Meniere's is caused by ENDOLYMPHATIC HYDROPS (excess endolymph). Viral infection may be an aetiological trigger in some cases but recurrent infection is not the mechanism.

questions.keyPoint: MENIERE'S VERTIGO DURATION = 20 MINUTES TO 12 HOURS. This is a key diagnostic criterion. Distinction: BPPV = seconds to <1 min; Meniere's = 20min to 12h; Vestibular neuritis = days to weeks. With the full tetrad (SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness = diagnostic).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 29. In patients presenting with facial palsy, which of the following should be done?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Examination should include the ear, the parotid and the cranial nerves
choices.explanation: TRUE: Any patient with facial palsy (CN VII) must have a comprehensive examination including: (1) EAR (vesicles in EAC/concha = Ramsay Hunt syndrome; TM for CSOM cholesteatoma), (2) PAROTID (tumour = parotid malignancy compressing CN VII), (3) CRANIAL NERVES (other CN involvement → stroke, cerebellopontine angle lesion). This helps distinguish Bell's palsy from secondary causes.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Antibiotics for Bell's palsy are controversial and NOT standard first-line. The main treatment is PREDNISOLONE (within 72h, improves recovery) ± antivirals (if Ramsay Hunt suspected).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bell's palsy should be diagnosed if the ear is discharging
choices.explanation: DISCHARGING EAR + facial palsy = CSOM complication (cholesteatoma eroding facial nerve canal) — NOT Bell's palsy. Bell's palsy = idiopathic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients should be warned that recovery is unlikely
choices.explanation: Bell's palsy has good prognosis: >80% recover completely with steroid treatment. Only ~5% have severe permanent deficit. Page 99 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: During treatment, patients should be advised to keep the affected eye open to prevent deterioration
choices.explanation: KEEP EYE CLOSED (or lubricated/patched). Lagophthalmos (inability to close eye) → corneal exposure → ulceration. Treatment: lubricating drops + lid tape at night + eye patch.

questions.keyPoint: FACIAL PALSY EXAMINATION: Ear (Ramsay Hunt vesicles, CSOM) + Parotid (tumour) + Cranial nerves (cerebellopontine angle lesion). Bell's palsy = idiopathic (diagnosis of exclusion). Treatment: prednisolone 50mg for 10 days within 72h. Eye protection (lubricants + patch).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch44. 30. Which of the following regarding the management of acoustic neuroma are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hearing will be lost during treatment for this condition
choices.explanation: Hearing may be PRESERVED in small tumours with conservative management (watchful waiting) or radiosurgery (Gamma Knife). Not always lost.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Predictable tumour growth allows for appropriately timed treatment
choices.explanation: Acoustic neuroma growth is UNPREDICTABLE — some grow rapidly, some remain stable for years, some regress. This unpredictability makes management complex.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Treatment options are complex and involve factors including tumour size and hearing levels
choices.explanation: TRUE: ACOUSTIC NEUROMA (vestibular schwannoma) management depends on multiple factors: TUMOUR SIZE, PATIENT AGE/FITNESS, HEARING LEVELS, CONTRALATERAL HEARING, TINNITUS, and PATIENT PREFERENCE. Options: (1) Watchful waiting (small tumours), (2) Gamma Knife/stereotactic radiosurgery, (3) Microsurgical removal (translabyrinthine, retrosigmoid, or middle fossa approach). MDT decision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: CT scanning is the investigation of choic
choices.explanation: MRI (gadolinium-enhanced) is the GOLD STANDARD for acoustic neuroma detection and assessment, not CT. Page 100 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: any acoustic neuromas are managed with WATCHFUL WAITING (especially small umours in elderly patients). Treatment is not always mandatory.

questions.keyPoint: ACOUSTIC NEUROMA: MRI (Gd-enhanced) = gold standard for diagnosis. Management = complex MDT decision (size + hearing + age + preference): (1) Watchful waiting (small/stable), (2) Gamma Knife radiosurgery, (3) Microsurgery. Presents with: UNILATERAL SNHL + tinnitus + balance problems ± CN V/VII involvement in large tumours.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 1. The pharynx extends from the level of the skull base to which vertebra?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: C4 = level of hyoid bone.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: C5 = laryngeal level.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The pharynx extends from the skull base to C6 (lower border of the cricoid cartilage), where it becomes the oesophagus. The cricopharyngeus (upper oesophageal sphincter) is at C6.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: C7 = root of neck level.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: T1 = thoracic inlet.

questions.keyPoint: PHARYNX: Skull base → C6 (cricoid level). Becomes oesophagus at C6. CRICOPHARYNGEUS = upper oesophageal sphincter at C6. Pharyngeal pouch (Zenker's diverticulum) forms JUST ABOVE cricopharyngeus (Killian's dehiscence).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 2. Infection of the parapharyngeal space places the patient at risk of:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Not a direct risk of parapharyngeal infection.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Tonsillitis may CAUSE parapharyngeal infection, not the reverse.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Dental infection may cause parapharyngeal infection, not result from it.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PARAPHARYNGEAL SPACE INFECTION → DESCENDING NECROTISING MEDIASTINITIS via the retropharyngeal space → visceral compartment → mediastinum. Life-threatening emergency. Also risk: jugular vein thrombosis (Lemierre syndrome), carotid artery erosion.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Epiglottitis = supraglottic infection. Not a complication of parapharyngeal infection.

questions.keyPoint: PARAPHARYNGEAL INFECTION → MEDIASTINITIS (most feared complication via descending necrotising mediastinitis). Also: Lemierre syndrome (IJV thrombosis + septic emboli), carotid erosion, Horner's syndrome. Treatment: IV antibiotics + surgical drainage + may need thoracic surgery.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 3. The superior and recurrent laryngeal nerves are branches of which cranial nerve?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CN VIII = vestibulocochlear nerve (hearing + balance).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN IX = glossopharyngeal (taste post. 1/3 tongue, pharyngeal sensation, carotid body, parotid gland).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Both SUPERIOR LARYNGEAL NERVE and RECURRENT LARYNGEAL NERVE are branches of the VAGUS NERVE (CN X). SLN: internal branch (sensation above vocal cords) + external branch (motor to cricothyroid). RLN: motor to all intrinsic laryngeal muscles (except cricothyroid) + sensation below vocal cords. Page 102 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CN XI = accessory nerve (SCM + trapezius).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CN XII = hypoglossal (tongue muscles).

questions.keyPoint: CN X (VAGUS) gives: SLN (Superior Laryngeal Nerve) → cricothyroid + sensation above cords. RLN (Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve) → all intrinsic laryngeal muscles (except cricothyroid) + sensation below cords. RLN palsy = hoarse voice. Bilateral RLN palsy = stridor/airway emergency.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 4. Which of the following is NOT a function of the larynx?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preparation of food bolus for swallowing
choices.explanation: The LARYNX does NOT prepare food for swallowing — that is the role of the TONGUE, TEETH, and ORAL CAVITY (mastication). The larynx PROTECTS the airway during swallowing (by the epiglottis closure + false cord adduction + true cord adduction).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Larynx DOES protect lower airway during swallowing (closes over trachea).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Phonation (voice production) IS a laryngeal function.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Larynx helps with Valsalva and effort closure (glottis closes for coughing, straining, childbirth).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Laryngeal closure allows breath-holding → thoracic fixation (for effort: lifting, defecation, childbirth).

questions.keyPoint: LARYNX FUNCTIONS: Airway protection (not food preparation), Phonation (voice), Pressure regulation (Valsalva/cough), Chest fixation (effort). FOOD BOLUS PREPARATION = oral cavity/tongue/teeth. The larynx CLOSES to protect the airway during swallowing — it does not prepare food.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 5. What does the level system for describing the neck refer to?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Not for skin lesions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A system for describing lymph nodes of the neck
choices.explanation: The NECK LEVEL SYSTEM (I-VI, sometimes VII) is used to describe the LOCATION OF LYMPH NODES in the neck. It is used in oncology for surgical planning (selective neck dissection) and for pathological staging of cervical lymph node metastases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A system for describing salivary glands
choices.explanation: Not for salivary glands specifically.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Describing movement of larynx during swallowing
choices.explanation: Not for laryngeal movement.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Not for venous drainage.

questions.keyPoint: NECK LEVELS (I-VI): Level I = submandibular/submental. II = upper jugular. III = mid-jugular. IV = lower jugular. V = posterior triangle. VI = central/anterior compartment. Used for neck dissection planning + staging of head and neck cancer LN metastases.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 6. Which of the following is an indication for adenoidectomy? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 104 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hyponasal speech = nasal blockage. Adenoidectomy may help if adenoids causing obstruction, but is not an indication per se.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: VPI (hypernasal speech) is a CONTRAINDICATION to adenoidectomy (removing adenoids worsens VPI, especially in patients with submucous cleft palate).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Epistaxis is not an indication for adenoidectomy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cleft palate (especially submucous) is a relative contraindication to adenoidectomy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNOEA in children (from adenotonsillar hypertrophy) IS an indication for adenoidectomy + tonsillectomy. Also: recurrent OME + chronic nasal obstruction + recurrent URTI from adenoids.

questions.keyPoint: ADENOIDECTOMY INDICATIONS: OSA (obstructive sleep apnoea), Recurrent OME (with hearing loss), Chronic nasal obstruction, Recurrent URTI. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Submucous cleft palate/VPI (risk of hypernasal speech post-adenoidectomy).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 7. In which patients presenting with unilateral nasal obstruction and recurrent epistaxis should angiofibroma be suspected?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Elderly male smokers with unilateral symptoms → suspect MALIGNANCY (SCC). JNA is a disease of adolescent males.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NPC is associated with Chinese/South-East Asian origin. JNA is not ethnicity-specific.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Middle-aged patients with atopy and asthma
choices.explanation: Middle-aged with atopy → allergic rhinitis + polyps. Not angiofibroma.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: JUVENILE NASOPHARYNGEAL ANGIOFIBROMA (JNA): suspect in ADOLESCENT MALES with recurrent profuse epistaxis + unilateral nasal obstruction. Androgen-dependent tumour. NO BIOPSY (haemorrhage risk). Diagnosis: CT + MRI + angiography.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Immunocompromised patients → opportunistic infections + certain malignancies. Not specifically JNA.

questions.keyPoint: JNA: ADOLESCENT MALE + recurrent PROFUSE EPISTAXIS + unilateral nasal obstruction = ANGIOFIBROMA. DO NOT BIOPSY. CT + MRI for diagnosis. Pre-op embolisation + surgical excision. Holman-Miller sign on lateral X-ray (anterior bowing of posterior maxillary sinus wall).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 8. Which virus is associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: EBV (EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS) = strongly associated with NPC, especially the undifferentiated (WHO Type III) form. EBV is also associated with: Burkitt lymphoma, Hodgkin lymphoma, primary CNS lymphoma, hairy leukoplakia, EBV-associated gastric cancer.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: VZV causes chickenpox/shingles. Not associated with NPC.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: HPV 6 and 11 = LARYNGEAL PAPILLOMATOSIS (recurrent respiratory papillomatosis). Not NPC.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HSV causes orolabial/genital herpes. Not associated with NPC.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Rhinovirus = common cold. Not associated with NPC.

questions.keyPoint: EBV (Epstein-Barr Virus) = NPC association (WHO Type III undifferentiated). Test: EBV-VCA IgA (screening in endemic areas). EBV also: Burkitt lymphoma + Hodgkin lymphoma + post-transplant lymphoma + infectious mononucleosis. NPC: radiosensitive → chemoradiotherapy.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 9. Treatment of a peritonsillar abscess requires:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Quinsy tonsillectomy (hot tonsillectomy) is rarely performed (high bleeding risk during acute infection). Tonsillectomy after second episode = standard approach.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PERITONSILLAR ABSCESS (QUINSY) = TRANSORAL INCISION AND DRAINAGE is the definitive treatment. Site: at the junction of the anterior pillar and the soft palate (above and lateral to the tonsil). Combined with IV antibiotics (amoxicillin/penicillin). Pain relief with analgesia. Oral fluids.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Transcervical approach = for parapharyngeal/deep neck abscesses. Not for peritonsillar abscess.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Endoscopic approach not standard for quinsy management.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Ultrasound-guided aspiration is used for deep/parapharyngeal abscesses. Not standard for quinsy (though some use simple needle aspiration for first attempt).

questions.keyPoint: QUINSY (PERITONSILLAR ABSCESS) TREATMENT: TRANSORAL I&D; + IV antibiotics. Site of incision: above/lateral to tonsil at junction of anterior pillar and soft palate. TONSILLECTOMY after 2nd episode (NOT during acute phase — hot tonsillectomy only in specialists' hands for specific indications).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 10. How should retropharyngeal abscess be treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Under local anaesthetic in the sitting position
choices.explanation: LA in sitting position is dangerous — risk of aspiration of pus if abscess ruptures spontaneously.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Under local anaesthetic with the patient supine
choices.explanation: LA insufficient and aspiration risk. Page 107 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Under general anaesthetic with the patient head down
choices.explanation: ETROPHARYNGEAL ABSCESS: Drains UNDER GENERAL ANAESTHESIA with the atient in the HEAD-DOWN (Trendelenburg) position. This prevents pus aspiration into the ungs if the abscess bursts during drainage. The airway is also secured under controlled onditions.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ntibiotics alone insufficient for established abscess (needs drainage). Antibiotics mportant adjunct.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: etropharyngeal abscess is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY requiring inpatient management, A, and drainage.

questions.keyPoint: RETROPHARYNGEAL ABSCESS DRAINAGE: Under GA + HEAD-DOWN (Trendelenburg) position to prevent pus aspiration. Can spread to posterior mediastinum via retropharyngeal space (danger space). ALWAYS secure airway first under GA before draining.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 11. A pharyngeal pouch develops in an area of weakness — where?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Where superior laryngeal nerve penetrates thyrohyoid membrane
choices.explanation: SLN enters through thyrohyoid membrane, not the site of pharyngeal pouch.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Between superior constrictor and skull base
choices.explanation: Not the site of pharyngeal pouch.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Where pharyngeal constrictors meet the midline raphe
choices.explanation: Not the specific site.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Not created by tonsillectomy. Page 108 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: etween the ower border of nferior onstrictor and ricopharyngeus
choices.explanation: KILLIAN'S DEHISCENCE: between THYROPHARYNGEUS (lower fibres of inferior constrictor) and CRICOPHARYNGEUS (upper oesophageal sphincter). This is an area of anatomical weakness where ZENKER'S DIVERTICULUM (pharyngeal pouch) forms. Increased UOS pressure + failure of cricopharyngeal relaxation → pulsion diverticulum.

questions.keyPoint: PHARYNGEAL POUCH (ZENKER'S DIVERTICULUM): KILLIAN'S DEHISCENCE = between inferior constrictor (thyropharyngeus) and cricopharyngeus. Pulsion diverticulum from increased UOS pressure + failure of cricopharyngeal relaxation. Classic presentation: elderly male + progressive dysphagia + regurgitation of undigested food + gurgling neck swelling + halitosis.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 12. Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the oropharynx is associated with:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SCC of the OROPHARYNX (tonsil, base of tongue, soft palate) is associated with TOBACCO SMOKING (traditional risk) AND increasingly with HPV-16/18 (the modern epidemic). HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC has BETTER PROGNOSIS than HPV-negative. HPV-positive = typically younger, non-smoker, multiple sexual partners.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: EBV = NPC (nasopharyngeal). Not oropharyngeal SCC.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Salted fish (nitrosamines) = NPC (nasopharyngeal). Not oropharyngeal SCC.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exposure to hardwood dust from furniture industry
choices.explanation: Hardwood dust = nasal adenocarcinoma (ethmoid). Not oropharyngeal SCC.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Poorly fitting dentures = oral cavity SCC (chronic mechanical irritation). Not specifically oropharyngeal SCC.

questions.keyPoint: OROPHARYNGEAL SCC: Tobacco + alcohol (traditional) + HPV-16/18 (increasing). HPV+ oropharyngeal SCC = BETTER PROGNOSIS, typically younger, non-smoker. HPV-negative = older, heavy smoker/drinker = worse prognosis. NPC = EBV. Nasal adenocarcinoma = hard wood dust.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 13. Which of the following statements regarding SCC of the hypopharynx are TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It carries a relatively good prognosis due to early presentation
choices.explanation: Hypopharyngeal SCC has POOR PROGNOSIS — late presentation (non-specific symptoms mimic common conditions).

choices.label: B
choices.text: It can be adequately assessed in the outpatient clinic
choices.explanation: Requires GA + direct laryngoscopy + biopsy for complete assessment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It rarely spreads to cervical lymph nodes
choices.explanation: Hypopharyngeal SCC has RICH LYMPHATIC DRAINAGE → frequently presents with cervical LN metastases (50-75% at presentation).

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is associated with submucosal spread
choices.explanation: TRUE: Hypopharyngeal SCC (especially post-cricoid type) tends to spread SUBMUCOSALLY → more extensive disease than clinically apparent → affects surgical margins. This is why adequate surgical margins are difficult and why recurrence is common.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It may present with otalgia referred through cranial nerve XI
choices.explanation: Referred otalgia from hypopharynx = via CN IX (glossopharyngeal/Jacobson's nerve). CN XI is motor only.

questions.keyPoint: HYPOPHARYNGEAL SCC: Poor prognosis (late presentation) + submucosal spread (difficult surgical margins) + rich lymphatic drainage (50-75% have LN mets at presentation). Post-cricoid type: female + Plummer-Vinson + anaemia. Referred otalgia via CN IX.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 14. In paediatric suspected acute epiglottitis, which of the following is CONTRAINDICATED?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Antibiotics (ceftriaxone IV) ARE part of treatment for epiglottitis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Examination of the mouth with headlight and spatula
choices.explanation: EXAMINING THE MOUTH/THROAT WITH SPATULA IS ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in suspected paediatric epiglottitis. Stimulating the already-swollen epiglottis with a spatula can cause COMPLETE AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION from laryngospasm. The child must be allowed to remain in a position of comfort (usually sitting forward, "tripod position") until the airway can be secured under controlled conditions.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Examination UNDER GA (with anaesthetist + ENT surgeon + equipment ready for emergency intubation/tracheostomy) IS appropriate — the controlled environment makes it safe.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Emergency intubation IS appropriate in acute respiratory failure.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Emergency tracheostomy IS appropriate if intubation fails.

questions.keyPoint: PAEDIATRIC EPIGLOTTITIS: NEVER examine the throat with a spatula/tongue depressor (complete airway obstruction risk). Keep child calm in position of comfort. Secure airway in controlled setting (GA + ENT + anaesthesia). H. influenzae type b (now rare with Hib vaccine). Classic: "thumb sign" on lateral neck X-ray.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 15. Which of the following are NOT indications for tracheostomy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Acute UAO IS an indication for tracheostomy/cricothyroidotomy. Page 111 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Anticipated/potential UAO (e.g., pre-op for radical head/neck surgery, post-op oedema) IS an indication.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX is NOT an indication for tracheostomy. It is treated by NEEDLE DECOMPRESSION (2nd intercostal space, mid-clavicular line) followed by chest drain. Tracheostomy addresses upper airway problems, not intrathoracic pathology.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Protection of lower airway from aspiration (e.g., neurological dysphagia, unconscious patients) IS an indication for tracheostomy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Prolonged ventilation (ICU) IS a major indication for tracheostomy (reduces dead space, facilitates weaning).

questions.keyPoint: TRACHEOSTOMY INDICATIONS: Airway obstruction (upper) + Prolonged ventilation + Protection (aspiration risk) + Pulmonary toilet. TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX = needle decompression + chest drain (not tracheostomy — different cavity/problem). Tracheostomy accesses the trachea, not the pleural space.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 16. Which of the following are advantages of a tracheostomy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Tracheostomy REDUCES anatomical dead space → MORE effective alveolar ventilation (not decreased).

choices.label: B
choices.text: An increase in the work of breathing
choices.explanation: Tracheostomy REDUCES work of breathing by reducing dead space + improving tracheal toilet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased rate of moisture exchange from the upper airway
choices.explanation: Tracheostomy BYPASSES the upper airway → LESS humidification (a disadvantage — need humidified circuit).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Tracheostomy may increase mucus production from the trachea due to direct irritation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TRUE: TRACHEOSTOMY REDUCES ANATOMICAL DEAD SPACE by ~50% (bypasses the nasopharynx, oropharynx, larynx = dead space). This improves alveolar ventilation efficiency, reduces work of breathing, and facilitates weaning from ventilator. Page 112 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: TRACHEOSTOMY ADVANTAGE: REDUCES ANATOMICAL DEAD SPACE by ~50%. Improves alveolar ventilation. Also: facilitates tracheal suction, allows oral feeding, reduces sedation requirement, facilitates ICU weaning. Disadvantage: bypasses upper airway humidification → need heated humidifier.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 17. Which of the following are NOT features of a modern tracheostomy tube?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Modern tracheostomy tubes HAVE a low-pressure, high-volume cuff (to reduce tracheal mucosal pressure necrosis).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Modern tubes HAVE a removable inner tube (can be cleaned without removing the whole tube).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Modern tubes have an outer flange (plate) for securing the tube to the neck.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A NASOGASTRIC TUBE PORT is NOT a feature of a tracheostomy tube. Tracheostomy tubes go into the TRACHEA; NG tubes go into the OESOPHAGUS — they are entirely separate.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Modern tracheostomy tubes are compatible with SPEAKING VALVES (Passy Muir valve) — allows airflow around the cuff during exhalation for vocalisation.

questions.keyPoint: Modern tracheostomy tube features: low-pressure cuff + inner tube + outer flange + 15mm connector (for ventilator) + speaking valve compatibility. NO NG TUBE PORT (completely different device — NG tube is oesophageal, not tracheal). The two must never be confused.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 18. Which of the following is true of cricothyroidotomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The procedure requires a high level of surgical expertise
choices.explanation: Cricothyroidotomy is EASIER than tracheostomy (the cricothyroid membrane is superficial and easy to identify). Can be performed by non-surgeons in emergency (CICO scenario).

choices.label: B
choices.text: It may be associated with subglottic stenosis
choices.explanation: TRUE: CRICOTHYROIDOTOMY can cause SUBGLOTTIC STENOSIS — the cricoid ring is the only complete tracheal ring and injury to the cricothyroid membrane or cricoid can → scar formation → subglottic narrowing. This is why cricothyroidotomy should be converted to formal TRACHEOSTOMY within 24-72 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a long-term solution to airway obstruction
choices.explanation: Cricothyroidotomy is a TEMPORARY EMERGENCY airway — should be converted to tracheostomy within 24-72h.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It involves excision of the anterior cricoid cartilage
choices.explanation: Incision is through the CRICOTHYROID MEMBRANE (between thyroid and cricoid cartilages), NOT excision of cartilage.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It should routinely be converted to a tracheostomy
choices.explanation: Actually this statement is also TRUE — conversion to tracheostomy is recommended.

questions.keyPoint: CRICOTHYROIDOTOMY: Emergency temporary airway. COMPLICATION = SUBGLOTTIC STENOSIS (from cricoid ring injury). Convert to FORMAL TRACHEOSTOMY within 24-72 hours. Easier than tracheostomy (superficial cricothyroid membrane). Used in CICO (Cannot Intubate Cannot Oxygenate) emergency.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 19. What is the preferred treatment for vocal fold nodules? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 114 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: VOCAL FOLD NODULES (singer's/screamer's nodes) = SPEECH THERAPY (voice therapy) is the FIRST-LINE TREATMENT. Nodules result from vocal MISUSE/ABUSE → hoarseness worse at end of day. Voice therapy addresses the underlying vocal behaviour. Surgery (microlaryngoscopy + microsurgical excision) only for nodules that fail prolonged voice therapy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Surgery is SECOND-LINE (after failed voice therapy). Risk of scarring if surgery done first.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Radiotherapy for malignant vocal cord disease. Nodules are benign.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Steroid injection not standard first-line for nodules.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Inhaled steroids treat laryngeal conditions like croup and subglottic stenosis. Not for nodules.

questions.keyPoint: VOCAL FOLD NODULES (singer's/screamer's nodes): SPEECH THERAPY = FIRST-LINE. Bilateral, symmetrical, at the junction of anterior 1/3 and posterior 2/3. From vocal misuse/overuse. Hoarseness worsens with use (worse at end of day). Surgery only after failed voice therapy.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 20. Which statement is true of unilateral vocal cord palsy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: LEFT RLN is longer (loops under aortic arch) → more vulnerable → LEFT vocal cord palsy is more common than right.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It should be investigated by CT larynx
choices.explanation: Investigation: CT from BASE OF SKULL TO AORTIC ARCH (to trace the entire RLN course). CT larynx alone misses the majority of the RLN course.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It may be associated with a normal voice
choices.explanation: TRUE: UNILATERAL VOCAL CORD PALSY may produce a normal or near-normal voice if the CONTRALATERAL CORD COMPENSATES adequately by crossing the midline during phonation. Voice may be normal + weak cough + risk of aspiration.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: BILATERAL (not unilateral) RLN palsy may require tracheostomy (bilateral = stridor + airway compromise). Unilateral = hoarse voice, not airway emergency.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It leaves the vocal cord fully adducted
choices.explanation: Unilateral RLN palsy → cord usually lies in PARAMEDIAN or ABDUCTION position, NOT fully adducted.

questions.keyPoint: UNILATERAL VOCAL CORD PALSY: May have NORMAL VOICE (if contralateral cord compensates). LEFT more common (longer RLN). Investigate: CT from skull base to aortic arch (trace entire RLN course). Causes: thyroid surgery (most common iatrogenic), thyroid Ca, lung Ca, aortic aneurysm, idiopathic.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 21. Early laryngeal cancer presents with:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Referred otalgia = ADVANCED laryngeal cancer (CN IX/X involvement).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LN mets = ADVANCED disease.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: HOARSENESS = EARLIEST SYMPTOM of GLOTTIC carcinoma (even tiny lesions affect the cord → voice change). That is why glottic Ca has the BEST PROGNOSIS. Any hoarseness >3 weeks in smoker = urgent laryngoscopy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Dysphagia = advanced laryngeal Ca or supraglottic/hypopharyngeal Ca.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Weight loss = advanced/metastatic disease.

questions.keyPoint: EARLY GLOTTIC LARYNGEAL Ca = HOARSENESS. Glottic Ca detected EARLY because even small lesions affect voice → BEST PROGNOSIS. Supraglottic = dysphagia + referred otalgia (early) + hoarseness (late). Subglottic = stridor. Any hoarseness >3 weeks in smoker = cancer until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 22. Which of the following statements regarding laryngectomy is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The procedure involves separation of the airway from the GI tract
choices.explanation: TRUE: TOTAL LARYNGECTOMY permanently separates the AIRWAY (trachea) from the FOOD PASSAGE (pharynx → oesophagus). The stoma (tracheostome) becomes the permanent airway. The patient can no longer breathe through the nose/mouth. Voice rehabilitation: TEP (tracheo-oesophageal puncture) prosthesis is most effective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It can treat malignancy without affecting the voice
choices.explanation: Total laryngectomy = COMPLETE LOSS OF NATURAL VOICE. Voice rehabilitation via TEP prosthesis gives tracheo-oesophageal voice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The surgical tracheostomy may be closed once post-op swelling subsides
choices.explanation: The tracheostome in total laryngectomy is PERMANENT — the larynx is gone, the trachea must be permanently redirected to the neck stoma.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Voice rehabilitation is possible: TEP prosthesis (most effective), oesophageal voice, or electrolarynx.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is the recommended treatment for early laryngeal cancer
choices.explanation: Early (T1-T2) glottic cancer = RT or endoscopic laser (voice-preserving). Total laryngectomy = reserved for advanced (T3-T4) or salvage.

questions.keyPoint: TOTAL LARYNGECTOMY: Permanently separates airway from food passage. PERMANENT TRACHEOSTOME. Voice rehabilitation: (1) TEP prosthesis (most effective, needs tracheo-oesophageal puncture), (2) Oesophageal voice, (3) Electrolarynx. Used for advanced T3-T4 laryngeal Ca or RT failure.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 23. Which of the following applies to branchial cysts? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 117 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: They usually present in the neonatal period
choices.explanation: Neonatal neck masses = cystic hygroma (lymphatic malformation) or dermoid. Branchial cysts present in young ADULTS (2nd-3rd decade).

choices.label: B
choices.text: They present in the midline of the neck
choices.explanation: Midline neck masses = thyroglossal duct cysts. Branchial cysts are LATERAL neck masses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Infection of the cyst leads to an increase in size facilitating surgical excision
choices.explanation: INFECTION makes surgery MORE DIFFICULT (adhesions + inflamed planes). Treat infection first then elective excision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are found at the junction of the upper and middle third of the sternomastoid muscle
choices.explanation: TRUE: SECOND BRANCHIAL CYSTS (most common type) are located at the junction of the UPPER AND MIDDLE THIRD of the SCM. They lie deep to the anterior border of SCM, overlying the carotid sheath.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Branchial cysts contain TURBID, CHOLESTEROL-RICH fluid (not clear). Thyroglossal duct cysts contain clear mucoid fluid.

questions.keyPoint: SECOND BRANCHIAL CYST (most common): Location = junction upper and middle 1/3 of SCM (anterior border). Contains TURBID cholesterol-rich fluid. Presents in young adults (2nd-3rd decade). EXCISE ELECTIVELY (risk of recurrent infection). Infection first = antibiotics, then excise when settled.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 24. Which of the following is true of a cystic hygroma?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cystic hygroma (lymphatic malformation) typically presents at BIRTH or in early infancy (not puberty).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: TRUE: CYSTIC HYGROMA is BRILLIANTLY TRANSLUCENT (transilluminates brightly). This is pathognomonic — the large spaces filled with clear lymph fluid transmit light beautifully. Page 118 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are firm on W palpation
choices.explanation: Cystic hygroma is SOFT and COMPRESSIBLE (filled with fluid, thin-walled lymphatic vessels).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cosmetic W concern is the only indication for treatment
choices.explanation: Cystic hygroma can cause AIRWAY COMPROMISE (large lesions in infancy can obstruct the airway) → emergency treatment. Also surgical treatment for functional reasons.

choices.label: E
choices.text: They undergo a W predictable growth pattern
choices.explanation: Cystic hygroma growth is UNPREDICTABLE — can grow rapidly (especially during URTI = lymph production increases) or remain stable.

questions.keyPoint: CYSTIC HYGROMA (lymphatic malformation): BRILLIANTLY TRANSLUCENT (pathognomonic). Soft, compressible, multicystic. Posterior triangle of neck, most common in children. Can be large and cause AIRWAY COMPROMISE (neonatal emergency). Treatment: sclerotherapy or surgical excision.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 25. A thyroglossal duct cyst is a remnant of which structure?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Thyrocervical trunk is an arterial branch of the subclavian artery. Not related to thyroid development.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cervical sinus = ectodermal depression formed by 2nd arch overgrowth of 3rd/4th arches. Related to branchial cysts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The track of the thymus through the neck into the mediastinum
choices.explanation: Thymic remnants = rare. Thymus descends from neck into mediastinum from 3rd pharyngeal pouch.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: 4th pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the inferior parathyroids and ultimobranchial body (parafollicular C cells).

choices.label: E
choices.text: The track of the thyroid from the tongue base to the neck
choices.explanation: THYROGLOSSAL DUCT CYST = remnant of the THYROGLOSSAL DUCT — the tract along which the thyroid gland descends from the FORAMEN CAECUM (at the base of the tongue) to its final position in the anterior neck (pre-tracheal). This duct normally obliterates by the 10th week. Persistence → thyroglossal duct cysts along the tract (can be in tongue, suprahyoid, infrahyoid = most common). Page 119 niversity of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: THYROGLOSSAL DUCT CYST: Remnant of the thyroid migration tract from FORAMEN CAECUM (tongue base) → pre-tracheal position. MIDLINE, moves with swallowing AND with tongue protrusion (unique!). May contain ectopic thyroid tissue. Sistrunk operation (removal with central hyoid bone body).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 26. Following blunt laryngeal trauma, which of these statements is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anatomy allows for significant swelling prior to loss of function
choices.explanation: The opposite is true — laryngeal swelling can RAPIDLY cause airway obstruction. Laryngeal oedema can be rapid and life-threatening.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Endotracheal intubation should be performed prior to formation of oedema
choices.explanation: Blind intubation in blunt laryngeal trauma is DANGEROUS (risk of creating a false passage). Securing the airway under direct vision (video laryngoscopy or awake tracheostomy) is preferred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fractured cartilages should be left untouched to optimise laryngeal function
choices.explanation: Displaced cartilage fractures should be REDUCED AND FIXATED to restore laryngeal architecture.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TRUE: In blunt laryngeal trauma, if airway needs to be secured surgically, a LOW TRACHEOSTOMY (below the cricoid, through tracheal rings 2-4) is preferred over cricothyroidotomy (risk of subglottic stenosis) or high tracheostomy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If laryngeal stent placed, an open procedure is required for removal
choices.explanation: Laryngeal stents can be removed endoscopically.

questions.keyPoint: BLUNT LARYNGEAL TRAUMA: (1) Assess airway urgency. (2) If intubation needed = video laryngoscopy or awake tracheostomy (NOT blind intubation). (3) LOW TRACHEOSTOMY preferred for surgical airway. (4) Cartilage fractures need reduction + fixation. CT larynx for assessment.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 27. Ludwig's angina describes which condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Submandibular swelling with inflammatory oedema of the floor of mouth
choices.explanation: LUDWIG'S ANGINA = spreading cellulitis/phlegmon of the submandibular, submental, and sublingual spaces bilaterally. Causes: dental infection (lower molars), floor of mouth trauma. Features: brawny, board-like induration of floor of mouth + neck + submandibular region, elevated tongue (pushed up and back → airway emergency), systemic toxicity, trismus. AIRWAY EMERGENCY.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spreading cellulitis of lower neck with dark discoloration from necrosis
choices.explanation: This describes necrotising fasciitis of the neck — different condition.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Gingivostomatitis = viral or bacterial gum/mouth inflammation. Not Ludwig's.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Oesophageal pain. Not Ludwig's.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cellulitis secondary to zoster lesions in cervical dermatomes
choices.explanation: Zoster cellulitis = shingles complication. Not Ludwig's.

questions.keyPoint: LUDWIG'S ANGINA: Bilateral submandibular/submental/sublingual space phlegmon. Most common cause: DENTAL INFECTION (lower molars). Features: board-like induration + elevated tongue → AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Treatment: IV antibiotics (broad-spectrum, anti-anaerobic) + surgical drainage + AIRWAY MANAGEMENT (awake tracheostomy if needed).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 28. A collar-stud abscess refers to which disease process?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bacterial cervical LN = acute pyogenic lymphadenitis — tender, hot, fluctuant. Single node. Not collar-stud.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infection of a necrotic malignant cervical lymph node
choices.explanation: Infected necrotic malignant LN = different — primary malignancy with necrotic LN.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: COLLAR-STUD ABSCESS = SCROFULA (tuberculous cervical lymphadenitis). The deep abscess in the neck breaks through the deep fascia, creating an HOURGLASS/COLLAR-STUD SHAPED COLLECTION with the deep portion communicating with the superficial collection through a narrow fascial defect. A palpable fluctuant superficial swelling + deep component.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tracking of a peritonsillar abscess into the parapharyngeal space
choices.explanation: Peritonsillar abscess extension = parapharyngeal abscess. Not collar-stud.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A postoperative wound infection after thyroidectomy
choices.explanation: Post-thyroidectomy infection would be through the thyroid collar incision, not a collar-stud abscess.

questions.keyPoint: COLLAR-STUD ABSCESS = TUBERCULOUS CERVICAL LYMPHADENITIS (SCROFULA). The abscess PERFORATES THE DEEP FASCIA creating a hourglass/collar-stud shape with superficial and deep components connected through a narrow fascial neck. M. tuberculosis most common. FNAC + AFB/TB culture for diagnosis. Anti-TB therapy ± surgical drainage.

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 29. Which of the following statements about carotid body tumours is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 90% of patients have a family history
choices.explanation: ~10% are familial (not 90%). Familial cases are associated with SDH (succinate dehydrogenase) gene mutations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are more common in populations living at high altitude
choices.explanation: TRUE: CAROTID BODY TUMOURS (chemodectomas/paragangliomas) are MORE COMMON in populations living at HIGH ALTITUDE. The chronic hypoxia at altitude causes carotid body hyperplasia → increased risk of tumour development.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Most carotid body tumours are NON-FUNCTIONING (non-secreting). Only 1-3% secrete catecholamines (unlike phaeochromocytoma).

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mass can be moved up and down but not side to side
choices.explanation: OPPOSITE: Carotid body tumours can be moved SIDE TO SIDE (Fontaine's sign) but NOT up and down (fixed to the carotid bifurcation).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: FNA is CONTRAINDICATED in suspected carotid body tumour (risk of haemorrhage). Diagnosis: CT/MRI + angiography.

questions.keyPoint: CAROTID BODY TUMOUR (paraganglioma): More common at HIGH ALTITUDE. Located at carotid bifurcation. Moves side-to-side (Fontaine's sign) but NOT up-down. Pulsatile mass in neck. Diagnosis: MRI (saliva and pepper appearance) + angiography. FNA CONTRAINDICATED. Treatment: surgical excision (pre-op embolisation for large tumours).

questions.stem: Bailey & Love Ch45. 30. A procedure which removes all lymph nodes on one side of the neck but PRESERVES the accessory nerve, jugular vein and sternomastoid muscle is called:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: RADICAL neck dissection removes ALL LN (I-V) + SCM + IJV + CN XI. Most disfiguring. Page 123 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Not a standard term.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SELECTIVE = removes only SOME levels (e.g., II-IV for oral cavity cancer). Does NOT remove all levels.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Not a standard term.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MODIFIED RADICAL NECK DISSECTION (MRND): removes ALL LN levels (I-V) WHILE PRESERVING one or more of: CN XI (accessory nerve), IJV (internal jugular vein), and/or SCM (sternomastoid muscle). Most commonly preserves all three. Less morbidity than radical (preserves shoulder function via CN XI).

questions.keyPoint: NECK DISSECTION TYPES: Radical = all LN (I-V) + SCM + IJV + CN XI REMOVED. MODIFIED RADICAL (MRND) = all LN (I-V) but PRESERVES CN XI + IJV + SCM. SELECTIVE = some LN levels only (not all). The question description = MRND (preserves CN XI + IJV + SCM = all 3 preserved).

questions.stem: 3-YEAR-OLD BOY: yellow discharge from right nostril for 3 days. Examination of nostril difficult (distressed child) but you see a small piece of plastic. Which of the following would you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue to try remove it yourself
choices.explanation: In a distressed child, repeated attempts to remove FB may cause trauma and push it deeper or risk aspiration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give oral antibiotics and see child in a week
choices.explanation: The discharge may indicate a partially obstructed FB. This needs referral, not just antibiotics.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cauterization is not indicated for a nasal foreign body.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Packing is for epistaxis control. Page 124 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Refer to ENT on call to remove foreign body
choices.explanation: NASAL FOREIGN BODY in a distressed child: if removal cannot be achieved simply and safely in the GP/A&E; setting, REFER TO ENT ON CALL. Specialist tools (Jobson Horne probe, balloon catheter, Nott's forceps) + calm environment + possibly sedation/GA for safe removal without aspiration.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL FOREIGN BODY in children: foul-smelling UNILATERAL discharge = FB until proven otherwise. Mother's kiss technique (caregiver blows into child's mouth while occluding unaffected nostril) = safe first attempt. Refer if not easily removed. NEVER forceful blind probing (aspiration risk).

questions.stem: 64-YEAR-OLD WOMAN: pain and difficulty swallowing, progressive worsening, now even liquids difficult, appears CACHEXIC. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Candidal oesophagitis causes odynophagia in immunocompromised patients. Progressive worsening to liquids + cachexia suggests malignancy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: OESOPHAGEAL CARCINOMA: Progressive dysphagia (solids → liquids) + weight loss/cachexia = MALIGNANT DYSPHAGIA until proven otherwise. Investigations: endoscopy + biopsy. SCC of upper/mid oesophagus; adenocarcinoma of lower oesophagus (Barrett's).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Oesophageal spasm causes intermittent dysphagia (to both solids and liquids simultaneously) + chest pain. Not progressive or causing cachexia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Benign stricture (reflux-related) causes dysphagia to solids. Less severe weight loss.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Plummer-Vinson syndrome web = upper oesophageal web in female with iron deficiency. Intermittent dysphagia initially.

questions.keyPoint: PROGRESSIVE DYSPHAGIA (solids → liquids) + WEIGHT LOSS/CACHEXIA = OESOPHAGEAL CARCINOMA until proven otherwise. Urgent endoscopy + biopsy. SCC = upper/mid oesophagus (smoking + alcohol). Adenocarcinoma = lower oesophagus/gastro-oesophageal junction (Barrett's oesophagus + GERD).

questions.stem: 72-YEAR-OLD MAN: difficulty swallowing + regurgitation of food. Several chest infections over last few months. SOFT SWELLING in anterior triangle of neck — makes a GURGLING SOUND on palpation. What is the most likely cause of his dysphagia?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Carotid aneurysm = pulsatile, not gurgling.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LN = firm, not gurgling.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Laryngocoele enlarges on Valsalva. Gurgling suggests fluid-filled sac.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CLASSIC PHARYNGEAL POUCH: elderly male + progressive dysphagia + regurgitation of UNDIGESTED food + aspiration pneumonia + GURGLING NOISE on palpating the neck swelling (Boyce's sign — compressing the pouch regurgitates food). Investigation: BARIUM SWALLOW. Treatment: endoscopic stapling (Dohlman) or open Zenker's diverticulectomy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Thyroglossal cyst = midline, moves with swallowing + tongue, no gurgling.

questions.keyPoint: PHARYNGEAL POUCH (ZENKER'S): Elderly male + progressive dysphagia + regurgitation of UNDIGESTED food + recurrent pneumonia (aspiration) + GURGLING NECK SWELLING (Boyce's sign). Barium swallow = diagnostic. DO NOT SCOPE (risk of perforation). Treatment: endoscopic stapling (Dohlman).

questions.stem: 7-YEAR-OLD BOY: father says he swallowed a 5 pence coin earlier. Has not experienced any symptoms and on examination is well. Which of the following investigations would you perform in the first instance?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Barium swallow is not the first-line investigation for FB ingestion in children.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Laryngoscopy assesses the larynx/hypopharynx. Coin in oesophagus/stomach needs X-ray first.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PLAIN X-RAYS (neck + chest + abdomen) in the FIRST INSTANCE for ingested foreign body. Coins are radio-opaque → X-ray shows location. Oesophageal coin (AP = round, lateral = linear stacking) requires urgent endoscopy. Gastric/small bowel coin = usually passes spontaneously (>91% within 4 days). Follow-up X-ray in 7 days if asymptomatic.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Investigation IS required to locate the coin (oesophageal = endoscopic removal; gastric = observe).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Endoscopy comes AFTER X-ray confirms oesophageal location. Not the first step.

questions.keyPoint: INGESTED FOREIGN BODY in children: FIRST = PLAIN X-RAYS (neck + chest + abdomen). Coins: radio-opaque. OESOPHAGEAL coin (AP = round/stacked) → urgent endoscopic removal. GASTRIC coin → observe (usually passes in 4-7 days). Battery = ENDOSCOPIC EMERGENCY (causes mucosal burns within 2h).

questions.stem: 23-YEAR-OLD MAN: recent recurrent nosebleeds, father was the same. Multiple DILATED BLOOD VESSELS around the nose and mouth on examination. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Haemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency) causes haemarthroses, muscle bleeds, spontaneous bleeding. Mucocutaneous telangiectasia is NOT a feature.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hypertension can cause epistaxis but not telangiectasia visible around nose and mouth.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: HHT (Hereditary Haemorrhagic Telangiectasia) = autosomal dominant. Classic features: (1) MUCOCUTANEOUS TELANGIECTASIA (visible around nose + lips + fingertips), (2) RECURRENT EPISTAXIS, (3) FAMILY HISTORY (AD inheritance), (4) Visceral AVMs (pulmonary, hepatic, cerebral). Diagnosis: Curaçao criteria (3/4 = definite HHT). Page 127 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Polyps are grey, avascular, insensate. Do not cause telangiectasia or family history pattern.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Sturge-Weber = port-wine stain (facial) + leptomeningeal angiomas + glaucoma. Not mucocutaneous telangiectasia + recurrent epistaxis.

questions.keyPoint: HHT (OSLER-WEBER-RENDU): Mucocutaneous telangiectasia + recurrent epistaxis + family history (AD) + visceral AVMs. Curaçao criteria: ≥3 of 4 = definite. Treatment: oestrogen creams + laser + septodermoplasty + bevacizumab. Screen: echocardiogram (pulmonary AVM).

questions.topic: Dysphagia: Small tongue + exaggerated gag reflex
questions.stem: 56-YEAR-OLD WOMAN: progressive difficulty swallowing, increased difficulty in articulating speech. TONGUE IS SMALL AND STIFF. EXAGGERATED GAG REFLEX. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: FILE ANSWER: Bulbar palsy. However, see flag below — small stiff tongue + exaggerated gag reflex = UMN features.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MG: fatigable weakness, ptosis, diplopia. Normal reflexes. No UMN signs.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Vocal cord paralysis causes hoarseness, not the described picture.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: See FLAG below.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Scleroderma causes dysphagia from oesophageal dysmotility, not UMN tongue signs.

questions.keyPoint: BULBAR vs PSEUDOBULBAR palsy: BULBAR (LMN) = flaccid tongue, fasciculations, absent gag, nasal voice, wasting. PSEUDOBULBAR (UMN) = SMALL STIFF TONGUE, EXAGGERATED gag reflex, spastic dysarthria (hot potato voice), emotional lability. CAUSES: Bilateral MCA strokes, MS, MND (UMN).

questions.stem: 46-YEAR-OLD WOMAN: gradual progressive hearing loss + IMBALANCE + TINGLING on left side of face. Vibrating tuning fork LOUDER WHEN PLACED BY THE EAR ON THE LEFT side rather than on the mastoid process. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ACOUSTIC NEUROMA (vestibular schwannoma): unilateral SNHL + tinnitus + balance problems. Tuning fork louder by ear = Rinne POSITIVE = AC>BC = SNHL (not conductive). LEFT Rinne positive but reduced hearing = LEFT SNHL. CN VIII compression. Associated CN V (tingling face = CN V involvement from larger tumour in CPA). MRI confirms.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Menière's: episodic vertigo + SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. No facial tingling typically.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Otosclerosis: conductive HL + Rinne NEGATIVE (BC>AC). Facial tingling not a feature.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Perforated TM: Rinne NEGATIVE (conductive HL). No facial tingling.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Presbycusis: bilateral symmetric HF SNHL in elderly. No facial tingling.

questions.keyPoint: ACOUSTIC NEUROMA: Unilateral SNHL (Rinne+ on affected side) + imbalance + tinnitus ± CN V symptoms (facial tingling from CPA tumour compressing CN V). MRI gadolinium-enhanced = GOLD STANDARD. Most common tumour of CPA. Arises from Schwann cells of superior vestibular nerve.

questions.stem: 44-YEAR-OLD MAN: 2-month history of PAINLESS, PURULENT, FOUL-SMELLING DISCHARGE from right ear. Progressive hearing loss. NO visible external abnormality. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 129 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: AOM: acute painful episode + fever. Not 2 months of painless offensive discharge.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cerumen (wax): conductive HL, no purulent discharge.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CHOLESTEATOMA (CSOM squamous type): painless, FOUL-SMELLING (offensive/foetid) discharge + progressive conductive HL. Normal external examination (no external inflammation = middle ear disease). ATTIC/PERIPHERAL perforation on otoscopy. Locally destructive. Treatment: MASTOIDECTOMY.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: OME: dull/amber intact TM. NO discharge.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Otitis externa: pain on pinna traction + discharge in swimmer + EAC inflamed. Not painless offensive discharge with progressive HL.

questions.keyPoint: CHOLESTEATOMA: PAINLESS + FOUL-SMELLING (offensive) discharge + PROGRESSIVE CONDUCTIVE HL + normal external ear + ATTIC PERFORATION on otoscopy. Film of pearl-like white material if seen. Treatment: MASTOIDECTOMY (canal wall up or down). Locally destructive (ossicles, CN VII, labyrinth).

questions.stem: 60-YEAR-OLD MAN: recurrent dizzy spells for 4 months, occurring daily. Dizzy spells last a few minutes and seem to occur on head movements. Keeps head still. No deafness or tinnitus. Neurological examination entirely normal. Favour diagnosis of BPPV. Which of the following descriptions of findings on Hallpike's manoeuvre would confirm this diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delayed onset (a few seconds) torsional nystagmus on descent FACING BOTH SIDES
choices.explanation: Bilateral nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike suggests central pathology (cerebellar disease). BPPV = UNILATERAL (one side only — the affected ear). Page 130 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed onset (a few seconds) torsional nystagmus on descent FACING ONE SIDE ONLY
choices.explanation: DIX-HALLPIKE POSITIVE FOR BPPV: DELAYED ONSET (latency 1-5 seconds) + ROTATORY TORSIONAL NYSTAGMUS + on descent facing ONE SIDE ONLY (the affected side). FATIGABLE (decreases with repeated testing). Geotropic (fast phase toward the ground). Resolves within 1 minute.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate torsional nystagmus on descent facing both sides
choices.explanation: Immediate (no latency) = CENTRAL pathology (cerebellar/brainstem). Both sides = also suggests central. BPPV has latency + unilateral.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immediate torsional nystagmus on descent facing one side only
