choices.explanation: Immediate (no latency) = central. Must have latency (1-5 sec) for BPPV.

choices.label: E
choices.text: No nystagmus on descent facing either side
choices.explanation: No nystagmus = negative Dix-Hallpike (BPPV not confirmed).

questions.keyPoint: POSITIVE DIX-HALLPIKE for BPPV: DELAYED (1-5 sec) + ROTATORY TORSIONAL nystagmus + ONE SIDE ONLY (affected side) + FATIGABLE. CENTRAL red flags: no latency, non-fatigable, direction-changing, vertical nystagmus, bilateral. REMEMBER: must test both sides.

questions.stem: 43-YEAR-OLD MAN: 3-month history of RECURRENT NOSEBLEEDS + MUCOSAL BLEEDING + HAEMOPTYSIS + RECURRENT SINUSITIS. NASAL DEFORMITY + chest auscultation reveals CRACKLES in left lower zone. Urine dipstick: MICROSCOPIC HAEMATURIA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CML: fatigue, splenomegaly, raised WBC with left shift. Not nasal deformity + haematuria + lung crackles in this pattern.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CLL: lymphocytosis, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly. Not this triad. Page 131 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: hurg-Strauss (EGPA): asthma + eosinophilia + rhinosinusitis + mononeuritis. p-ANCA. imilar but prominent asthma + eosinophilia distinguishes it.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: oodpasture's: pulmonary haemorrhage + glomerulonephritis. Anti-GBM antibodies. No inusitis or nasal deformity.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: EGENER'S (GPA) CLASSIC TRIAD: (1) UPPER AIRWAY (saddle-nose deformity + inusitis + epistaxis), (2) LUNGS (haemoptysis + crackles = pulmonary nodules/cavities), 3) KIDNEYS (haematuria = glomerulonephritis). c-ANCA (PR3) positive in >90%.

questions.keyPoint: WEGENER'S (GPA): SADDLE-NOSE + SINUSITIS + HAEMOPTYSIS + HAEMATURIA. c-ANCA (PR3-ANCA) >90% positive. Necrotising granulomatous vasculitis. Treatment: cyclophosphamide + prednisolone (induction) → azathioprine/methotrexate (maintenance). Urgent (renal failure risk).

questions.stem: 75-YEAR-OLD WOMAN: 1-week SEVERE LEFT-SIDED EARACHE (especially at night), carrying tissues (copious exudate). CONTROLLED DIABETES MELLITUS. Physical: GRANULATION TISSUE in external auditory canal. ESR elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: OME: painless, dull/amber intact TM, no granulation tissue.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Mastoiditis: post-auricular swelling + tenderness + auricle displaced anteroinferiorly. No granulation in EAC.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MALIGNANT (NECROTISING) OTITIS EXTERNA: ELDERLY DIABETIC + severe unilateral otalgia (especially at night) + GRANULATION TISSUE at bone-cartilage junction in EAC + copious discharge + ELEVATED ESR. Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Can → skull base osteomyelitis + CN palsies.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bullous myringitis: viral, haemorrhagic blisters on TM, severe otalgia. No granulation tissue.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Answer is malignant OE.

questions.keyPoint: MALIGNANT OE: Elderly DIABETIC + severe night otalgia + GRANULATION TISSUE at bone-cartilage junction of EAC + elevated ESR. Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Investigate: CT temporal bone + Tc-99m bone scan. Treat: prolonged IV ciprofloxacin + surgical debridement + hyperbaric O2 in refractory cases.

questions.stem: 4-YEAR-OLD GIRL: sore throat, finding it painful to swallow saliva. 1-week history of sore throat and feeling unwell. Febrile 39°C, DROOLING SALIVA. FAINT HIGH-PITCHED SOUND HEARD ON INSPIRATION. What will you do next?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Call for senior help and an ENT specialist
choices.explanation: PAEDIATRIC EPIGLOTTITIS MANAGEMENT: (1) CALL FOR HELP (senior paediatrician + ENT + anaesthesia) IMMEDIATELY. (2) Do NOT examine the throat (no spatula). (3) Keep child CALM in position of comfort. (4) Secure airway in controlled setting (GA). (5) IV ceftriaxone after airway secured.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediately set up an adrenaline nebuliser
choices.explanation: Adrenaline nebuliser = for CROUP (viral laryngotracheitis, barking cough). Not first-line for epiglottitis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Oral antibiotics inappropriate — child cannot swallow due to severe epiglottitis. IV route after airway secured.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Requesting X-ray requires lying the child flat + moving to radiology department — DANGEROUS (can precipitate complete airway obstruction). Do NOT send child to X-ray.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Surgical airway is last resort (if cannot intubate/ventilate). Not first step.

questions.keyPoint: PAEDIATRIC EPIGLOTTITIS (H. influenzae type b): Drooling + dysphagia + stridor + fever. CALL FOR HELP FIRST (ENT + anaesthesia + senior paediatrician). DO NOT examine throat. Keep calm. Secure airway in controlled environment under GA. IV ceftriaxone. X-ray = DANGEROUS (avoid in acute setting).

questions.stem: 19-YEAR-OLD WOMAN: 2-day history of LEFT-SIDED EARACHE. Pain worsened over 24 hours, feels "blocked", using COTTON WOOL BUDS to clean ear. Afebrile 36.7°C. Pain on PRESSURE on tragus/pinna. 1. Ear pain (1) — What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ACUTE OTITIS EXTERNA: Cotton bud use (disrupts protective cerumen + traumatises canal skin) → secondary bacterial infection. Pain on pinna/tragus movement + canal skin erythema/oedema. Afebrile = no systemic involvement.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Chronic OE: months of symptoms, thickened canal skin. Not acute onset.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: AOM: systemic fever + TM bulging. Pain not specifically worse on pinna traction.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Mastoiditis: post-auricular swelling + auricle displacement.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CSOM: persistent mucopurulent discharge (not cotton bud-related acute pain).

questions.keyPoint: ACUTE OE: Cotton bud use → removes cerumen → canal maceration + secondary infection (Pseudomonas/S.aureus). PAIN ON PINNA TRACTION = key clinical sign. Treatment: TOPICAL acidifying + antibiotic + steroid drops (e.g., Otomize). Systemic if spreading cellulitis.

questions.topic: Ear Pain: Management of Acute OE
questions.stem: Same 19-year-old woman with acute otitis externa (cotton bud use). 2. Management of ear pain — What is most appropriate therapy? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 134 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discharge with advice to stop using cotton wool buds
choices.explanation: Advice alone is insufficient — active infection requires treatment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discharge with combination of ACIDIFYING and antibiotic ear drops
choices.explanation: TREATMENT of ACUTE OE: TOPICAL ACIDIFYING (e.g., acetic acid 2% = restores normal acidic pH of EAC to inhibit bacterial growth) + ANTIBIOTIC (ciprofloxacin/neomycin) + STEROID drops. Combination addresses all aspects: infection + inflammation + pH restoration.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Analgesia alone does not treat the infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Referral to ENT specialist for myringotomy
choices.explanation: Myringotomy is for OME/AOM. Not for otitis externa.

choices.label: E
choices.text: None of the above as the ear canal is 'self-cleaning'
choices.explanation: The ear canal is self-cleaning under normal circumstances, but active OE requires treatment.

questions.keyPoint: ACUTE OE TREATMENT: Topical ACIDIFYING drops (acetic acid 2%) + topical ANTIBIOTIC + topical STEROID. Also: keep ear DRY, stop cotton buds, analgesia. Oral antibiotics only for spreading cellulitis or systemic features. Ear wick if canal very oedematous (to allow drops to penetrate).

questions.stem: 6-YEAR-OLD ASIAN BOY: right-sided earache all day, lethargic, temperature 39°C, PR 110, BP 90/40, no cervical lymphadenopathy. Bright red right TM (bulging). 3. Pathogens in ear disease — Most likely pathogen?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Group B Strep = neonatal sepsis. Not ASOM in 6-year-old.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: H. INFLUENZAE = second most common cause of ASOM (~30%). S. pneumoniae ~40% (most common). In a 6-year-old, either is possible. Note: unimmunised child (Hib vaccine not given) → H. influenzae type b more likely. The file marks H. influenzae as the answer here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TB otitis media is extremely rare (atypical presentation). Page 135 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: . catarrhalis ~20%. Third most common. Less likely than H. influenzae here.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: . influenzae is the answer per file.

questions.keyPoint: ASOM PATHOGENS: S. pneumoniae (40%) > H. influenzae (30%) > M. catarrhalis (20%). In children with incomplete vaccination, H. influenzae type b more prominent. Treatment: amoxicillin (first-line). Co-amoxiclav if beta-lactamase producing H. influenzae or M. catarrhalis.

questions.stem: Same patient as Q3. Infection progressing to deeper structures. 8. Causative pathogens in ear infections — most likely responsible for deep/chronic infection?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA = most important pathogen in CHRONIC and DEEP ear infections. Especially: (1) Malignant otitis externa in diabetics, (2) CSOM (especially squamous type) with offensive discharge, (3) Post-tympanostomy tube infection. Pseudomonas has high intrinsic antibiotic resistance and produces biofilm.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Viridans streptococci = dental infections + subacute bacterial endocarditis. Less relevant in ear disease.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: S. aureus = acute otitis externa (furunculosis) + ASOM. Less typical of deep/chronic infection.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: E. coli less common in ear infections.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: M. catarrhalis = ASOM (acute). Less typical of deep/chronic ear infection.

questions.keyPoint: PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA = key pathogen in CHRONIC + DEEP ear infections (CSOM squamous, malignant OE). Also: post-grommet infection, persistent CSOM. Treat with CIPROFLOXACIN (topical or systemic). Biofilm formation makes eradication difficult.

questions.topic: Decline in Hearing: Right Conductive Deafness
questions.stem: 22-YEAR-OLD CAUCASIAN MAN: unable to hear properly in right ear after a blow during rugby. Rinne's NEGATIVE (bone > air). Weber test: sound heard BEST ON THE RIGHT. 9. Decline in hearing — most appropriate statement?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Weber lateralises to the WORSE side in conductive HL. Sound heard best on RIGHT = right is affected (if conductive). Rinne negative (BC>AC) = CONDUCTIVE. Therefore RIGHT.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: RINNE NEGATIVE (BC>AC) = CONDUCTIVE HL. WEBER lateralises to RIGHT = confirms RIGHT conductive HL (sound lateralises to the WORSE hearing side in conductive HL). Post-trauma → ossicular dislocation or haemotympanum causing RIGHT CONDUCTIVE HL.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SNHL: Rinne POSITIVE (AC>BC). Weber lateralises to the BETTER (contralateral) ear. Not this pattern.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Weber would lateralise LEFT for left conductive. Here it lateralises RIGHT.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Answer is right-sided conductive deafness.

questions.keyPoint: TUNING FORK INTERPRETATION: Rinne NEGATIVE (BC>AC) = CONDUCTIVE. Weber lateralises to AFFECTED ear in CONDUCTIVE HL, and to BETTER ear in SNHL. Post-trauma RIGHT Rinne negative + Weber to right = RIGHT CONDUCTIVE HL (haemotympanum or ossicular dislocation).

questions.stem: Same patient as Q9. Otoscopy: limited amount of cerumen + DEFECT in tympanic membrane. 10. Diagnosis of ear disease (1) — most likely? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 137 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Otosclerosis: progressive conductive HL in young adults. No TM perforation. Not from acute trauma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Wax: conductive HL from occlusion. No TM defect.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ET obstruction: retracted/dull TM with effusion. No perforation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TM PERFORATION confirmed on otoscopy (defect visible) after trauma. Explains Rinne negative (conductive HL) + Weber to right. Management: keep dry, heals in 3 months, myringoplasty if not healed.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Answer is perforated TM.

questions.keyPoint: TRAUMATIC TM PERFORATION: Visible defect on otoscopy after trauma. Rinne negative (conductive HL). Weber lateralises to affected side. Management: keep dry + no swimming + no nose-blowing + reassurance + myringoplasty if not healed in 3 months.

questions.stem: 34-YEAR-OLD MAN: long history of ear problems since childhood. 10-day pain in and behind same ear. Continuous discharge from ear. "Under the weather" + fever 37.5°C. Bulging RED tympanic membrane. 5. Ear pain (2) — most appropriate management statement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discharge on high-dose oral antibiotics for 7 days
choices.explanation: Systemic symptoms + bulging red TM with history of ear problems = complicated ASOM or early mastoiditis. This needs admission + IV antibiotics, not outpatient oral treatment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Admit for intravenous antibiotics and investigation
choices.explanation: ASOM with systemic features (fever + tachycardia + feeling unwell + posterior ear pain = early mastoiditis) → ADMIT FOR IV ANTIBIOTICS (IV amoxicillin or co-amoxiclav) + CT to exclude mastoiditis/intracranial complications. Not safe for outpatient management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Condition cannot be treated by myringotomy + tympanostomy tube
choices.explanation: Myringotomy + grommet is a treatment option for certain cases. Not the immediate priority here.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mastoidectomy should be performed as soon as possible
choices.explanation: Mastoidectomy is indicated for confirmed coalescent mastoiditis with bone destruction — not immediately without IV antibiotics trial and investigation. Page 138 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Answer is B.

questions.keyPoint: ASOM WITH SYSTEMIC FEATURES (fever + pain + unwell) = ADMIT for IV antibiotics + investigation to exclude MASTOIDITIS or intracranial complications. CT temporal bones if mastoiditis suspected. Do NOT send home on oral antibiotics alone.

questions.stem: Question about which complication may NOT occur with acute otitis media. 6. Complications of otitis media — which may all complicate acute otitis media, EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Facial paralysis (CN VII) IS a complication of ASOM — direct spread to facial nerve in the Fallopian canal.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Extradural abscess IS a complication — spreads through the tegmen.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mastoiditis IS the most common complication of ASOM.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CAVERNOUS SINUS THROMBOSIS is more commonly associated with FRONTAL SINUSITIS + FACIAL INFECTIONS (via ophthalmic veins). While it can occur theoretically from ASOM (via lateral sinus → cavernous sinus), it is NOT a DIRECT complication of ASOM and is NOT typically listed as a standard ASOM complication. The file marks this as the EXCEPT answer.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Labyrinthitis IS a complication of ASOM (spread to the labyrinth → vertigo + SNHL).

questions.keyPoint: ASOM COMPLICATIONS: Mastoiditis (most common) + Facial palsy + Labyrinthitis + Petrositis + Extradural abscess + Subdural empyema + Meningitis + Brain abscess + Lateral sinus thrombosis. CAVERNOUS SINUS THROMBOSIS is primarily from SINUSITIS (frontal/ethmoid), not ASOM directly.

questions.stem: 65-YEAR-OLD CAUCASIAN WOMAN: gradual difficulty hearing in LEFT ear. Has to ask people to speak up in public. Weber's test localises to the RIGHT. Rinne's test POSITIVE BILATERALLY. 11. Difficulty hearing — most appropriate statement?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: RINNE POSITIVE BILATERAL = no conductive element. WEBER to RIGHT = RIGHT is the BETTER ear (in SNHL, Weber goes to better/unaffected side). Therefore LEFT ear has SNHL. AGE + bilateral Rinne positive + unilateral worse hearing = PRESBYCUSIS or LEFT SNHL from other cause.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Rinne POSITIVE bilaterally excludes conductive on both sides.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Weber to RIGHT = RIGHT is BETTER ear in SNHL. LEFT is affected.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Rinne positive bilaterally excludes conductive HL.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Answer is A.

questions.keyPoint: KEY RULE: RINNE POSITIVE BOTH SIDES = no conductive HL. WEBER to RIGHT = RIGHT is better ear in SNHL = LEFT ear is affected. Therefore: LEFT SENSORINEURAL HEARING LOSS.

questions.stem: Same 65-year-old woman as Q11. 12. Diagnosis of ear disease (2) — most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Otosclerosis: Rinne NEGATIVE (conductive). This patient has Rinne POSITIVE bilaterally = not otosclerosis. Page 140 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden SNHL = acute onset (<72h). This is GRADUAL hearing loss.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ossicular discontinuity = conductive HL (Rinne negative). Not this case.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PRESBYCUSIS (age-related SNHL): 65-year-old + gradual bilateral progressive HL + Rinne positive bilaterally (no conductive) + Weber to better ear = SNHL = PRESBYCUSIS. Most common cause of HL in the elderly (>60yo). Bilateral, symmetrical, high-frequency loss first. No cure — hearing aids effective.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Answer is D.

questions.keyPoint: PRESBYCUSIS: Most common cause of hearing loss in elderly (>60yo). Gradual, bilateral, progressive high-frequency SNHL. Rinne positive bilaterally. Audiogram: bilateral symmetric downsloping (high-freq) SNHL. Treatment: hearing aids (very effective). No preventive treatment available.

questions.stem: 60-YEAR-OLD CAUCASIAN WOMAN: new-onset dizziness when she turned her head. Room spinning. Tinnitus + otalgia + otorrhoea. Less severe episode 2 months ago. Afebrile, normal BP and RR. OTOSCOPIC ASSESSMENT WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS. 13. Dizziness — what will you do next?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CT unnecessary here — normal otoscopy + history suggesting BPPV. CT reserved for central pathology (focal neurological signs, direction-changing nystagmus, no latency).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MRI not first-line without red flags for central cause.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: DIX-HALLPIKE TEST = appropriate first step for suspected positional vertigo (turns head → room spins + normal otoscopy). Confirms BPPV and guides treatment (Epley's manoeuvre).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Betahistine is for Meniere's disease (episodic vertigo + SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness). Not first-line for BPPV.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Antiemetics/vestibular sedatives (cyclizine, prochlorperazine) treat symptoms but do not diagnose or cure BPPV.

questions.keyPoint: Suspected BPPV (positional vertigo + normal otoscopy + no neurological signs) → PERFORM POSITIONAL TESTING (Dix-Hallpike). If positive → Epley's manoeuvre. No imaging needed unless red flags (immediate nystagmus, direction-changing, neurological signs, headache).

questions.stem: 17-YEAR-OLD BOY: SERIOUS RTA, no seat belt, "bulls-eyed" windscreen. Major bruising to forehead + face. RIGHT EYE BADLY BRUISED. Blood trickling from right nostril. 15. Facial trauma — most appropriate statement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An orbital fracture may be responsible for the epistaxis
choices.explanation: TRUE: ORBITAL FRACTURES (medial wall or floor) can involve the maxillary sinus, and bleeding from the orbit/sinus draining into the nasal cavity CAN cause epistaxis. Periorbital haematoma + facial trauma + epistaxis → suspect orbital and/or midface (Le Fort) fracture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: CSF rhinorrhoea does not complicate facial injury
choices.explanation: CSF rhinorrhoea CAN occur with facial injury involving the anterior skull base (cribriform plate fracture) → must be tested for (glucose test, beta-2 transferrin).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Zygomatic fracture may narrow the oropharyngeal isthmus
choices.explanation: Zygomatic fracture → typically causes restricted mouth opening (trismus from zygomatic arch impinging on coronoid process), not specifically oropharyngeal narrowing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhalation injury should not be suspected
choices.explanation: In any RTA with facial trauma, airway burn/inhalation injury should be considered (especially if there was fire/smoke).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sensory loss involving the infraorbital nerve is unlikely
choices.explanation: Infraorbital nerve (V2) runs through the infraorbital foramen and is commonly affected by orbital floor fractures → infraorbital cheek numbness is a classic sign of orbital blowout fracture.

questions.keyPoint: ORBITAL FRACTURES can cause EPISTAXIS via blood draining from the orbital/sinus area into the nasal cavity. Also: infraorbital numbness (V2) is classic sign of orbital floor fracture. CSF rhinorrhoea from anterior skull base fracture must be excluded with glucose testing or beta-2 transferrin.

questions.stem: 40-YEAR-OLD MAN: progressively severe nasal obstruction + snoring at night + clear discharge + recurrent attacks of sinusitis. NO tenderness across paranasal sinuses on palpation. 16. Nasal obstruction — what will you do next?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MRI not first-line for nasal obstruction assessment. CT sinuses if imaging needed after clinical examination.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Allergy testing useful for suspected allergic rhinitis, but the first step is clinical examination of the nose.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Plain sinus X-rays largely obsolete. CT has replaced plain X-ray for sinus assessment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Examine the nose with a light and mirror
choices.explanation: ANTERIOR RHINOSCOPY (examination with light + mirror/speculum = Thudichum speculum) or NASENDOSCOPY is the NEXT STEP — direct visualisation of nasal cavity, turbinates, septum, and meatus. Identifies: polyps, septal deviation, hypertrophied turbinates, mucosal changes.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Rhinomanometry (objective nasal airflow measurement) is useful for research and pre/post-operative assessment, not the immediate next clinical step.

questions.keyPoint: NASAL OBSTRUCTION → EXAMINE THE NOSE FIRST (anterior rhinoscopy + rigid nasendoscopy). Identifies the cause: DNS, polyps, turbinate hypertrophy, mass. CT sinuses if pathology identified on examination. History + examination BEFORE investigations.

questions.stem: 50-YEAR-OLD ASIAN WOMAN: nose bleed persisting >10 minutes at home (pinching nose + towel). Friend applying ice pack to forehead. Previous attacks resolved spontaneously. Not in respiratory distress. BP 130/70, PR 100. PHYSICAL ASSESSMENT: ANTERIOR BLEEDING POINT visible. AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 143 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine 17. Epistaxis (1) — what is the next procedure to control the bleeding?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Anterior nasal packing = next step if cautery fails. An ANTERIOR BLEEDING POINT is visible → try cautery first.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Posterior packing = for posterior (invisible) epistaxis. This is ANTERIOR with a visible bleeding point.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SILVER NITRATE CAUTERY = treatment of choice for ANTERIOR EPISTAXIS with a VISIBLE BLEEDING POINT (Little's area). Apply directly to the bleeding vessel + surrounding mucosa after local anaesthesia (lidocaine + vasoconstrictor spray). Safe, effective, outpatient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arterial ligation of the sphenopalatine artery
choices.explanation: Sphenopalatine artery ligation = for POSTERIOR/REFRACTORY epistaxis not controllable with packing. Not for anterior visible bleeding point.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Answer is C.

questions.keyPoint: ANTERIOR EPISTAXIS + VISIBLE BLEEDING POINT (Little's area) = SILVER NITRATE CAUTERY. Do NOT cauterise both sides simultaneously (septal perforation risk). Contraindicated in children <12 years (use Naseptin cream instead). Next step if cautery fails: ANTERIOR NASAL PACKING.

questions.stem: 10-YEAR-OLD BOY: multiple episodes of MODERATELY SEVERE EPISTAXIS requiring hospital admission. Multiple "stuffy nose" episodes + headaches. Mother thinks he looks "puffy". No history of foreign body or nasal trauma. No easy bruising. 18. Epistaxis (2) — most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Leukaemia causes systemic symptoms, bruising, pallor, bone pain. No mention of these.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: JUVENILE NASAL ANGIOFIBROMA (JNA): Though usually 14-18 years, this presentation (recurrent profuse epistaxis + nasal obstruction + puffy appearance from venous congestion) in a young male is classic. JNA = adolescent males + recurrent profuse epistaxis + unilateral nasal obstruction. Puffy face = venous congestion.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Trauma to Little's area = minor anterior epistaxis, not hospitalisation-level recurrence. Page 144 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: llergic rhinitis → bilateral symptoms, sneezing, watery rhinorrhoea. Not recurrent pistaxis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: olyps = painless, grey, insensate bilateral masses. Not major recurrent epistaxis.

questions.keyPoint: JNA: Young MALE + recurrent PROFUSE EPISTAXIS + nasal obstruction. NO BIOPSY. CT + MRI + angiography for diagnosis. Pre-op embolisation → surgical excision. Androgen-sensitive (regresses after puberty in some). May occasionally present in younger boys (<14 years).

questions.stem: 33-YEAR-OLD AFRO-CARIBBEAN WOMAN: 4-day sore throat RIGHT SIDE (worse). Cannot swallow own saliva. Unwell, myalgia, arthralgia. Febrile 37.9°C, PR 100, BP 144/90, RR 12. ASYMMETRIC TONSILLAR ENLARGEMENT (right > left) with MARKED EXUDATE. UVULA DEVIATED TO THE LEFT. Bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. RELUCTANT TO OPEN MOUTH (trismus). 19. Sore throat (2) — which is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Oral amoxicillin insufficient for a peritonsillar abscess — cannot swallow (trismus + pain), needs parenteral antibiotics.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspiration of abscess in the emergency department
choices.explanation: BEST AVAILABLE ANSWER. Quinsy (peritonsillar abscess) is treated by DRAINAGE plus antibiotics. Needle aspiration in the ED is a recognised first-line drainage method — less invasive than transoral incision & drainage, done under local anaesthetic — and is the only drainage option offered, so it is the best of A–D. The fully ideal answer (transoral incision & drainage AND IV antibiotics, since the patient cannot swallow) is not listed as a single option. See flag. Ref: Bailey & Love; ENT UK peritonsillar abscess guidance.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mouthwash = adjunct. Not treatment for peritonsillar abscess.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regular oral analgesia and discharge to GP
choices.explanation: Peritonsillar abscess needs drainage + parenteral antibiotics. Discharge is inappropriate.

questions.keyPoint: PERITONSILLAR ABSCESS (Quinsy): Uvula deviation to OPPOSITE side + trismus + peritonsillar bulge. Treatment = TRANSORAL I&D; + IV ANTIBIOTICS (not oral amoxicillin — patient cannot swallow effectively). Of the options given, drainage (needle aspiration — option B) is the best answer; ideally combined with IV antibiotics.

flag.note: Flawed option set: the ideal management (transoral I&D + IV antibiotics) is not offered as a single option. Best available answer marked = B (aspiration/drainage in ED). Ref: Bailey & Love; ENT UK.

questions.stem: 68-YEAR-OLD CAUCASIAN MAN: 3-week FIRM IRREGULAR SWELLING at angle of right jaw. Lethargic + weight loss over few months. Physical: 4×3 cm FIRM IRREGULAR SWELLING from right parotid gland. ASYMMETRIC SMILE + UNABLE TO PURSE LIPS (facial nerve involvement). 20. Parotid swelling — most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pleomorphic adenoma = most common parotid tumour (70%), but PAINLESS + SLOW-GROWING + NO facial nerve involvement (malignancy sign = CN VII palsy).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Warthin's = bilateral, elderly males, smokers, PAINLESS, NO CN VII involvement.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PAROTID CARCINOMA: FIRM + IRREGULAR + RAPID GROWTH + WEIGHT LOSS + FACIAL NERVE PALSY (CN VII involvement = PATHOGNOMONIC of MALIGNANCY in parotid tumours). ASYMMETRIC SMILE + cannot purse lips = lower facial nerve involvement (buccal + marginal mandibular branches). Investigation: MRI + FNAC. Treatment: parotidectomy + neck dissection + radiotherapy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Calculus = intermittent swelling with eating (Chvostek's sign equivalent). No facial nerve involvement.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Parotid carcinoma is the answer.

questions.keyPoint: PAROTID TUMOUR + FACIAL NERVE PALSY = MALIGNANCY (parotid carcinoma). CN VII involvement in a parotid mass = PATHOGNOMONIC of MALIGNANT parotid tumour. Benign tumours (pleomorphic adenoma, Warthin's) do NOT involve the facial nerve. Most common malignant = mucoepidermoid carcinoma.

questions.stem: 70-YEAR-OLD GENTLEMAN: gradual visual deterioration, admitted for surgery. SLIT LAMP PHOTOGRAPHY shows dense opacity of the lens (cataract). The true statement about his condition is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is a need to implant an intraocular lens
choices.explanation: TRUE: Cataract surgery (phacoemulsification) involves REMOVAL of the cloudy natural lens followed by IMPLANTATION of an INTRAOCULAR LENS (IOL). Without IOL implantation, the patient would be left aphakic (high hypermetropia requiring thick glasses/contact lenses).

choices.label: B
choices.text: History of trauma is not relevant
choices.explanation: TRAUMATIC CATARACT is a recognised cause (penetrating injury, blunt trauma). History IS relevant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: His condition is considered very early
choices.explanation: If admitted for surgery and slit lamp shows clear opacity → NOT early stage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He could be managed by topical medication as an alternative to surgery
choices.explanation: There is NO proven effective topical medication to reverse or halt cataract progression. SURGERY is the only effective treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The commonest cause for his problem is steroid use
choices.explanation: Age-related (senile) cataract is BY FAR the most common cause overall. Steroid-induced cataract = posterior subcapsular type, important risk factor but NOT the most common.

questions.keyPoint: CATARACT SURGERY: IOL IMPLANTATION is mandatory after phacoemulsification to replace the optical function of the removed lens. No effective topical treatment. Most common cause = AGE-RELATED (senile). Types: nuclear sclerosis (common), posterior subcapsular (steroids), anterior subcapsular.

questions.stem: Same cataract patient. Best option for treatment of cataract is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cataract removal by intracapsular cataract procedure
choices.explanation: ICCE (intracapsular) = OUTDATED. Removes entire lens + capsule. High complication rate. Replaced by ECCE then phacoemulsification.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LASIK corrects refractive errors (myopia, hypermetropia, astigmatism) by corneal reshaping. It does NOT treat cataract (a lens problem).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: No topical medication treats cataract.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Glasses correct refractive error from cataract but do not remove it. Not a treatment — a workaround for early stages only.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PHACOEMULSIFICATION = GOLD STANDARD modern cataract surgery. Ultrasound probe emulsifies the lens nucleus → aspiration → IOL implanted in posterior capsule. Small incision (2.2-2.8mm) → rapid recovery. >90% success.

questions.keyPoint: PHACOEMULSIFICATION = GOLD STANDARD for cataract surgery. Small incision (self-sealing), ultrasonic emulsification, IOL implantation in posterior chamber. Replaced ICCE (outdated) and ECCE (larger incision). Vision recovery in days.

questions.stem: 45-YEAR-OLD MALE with HIGH INTRAOCULAR PRESSURE. Optic disc image shows INCREASED CUP-TO-DISC RATIO (cupping). Identify the FALSE statement. Identify the false statement about his condition:

choices.label: A
choices.text: OCT is a valuable imaging tool
choices.explanation: TRUE — OCT is valuable for RNFL (retinal nerve fibre layer) assessment in glaucoma. Page 148 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: FALSE — HIGH IOP + OPTIC DISC CUPPING = GLAUCOMA requires treatment to lower IOP and prevent optic nerve damage. "No need for treatment" is the FALSE statement (incorrect claim about glaucoma management).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The anterior chamber angle could be open or closed
choices.explanation: TRUE — increased IOP can be from open-angle or closed-angle glaucoma. Image alone cannot always distinguish.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The other eye should be checked
choices.explanation: TRUE — glaucoma often bilateral; other eye must be assessed.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perimetry is needed to assess the visual field
choices.explanation: TRUE — visual field testing (perimetry) is essential for glaucoma assessment.

questions.keyPoint: GLAUCOMA with HIGH IOP + OPTIC DISC CUPPING = ALWAYS requires treatment. "No need for treatment" = FALSE. Treatment: topical beta-blockers (timolol) → add prostaglandin analogues → laser → surgery. Goal = lower IOP to prevent progressive optic nerve damage.

questions.stem: Same POAG patient. How is glaucoma treated?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Medicine (topical drops) alone is incomplete answer.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Laser alone is incomplete.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Options A and B are both used.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All of the above (medicine + laser + surgery)
choices.explanation: ALL THREE treatment modalities are used in glaucoma management, chosen based on severity, disease progression, and patient response: (1) MEDICAL (eye drops: prostaglandins, beta-blockers, CAIs), (2) LASER (SLT or ALT), (3) SURGERY (trabeculectomy, tube shunt, MIGS). Stepped approach.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Surgery alone is incomplete.

questions.keyPoint: GLAUCOMA TREATMENT = STEPPED APPROACH: (1) Topical drops (prostaglandins first-line), (2) Laser (SLT), (3) Surgery (trabeculectomy/tube shunt). All three modalities used depending on severity. Goal: lower IOP to preserve optic nerve and visual field.

questions.stem: 46-YEAR-OLD DIABETIC PATIENT: deterioration of vision over last 2 years. Fundoscopy shows specific features. Identify the sign that is NOT PRESENT in background DR. Identify the sign that is NOT present (in background diabetic retinopathy):

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: RETINAL DETACHMENT is NOT a feature of BACKGROUND (non-proliferative) DR. It occurs in PROLIFERATIVE DR (traction from fibrovascular membranes on new vessels). Background DR = microaneurysms + dot haemorrhages + hard exudates + cotton wool spots (ischaemic infarcts).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: This is the diagnosis — it IS present.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hard exudates (lipid deposits from leaking vessels) ARE present in background DR.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cotton wool spots (soft exudates = nerve fibre layer infarcts) ARE present in background DR.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Dot haemorrhages (from microaneurysm rupture) ARE present in background DR.

questions.keyPoint: BACKGROUND (NON-PROLIFERATIVE) DR features: Microaneurysms + Dot haemorrhages + Hard exudates + Cotton wool spots. NOT present: Neovascularisation, retinal detachment (these = PROLIFERATIVE DR). Macular oedema can occur in background DR (main cause of visual loss in NPDR).

questions.stem: 35-YEAR-OLD MAN WITH ANKYLOSING SPONDYLITIS: red eye + photophobia. Slit lamp shows collection of cells in anterior chamber. What is the correct statement about his eye condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The anterior chamber sign is called hypopyon
choices.explanation: HYPOPYON = LAYERED COLLECTION OF WHITE CELLS (WBCs/neutrophils) in the INFERIOR ANTERIOR CHAMBER. In anterior uveitis, inflammatory cells collect in the AC → in severe cases → hypopyon (white layering at the bottom of the AC). The file marks A as correct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The anterior chamber sign is called hyphema
choices.explanation: HYPHEMA = RBCs (blood) in the anterior chamber. The anterior chamber finding in uveitis is CELLS/FLARE or HYPOPYON (white cells), NOT hyphema (red cells).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The redness is due to a microbial infection
choices.explanation: Anterior uveitis in AS is AUTOIMMUNE (HLA-B27 associated), not microbial infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pupil size and shape is never affected
choices.explanation: POSTERIOR SYNECHIAE (iris-lens adhesions) DO affect pupil shape (irregular pupil).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TOPICAL STEROIDS are the MAINSTAY of treatment for anterior uveitis.

questions.keyPoint: ANTERIOR UVEITIS (HLA-B27, AS): Cells and flare in AC (slit lamp) + ciliary injection + SMALL MIOTIC pupil (ciliary spasm) + photophobia. Severe → HYPOPYON (white cell layering in inferior AC). Treatment: TOPICAL STEROIDS + cycloplegics (dilate pupil to prevent synechiae).

questions.stem: Diabetic Retinopathy — ALL ARE TRUE EXCEPT: Diabetic Retinopathy. All are TRUE EXCEPT: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 151 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.text: DR is one of the common causes of blindness among children with type I diabetes
choices.explanation: DR is primarily a disease of adults with LONG-STANDING diabetes. Children with type I DM rarely develop significant retinopathy (DR takes years to develop from diagnosis). The file marks A as having a pink border = the wrong/EXCEPT answer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: VEGF stimulates endothelial growth causing new vessel formation and vascular permeability
choices.explanation: TRUE — VEGF is the key driver of neovascularisation and increased permeability in DR.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pregnant women with proliferative DR do poorly without treatment but those who had prior panretinal photocoagulatio n rem
choices.explanation: FILE marks C as the EXCEPT: "women who had prior PRP remain stable" is debated. In reality, DR can progress in pregnancy even with prior PRP. The file marks C in a red border = the FALSE statement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No clinically significant retinopathy in first 5 years of type I diabetes
choices.explanation: TRUE — retinopathy rarely develops in the first 5 years of type I DM. Risk increases steeply after 5+ years.

choices.label: E
choices.text: In type II DM, incidence of DR increases with disease duration
choices.explanation: TRUE — DR is directly related to duration of diabetes.

questions.keyPoint: Proliferative DR can WORSEN in pregnancy even with prior panretinal photocoagulation (PRP). Prior PRP reduces (but doesn't eliminate) the risk. VEGF-mediated neovascularisation is key in PDR. Duration of diabetes = strongest risk factor for DR development.

questions.stem: Dry eye disease — increased tear osmolarity — all correct EXCEPT: In dry eye diseases, increased tear osmolarity — all are correct EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TRUE — wider fissure increases exposed cornea surface → MORE tear evaporation → more dry eye.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: TRUE — meibomian gland dysfunction → reduced lipid layer → increased evaporation → dry eye.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PUNCTAL STENOSIS (blocked punctum) would DECREASE tear drainage → INCREASE tear film on the eye → DECREASED tear osmolarity (more dilute). It does NOT increase tear osmolarity. Therefore this is the EXCEPT (does NOT cause increased osmolarity).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TRUE — decreased corneal sensation (in diabetic neuropathy, herpes, CL wear) → reduced blink reflex → reduced tear replenishment → increased evaporation → increased osmolarity.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TRUE — OCP scarring → goblet cell loss → reduced mucin → unstable tear film → increased osmolarity.

questions.keyPoint: PUNCTAL STENOSIS: Blocked lacrimal punctum → REDUCES tear DRAINAGE → tears accumulate on eye → LOWER osmolarity (EPIPHORA, not dry eye). All other options (wide fissure, meibomitis, reduced corneal sensation, OCP) INCREASE tear osmolarity → dry eye. Dry eye treatment: artificial tears → cyclosporin → plugs (paradoxically used for dry eye to retain tears).

questions.stem: Treatment of patient with congenital esotropia. Treatment of patient with congenital esotropia:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Prism glasses can reduce the angle of deviation but do not correct congenital esotropia definitively.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lateral rectus RECESSION would WEAKEN the ABDUCTORS → worsen esotropia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Orthoptic exercises (for fusional vergence) not effective for CONGENITAL esotropia with large angle.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Incorrect muscle — lateral rectus recession would worsen convergence. Page 153 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: BILATERAL MEDIAL RECTUS RECESSION = treatment for congenital (infantile) esotropia. Weakening both MEDIAL RECTI (the converging muscles) REDUCES the inward pull → reduces angle of deviation. Early surgery (by 1-2 years) allows binocular vision development.

questions.keyPoint: CONGENITAL ESOTROPIA treatment: BILATERAL MEDIAL RECTUS RECESSION (weaken the converging muscles). Timing: early (1-2 years) for binocular vision development. Accommodative esotropia → glasses (optical correction). Surgery for NON-ACCOMMODATIVE/congenital large-angle esotropia.

questions.stem: Blowout fracture of orbit commonly produces: Blow out fracture of orbit commonly produces:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Nasal bone fractures are from direct nasal trauma. Blowout mechanism → intraorbital pressure → floor/medial wall.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Frontal bone is thick and strong. Not commonly fractured in blowout.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: FLOOR OF ORBIT (inferior wall) = MOST COMMONLY FRACTURED in blowout. Thinnest wall over maxillary sinus. Inferior rectus entrapment → vertical diplopia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Retinal haemorrhage can occur with severe eye trauma but is NOT what a blowout fracture "commonly produces".

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Septal deviation from direct nasal trauma. Not from orbital blowout.

questions.keyPoint: BLOWOUT FRACTURE → FLOOR OF ORBIT (most common) → inferior rectus entrapment → VERTICAL DIPLOPIA + restricted upgaze. CT coronal = gold standard for assessment. Instruction: NO NOSE-BLOWING. Surgery if persistent diplopia or enophthalmos >2mm.

questions.stem: Choroidal melanomas and Metastatic Tumors vary. Which statement is FALSE? Choroidal melanomas and Metastatic Tumors vary in several ways. Which of the following statements is FALSE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Treatment for metastatic tumors and choroidal melanomas is the same
choices.explanation: FALSE? Actually treatments DO differ: melanoma → brachytherapy/proton beam/enucleation. Metastatic → treat primary + systemic treatment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They differ in age of presentation
choices.explanation: TRUE — melanoma typically adults >50; metastatic tumors can occur in breast/lung cancer patients (various ages).

choices.label: C
choices.text: B-scan shows Metastatic Tumor as high internal reflectivity while choroidal melanoma has low
choices.explanation: TRUE — Metastatic: HIGH internal reflectivity on B-scan. Melanoma: LOW internal reflectivity (characteristic acoustic quiet zone = "collar-stud" lesion sometimes).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Metastatic tumors are usually multifocal and multilobular while choroidal melanomas are singular
choices.explanation: TRUE — metastatic = often multiple/bilateral; melanoma = usually solitary/unilateral.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both choroidal melanomas and Metastatic Tumors are commonly bilateral
choices.explanation: FALSE: Choroidal melanoma = USUALLY UNILATERAL. Metastatic tumors = more commonly BILATERAL/multiple. Therefore "BOTH are commonly bilateral" is FALSE. File marks E as the false statement.

questions.keyPoint: CHOROIDAL MELANOMA = usually UNILATERAL (solitary). METASTATIC tumours = more commonly BILATERAL/multiple (breast + lung cancer most common primary sites). B-scan: melanoma = low internal reflectivity (acoustic quiet zone); metastatic = high internal reflectivity.

questions.stem: Mastoiditis question. Mastoiditis is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Usually associated with subtotal perforation of tympanic membrane
choices.explanation: Mastoiditis associated with TOTAL or LARGE central perforation in CSOM (or even intact TM in early/masked mastoiditis).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive fistula test is early sign
choices.explanation: Positive fistula test indicates LABYRINTHINE FISTULA (a LATE complication of cholesteatoma/CSOM), not early mastoiditis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: IV antibiotics FIRST. Cortical mastoidectomy only if not responding to antibiotics or complications present.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Maximum area to elicit tenderness is over the tragus
choices.explanation: Tragus tenderness = otitis externa. Mastoiditis tenderness = over the MASTOID PROCESS (post-auricular area).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TRUE: Mastoiditis is MOST COMMON in INFANTS AND CHILDREN (peak incidence in children <2 years). This is because: (1) children have more ASOM, (2) pneumatisation of mastoid not yet complete, (3) Eustachian tube anatomy, (4) immature immune system.

questions.keyPoint: MASTOIDITIS most common in INFANTS AND CHILDREN (peak <2 years). Tenderness over MASTOID PROCESS (not tragus). Treatment: IV antibiotics first → cortical mastoidectomy if not improving. Positive fistula test = LABYRINTHINE fistula (late complication).

questions.stem: All are recognized causes for entropion EXCEPT: All the following are recognized causes for entropion EXCEPT: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 156 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Trachoma IS a cause of CICATRICIAL entropion (scarring of the palpebral conjunctiva → posterior lamella shortening → lid margin turns inward).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Spastic entropion (from orbicularis spasm) IS a recognised cause (seen in elderly with eye irritation).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Congenital entropion IS a recognised type.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Involutional entropion (from lid laxity in elderly) IS the most common type.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: FACIAL NERVE PALSY causes ECTROPION (not entropion). CN VII palsy → orbicularis weakness → lid falls away from eyeball (ectropion). ENTROPION = lid turns inward. ECTROPION = lid turns outward. These are opposites.

questions.keyPoint: FACIAL NERVE PALSY → ECTROPION (lid turns OUTWARD) because orbicularis oculi is WEAKENED → cannot hold lid against globe. ENTROPION causes: trachoma, orbicularis spasm, senile laxity, congenital. ECTROPION causes: CN VII palsy, senile laxity (also), cicatricial.

questions.stem: YOUNG CHILD: suffering from fever + sore throat. Began to complain of lacrimation. FOLLICLES in lower palpebral conjunctiva with TENDER PREAURICULAR LYMPH NODES. The most probable diagnosis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ADENOVIRAL CONJUNCTIVITIS: FOLLICLES in lower palpebral conjunctiva + PREAURICULAR LYMPH NODES (tender) + preceding URTI (fever + sore throat). Classic presentation. Highly contagious (epidemic keratoconjunctivitis or pharyngoconjunctival fever). Self-limiting ~2 weeks. No specific antiviral treatment.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Trachoma: chronic chlamydial infection → superior tarsal conjunctival follicles + Herbert's pits + pannus. NOT preceded by acute URTI.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Allergic: bilateral itching + papillae (not follicles) + watery discharge. No preauricular LN. Seasonal/perennial.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Gonococcal: HYPERACUTE (profuse purulent discharge, massive chemosis). Medical emergency.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Staphylococcal: mucopurulent discharge + lid margin crusting. No follicles or preauricular LN. Page 157 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: ADENOVIRAL CONJUNCTIVITIS: FOLLICLES (lymphoid hyperplasia) in lower palpebral + TENDER PREAURICULAR LN + preceding URTI. PAPILLAE = allergic. FOLLICLES = viral (adenovirus, EBV) or chlamydial. Adenoviral = most common infective conjunctivitis. Highly contagious.

questions.stem: Type of laser used in photorefractive keratectomy (PRK). Type of laser that is used in photorefractive keratectomy (PRK):

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Krypton laser = retinal photocoagulation (red wavelength).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Argon laser = retinal photocoagulation (for diabetic retinopathy, retinal tears), trabeculoplasty.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CO2 laser = removal of warts, papillomas on skin. Not for corneal refractive surgery.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: EXCIMER LASER (ultraviolet, 193 nm) = used in PRK and LASIK. UV laser ablates corneal tissue with extreme precision (photoablation), reshaping the corneal curvature to correct refractive error. No thermal damage to surrounding tissue.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: YAG laser = posterior capsulotomy (after cataract surgery), laser iridotomy (AACG). Not for refractive surgery.

questions.keyPoint: EXCIMER LASER (193 nm UV) = PRK and LASIK. ARGON laser = retinal photocoagulation + trabeculoplasty. YAG laser = posterior capsulotomy + laser iridotomy. CO2 laser = warts/papillomas. Remember: laser uses by wavelength and application.

questions.stem: MOTHER with her 9-YEAR-OLD SON consulting an ophthalmic clinic for his CONSTANTLY ABNORMAL-LOOKING EYES. Photo shows right upper eyelid drooping covering the pupil. What is the abnormality in his eyes?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pseudoptosis = apparent ptosis from other cause (e.g., smaller right eye from enophthalmos or microphthalmos giving impression of drooping lid). The image shows TRUE drooping of the upper lid.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The 9-year-old has RIGHT PTOSIS — the upper eyelid of the right eye droops, partially covering the pupil. This is the correct term for drooping of the upper eyelid.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: No inward deviation of the left eye is described.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lid retraction = lid too high, exposing the sclera above the limbus. Not what is described.

questions.keyPoint: PTOSIS = DROOPING OF THE UPPER EYELID. RIGHT ptosis in a child = congenital until proven otherwise (levator palpebrae superioris dysgenesis). Most common CAUSE in children = CONGENITAL. Must exclude: CN III palsy (dilated pupil + ophthalmoplegia), Horner syndrome (miosis + anhidrosis + enophthalmos).

questions.stem: Same 9-year-old with right ptosis. What is the most common cause for this abnormality? (*Related to previous scenario)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CONGENITAL PTOSIS is the most common cause of ptosis in CHILDREN (and in this age group). Caused by levator palpebrae superioris muscle dysgenesis. Key feature: absent eyelid crease. Unilateral in most cases. Risk of deprivation amblyopia if covering the visual axis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN III palsy in children: ptosis + exotropia + dilated pupil + ophthalmoplegia. These features not described here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diabetic CN III palsy = adult, painful, pupil SPARING. Not a paediatric cause of congenital ptosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Traumatic ptosis: history of trauma required. Absent eyelid crease since birth = congenital, not traumatic.

questions.keyPoint: MOST COMMON CAUSE of PAEDIATRIC PTOSIS = CONGENITAL (levator dysgenesis). Absent eyelid crease + present from birth = pathognomonic. CN III palsy in children (rarer) = ptosis + dilated pupil + ophthalmoplegia. Horner's in children = ptosis + miosis + heterochromia iridis.

questions.stem: Same 9-year-old with right congenital ptosis. What treatment options are involved? (*Related)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Glasses can correct refractive error but do NOT treat ptosis. If amblyopia present, glasses to correct refractive error + patching are used, but the ptosis itself requires surgery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medication to improve levator palpebrae superioris strength
choices.explanation: No medication reliably strengthens the dysgenetic levator muscle.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LEVATOR RESECTION SURGERY = definitive treatment for congenital ptosis with ADEQUATE LEVATOR FUNCTION (>4mm). The levator is shortened and reattached to the tarsal plate → raises the upper eyelid. If LF <4mm → FRONTALIS SLING. At 9 years old: amblyopia risk still present if not already treated.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Medial rectus recession = for esotropia (squint surgery). Not for ptosis.

questions.keyPoint: CONGENITAL PTOSIS TREATMENT: LEVATOR RESECTION (if LF >4mm) or FRONTALIS SLING (if LF <4mm). At age 9, critical period has largely passed but amblyopia treatment should still be continued if VA is reducible. Surgery for cosmesis + prevent further suppression.

questions.stem: Davidson's Chapter 13 Single Best Answer Questions. 1. Which of the following drugs is known to cause visual impairment as a side effect?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CHLOROQUINE/HYDROXYCHLOROQUINE causes RETINAL TOXICITY (bull's eye maculopathy) → bilateral progressive visual field loss. Risk increases with cumulative dose and duration. Requires regular ophthalmic screening.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Gentamicin = ototoxic (cochlear/vestibular) not typically retinotoxic.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Thiazide diuretics rarely cause acute angle closure glaucoma (rare) but not a classic direct cause of visual impairment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Metformin not known to cause visual impairment. May be used in Meniere's but not ophthalmically.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Statins rarely associated with optic neuropathy but not a classic cause of visual impairment.

questions.keyPoint: CHLOROQUINE/HYDROXYCHLOROQUINE: BULL'S EYE MACULOPATHY → visual field loss. Monitor with regular ophthalmic review (Humphrey visual field + OCT). Risk: cumulative dose >5mg/kg/day hydroxychloroquine. Used for RA, SLE, malaria prophylaxis.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 2. Which of the listed extraocular muscles is correctly paired with the cranial nerve that innervates it?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CN III (OCULOMOTOR) innervates: Superior rectus, Inferior rectus, Medial rectus, Inferior oblique, Levator palpebrae superioris. CN IV = Superior oblique (SO4). CN VI = Lateral rectus (LR6). Mnemonic: LR6SO4, the rest are III.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Inferior oblique = CN III. Superior oblique = CN IV.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Lateral rectus = CN VI. CN IV = superior oblique.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Medial rectus = CN III. CN VI = lateral rectus. Page 161 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Superior oblique = CN IV. Mnemonic SO4.

questions.keyPoint: EXTRAOCULAR MUSCLE INNERVATION: CN VI = Lateral Rectus (LR6). CN IV = Superior Oblique

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 3. Which of the following statements is true of refractive errors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Myopia is treated with convex lenses
choices.explanation: Myopia (short-sighted) = CONCAVE (MINUS) lenses. Hypermetropia = CONVEX (PLUS) lenses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Astigmatism is not correctable with lenses
choices.explanation: Astigmatism IS correctable with CYLINDRICAL lenses (or toric contact lenses/IOLs).

choices.label: C
choices.text: In hypermetropia, near vision is better than long-distance vision
choices.explanation: In hypermetropia, NEAR VISION IS WORSE (requires more accommodation for near). Young hypermetropes can accommodate but eventually near vision degrades. Distance vision usually better.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Presbyopia is the progressive development of short-sightedne ss with age
choices.explanation: Presbyopia = progressive loss of ACCOMMODATION with age (loss of elasticity of lens) → difficulty reading. NOT progressive short-sightedness.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypermetropia is a cause of childhood squint
choices.explanation: TRUE: HYPERMETROPIA causes ACCOMMODATIVE ESOTROPIA. The hypermetropic child must accommodate excessively to see clearly → accommodation-convergence reflex → convergent squint (esotropia). Correction with plus lenses = resolves squint.

questions.keyPoint: HYPERMETROPIA → ACCOMMODATIVE ESOTROPIA in children. Myopia = concave lens. Hypermetropia = convex lens. Astigmatism = cylindrical lens. Presbyopia = loss of accommodation with age (reading glasses needed). SO3 = Squint, Headache, Ocular fatigue = symptoms of uncorrected refractive error.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 4. Which of the following is a risk factor for open-angle glaucoma?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: AFRICAN CARIBBEAN origin = higher risk for glaucoma. Caucasian is NOT a specific RF.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cataract is not an independent RF for open-angle glaucoma. Mature cataract can CAUSE secondary glaucoma (phacolytic) but is not an RF for PRIMARY open-angle glaucoma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hypermetropia = RF for ACUTE ANGLE-CLOSURE glaucoma (narrow anterior chamber angle). Not primary OAG.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: DIABETES MELLITUS is a recognised RISK FACTOR for PRIMARY OPEN-ANGLE GLAUCOMA. Mechanism: basement membrane changes + increased aqueous outflow resistance + oxidative damage to trabecular meshwork.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: OLDER AGE (not young age) is a RF for POAG.

questions.keyPoint: POAG RISK FACTORS: Older age + Black African-Caribbean ethnicity + Family history (1st degree relative) + ELEVATED IOP + DIABETES + Myopia + Thin central cornea. Hypermetropia = RF for AACG, NOT POAG.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 5. Causes of gradual visual loss include: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 163 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Retinal artery embolism = SUDDEN (seconds) painless visual loss.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Conjunctivitis = red eye with discharge. NOT significant visual loss.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Vitreous haemorrhage = SUDDEN visual loss (floaters → blurring → blackout).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: GCA = SUDDEN painless visual loss from anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: AGE-RELATED MACULAR DEGENERATION (AMD) = GRADUAL visual loss (central vision, particularly for fine detail). Dry AMD = slow progression. Wet AMD = can be sudden if choroidal neovascularisation → haemorrhage.

questions.keyPoint: GRADUAL visual loss: AMD + cataracts + glaucoma + diabetic retinopathy + POAG. SUDDEN painless visual loss: retinal artery occlusion + retinal vein occlusion + GCA + vitreous haemorrhage + retinal detachment. SUDDEN PAINFUL visual loss: AACG + orbital cellulitis.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 6. Medical treatment for primary open-angle glaucoma includes:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Steroid eye drops INCREASE IOP (steroid response) → worsen glaucoma. Contraindicated.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Tropicamide = MYDRIATIC (dilates pupil). Risks triggering AACG in susceptible patients. Contraindicated in narrow angle.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TIMOLOL (beta-blocker, topical) = once cornerstone of POAG treatment (though now prostaglandin analogues often used first). Reduces aqueous production. Systemic absorption can cause bradycardia, bronchospasm (contraindicated in asthma).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Chloramphenicol = antibiotic (for bacterial conjunctivitis). Not for glaucoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Fluorescein = diagnostic dye for corneal staining. Not treatment.

questions.keyPoint: POAG MEDICAL TREATMENT: (1) Prostaglandin analogues (latanoprost = first-line now), (2) Beta-blockers (timolol — avoid in asthma/COPD), (3) Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (dorzolamide drops, acetazolamide tablets), (4) Alpha-2 agonists (brimonidine), (5) Miotics (pilocarpine). Goal: lower IOP.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 7. Which of the following visual field defects is paired correctly with the anatomical site of the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left optic tract — left monocular blindness
choices.explanation: Left optic TRACT lesion = RIGHT HOMONYMOUS HEMIANOPIA (affects nasal fibres from right eye + temporal from left eye = right visual field from both eyes).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Optic chiasm = BITEMPORAL HEMIANOPIA (crossing nasal fibres from both eyes are damaged).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MACULAR LESION = CENTRAL SCOTOMA (loss of central vision). The macula represents central visual field. Causes: AMD, macular hole, toxic (chloroquine), macular oedema.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left occipital cortex — right monocular blindness
choices.explanation: Occipital cortex = HOMONYMOUS HEMIANOPIA (with macular sparing if peripheral lesion). NOT monocular.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left optic nerve — right homonymous hemianopia
choices.explanation: LEFT optic nerve lesion = LEFT MONOCULAR BLINDNESS. Homonymous hemianopia = optic tract/radiation/cortex lesion.

questions.keyPoint: VISUAL FIELD DEFECTS: Monocular blindness = optic nerve. Bitemporal hemianopia = optic chiasm (pituitary). Homonymous hemianopia = optic tract/radiation/cortex. CENTRAL SCOTOMA = macular lesion (AMD, macular hole, toxic). Arcuate scotoma = glaucoma.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 8. Which of the following fundoscopy findings is a key feature of grade 4 hypertensive retinopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cotton wool spots = Grade 3 hypertensive retinopathy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Silver wiring = Grade 2 hypertensive retinopathy (more severe than copper wiring).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Flame haemorrhages = Grade 3 hypertensive retinopathy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PAPILLOEDEMA (swelling of the optic disc) = GRADE 4 HYPERTENSIVE RETINOPATHY = MALIGNANT HYPERTENSION. This is a medical emergency requiring urgent blood pressure control.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: AV nipping = Grade 2 hypertensive retinopathy.

questions.keyPoint: HYPERTENSIVE RETINOPATHY GRADES (Keith-Wagener-Barker): Grade 1 = copper wiring. Grade 2 = AV nipping + silver wiring. Grade 3 = flame haemorrhages + cotton wool spots + hard exudates. Grade 4 = PAPILLOEDEMA (malignant hypertension). Grade 4 = EMERGENCY.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 9. Myopia (short-sightedness) is a risk factor for which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HIGH MYOPIA = significant risk factor for RETINAL DETACHMENT. Reason: longer axial length → thinner, more stretched retina → increased risk of peripheral retinal degeneration (lattice degeneration) → retinal tears → retinal detachment.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Closed-angle glaucoma = HYPERMETROPIA (shallow anterior chamber from shorter axial length). Myopia = long eye = deep anterior chamber = LOWER risk of angle closure.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Optic neuritis is related to demyelinating disease (MS) and is not associated with myopia. Page 166 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: piscleritis = idiopathic or associated with systemic inflammatory diseases. Not associated ith myopia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: orneal ulceration from contact lenses (common in myopes) could be argued, but retinal etachment is the classical association.

questions.keyPoint: HIGH MYOPIA (>6 dioptres) = risk factor for RETINAL DETACHMENT + POAG + CATARACT + Posterior staphyloma + Lattice degeneration + Macular degeneration (myopic maculopathy). High myopes should have regular retinal examination.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 10. Which of the following predisposes to cataract formation?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SYSTEMIC (AND TOPICAL) CORTICOSTEROIDS cause POSTERIOR SUBCAPSULAR CATARACT (PSC). Long-term steroid use → PSC which causes GLARE and vision problems, particularly in bright light (visual axis involved when pupil constricts).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bacterial conjunctivitis does not cause cataract.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hypertension not a direct cause of cataract (though may be associated with poor metabolic control in diabetics).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Retinal detachment not a cause of cataract (though may complicate surgery).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Papilloedema does not cause cataract.

questions.keyPoint: CATARACT CAUSES: SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS → POSTERIOR SUBCAPSULAR cataract (PSC). Also: Age (nuclear sclerosis, most common overall), Diabetes (nuclear/PSC), UV radiation, Trauma (sub-capsular), Galactosaemia (oil droplet cataract), Down syndrome, Myotonic dystrophy, Wilson's disease.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 11. Which of the following is a cause of dilated pupils?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Opioid overdose = MIOSIS (pinpoint pupils). Classic "pinpoint pupils" in opioid toxicity.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BRAIN DEATH → FIXED DILATED PUPILS (midriasis). Loss of all brainstem reflexes including pupillary light reflex → fixed, dilated, non-reactive pupils. Also: herniation (Cushing response → blown pupil from CN III compression by uncal herniation).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Horner's = MIOSIS (ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis). Loss of sympathetic → pupil constricts.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PONTINE HAEMORRHAGE = PINPOINT PUPILS (bilateral) from sympathetic pathway destruction in the pons.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pilocarpine = MIOTIC drug (used for glaucoma and Adie's pupil diagnosis). Constricts the pupil.

questions.keyPoint: DILATED PUPILS: Brain death + CN III palsy (uncal herniation) + anticholinergics (atropine) + sympathomimetics + Adie's pupil + mydriatic drops. MIOSIS: Opioids + pontine lesions + Horner's + pilocarpine + organophosphate poisoning.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 12. Causes of acute visual loss include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: KCS (dry eye) = gradual, chronic visual disturbance. Not acute visual loss.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CENTRAL RETINAL VEIN OCCLUSION (CRVO) and BRANCH RETINAL VEIN OCCLUSION (BRVO) = ACUTE (or rapid) visual loss. CRVO: sudden painless visual loss + flame haemorrhages in all 4 quadrants + disc swelling + "stormy sunset" fundus. Page 168 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MD = GRADUAL visual loss (dry type). Wet AMD can cause sudden loss if acute aemorrhage, but typically gradual.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ataract = GRADUAL progressive visual decline.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: hyroid eye disease causes proptosis + diplopia. Visual loss (compressive optic europathy) can occur but is not the typical "acute visual loss" cause in standard teaching.

questions.keyPoint: ACUTE VISUAL LOSS causes: CRAO + CRVO + Vitreous haemorrhage + Retinal detachment + GCA (AION) + AACG + Optic neuritis. CRVO = sudden painless VA loss + "stormy sunset" fundus (flame haemorrhages all 4 quadrants). Treatment: lower IOP + anti-VEGF for macular oedema.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 13. Which of the following is NOT a recognized ocular complication of diabetes mellitus?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Blepharitis (lid margin inflammation) is more common in diabetics. A recognised association.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN VI PALSY is NOT a typical DM-related ocular complication. DM more commonly causes CN III palsy (pupil-SPARING because ischaemia affects the core fascicles rather than outer pupillomotor fibres). CN VI palsy (lateral rectus) is not the classic DM cranial nerve complication.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: POAG is more common in diabetics — a recognised association.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Traction retinal detachment from proliferative DR IS a recognised complication.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vitreous haemorrhage from new vessels in PDR IS a recognised complication.

questions.keyPoint: DM OCULAR COMPLICATIONS: DR (the main one) + Cataract + POAG + Rubeotic glaucoma + CN III palsy (pupil-SPARING) + Corneal epithelial abnormalities. CN VI palsy is NOT the typical DM cranial nerve complication. DM CN palsy = CN III (pupil sparing = ischaemic, surgical = pupil involved).

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 14. Which of the following is NOT a cause of papilloedema?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CST raises intracranial pressure → papilloedema.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Raised ICP IS the mechanism of papilloedema.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: AMD does NOT cause papilloedema. AMD = macular disease (photoreceptor degeneration). No mechanism for raising ICP or optic disc oedema.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Optic neuritis = disc swelling (papillitis when disc involved). Note: typically unilateral + painful. Papilloedema = typically bilateral + painless.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CRVO causes disc swelling (as part of the fundal picture). Not the same as papilloedema (due to raised ICP) but discs can appear swollen.

questions.keyPoint: PAPILLOEDEMA CAUSES (raised ICP): SOL (space-occupying lesion) + Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) + Hydrocephalus + Venous sinus thrombosis + Meningitis + Malignant hypertension + Sleep apnoea. AMD does NOT cause papilloedema (it's a macular condition).

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 15. Causes of acute red eye include: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 170 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Maculopathy = visual loss, NOT red eye.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: AMD = visual loss, not red eye.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: AACG (ACUTE ANGLE CLOSURE GLAUCOMA) = classic ACUTE RED EYE with: severe pain + nausea/vomiting + mid-dilated fixed pupil + raised IOP + corneal oedema. Redness from ciliary injection.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Optic neuritis = painful EYE MOVEMENT + visual loss. Eye may appear normal externally. Not typically "red eye."

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Astigmatism = refractive error. Not red eye.

questions.keyPoint: ACUTE RED EYE causes: AACG + anterior uveitis + keratitis + conjunctivitis + scleritis/episcleritis + subconjunctival haemorrhage. AACG = ACUTE RED EYE with SEVERE PAIN + N&V; + mid-dilated fixed pupil + corneal oedema = EMERGENCY.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 16. Which of the following is a cause of ptosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BOTULISM causes PTOSIS by blocking acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction → weakness of levator palpebrae superioris + extraocular muscles + other muscles. Also: fixed dilated pupils (blocks ACh at pupillary sphincter = mydriasis).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN VII palsy → ECTROPION (lower lid falls away) + inability to close eye (lagophthalmos). NOT ptosis (ptosis = CN III or Horner or levator dysgenesis).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sjögren's causes dry eyes + dry mouth. Not ptosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Presbyopia = loss of accommodation with age. Not ptosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cataract = lens opacity. Not ptosis.

questions.keyPoint: PTOSIS CAUSES: CN III palsy + Horner's syndrome + Myasthenia gravis + BOTULISM + Congenital (levator dysgenesis) + Mechanical (lid mass). BOTULISM blocks presynaptic ACh release → descending paralysis (ptosis → diplopia → respiratory failure). Fixed dilated pupils in botulism (unlike MG).

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch13. 17. Features of diabetic background retinopathy include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Vitreous haemorrhage = PROLIFERATIVE DR (from new vessels rupturing into vitreous).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BLOT HAEMORRHAGES (deep, rounded haemorrhages in the outer plexiform layer) ARE features of background (non-proliferative) DR. Along with: dot haemorrhages (microaneurysm rupture) + hard exudates + cotton wool spots.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Actually cotton wool spots ARE a feature of background DR (pre-proliferative when multiple). However, file marks BLOT haemorrhages as the correct answer.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Neovascularisation = PROLIFERATIVE DR.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: RP = inherited retinal dystrophy. Not a feature of DR.

questions.keyPoint: BACKGROUND (NON-PROLIFERATIVE) DR: Microaneurysms + DOT haemorrhages + BLOT haemorrhages + Hard exudates + Cotton wool spots (signs of ischaemia). PROLIFERATIVE DR: + Neovascularisation + Vitreous haemorrhage + Traction RD. Blot haemorrhages = deep intraretinal haemorrhages.

questions.stem: 70-YEAR-OLD MAN: gradual visual deterioration. Slit lamp photography shows mature white cataract. True statement about his condition? AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 172 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine The true statement about his condition is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is a need to implant an intraocular lens
choices.explanation: CATARACT: treated by phacoemulsification (removal of opaque lens) + INTRAOCULAR LENS (IOL) IMPLANTATION to restore focusing ability. Without IOL, patient would need very thick glasses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: History of trauma is not relevant
choices.explanation: History of trauma IS relevant — traumatic cataract can develop from blunt/penetrating ocular injury.

choices.label: C
choices.text: His condition is considered very early
choices.explanation: Mature white cataract (totally opaque lens) = ADVANCED cataract, not early.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He could be managed by topical medication as an alternative to surgery
choices.explanation: No proven medical treatment for established cataract. Surgery is the ONLY definitive treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The commonest cause for his problem is steroid use
choices.explanation: Most common cause of cataract overall = AGE (senile). Steroids = posterior subcapsular cataract (iatrogenic cause).

questions.keyPoint: CATARACT: only definitive treatment = phacoemulsification + IOL implantation. No medical treatment proven effective. Most common cause = age (senile). Other causes: diabetes, steroids (PSC), trauma, uveitis, radiation.

questions.stem: Same cataract patient. Best option for treatment of cataract is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cataract removal by intracapsular cataract procedure
choices.explanation: ICCE = old procedure (lens + capsule removed). Risk: vitreous loss, aphakic bullous keratopathy. RARELY used now.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LASIK corrects refractive errors (myopia, hypermetropia, astigmatism). Does NOT treat cataract.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: No proven pharmacological treatment for established cataract.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Glasses cannot correct vision through an opaque cataractous lens. Page 173 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PHACOEMULSIFICATION = GOLD STANDARD modern cataract surgery. Ultrasound probe emulsifies lens through a small (<3mm) incision. IOL is inserted through the intact posterior capsule. Advantages: small incision, fast recovery, no sutures needed in most cases.

questions.keyPoint: PHACOEMULSIFICATION = gold standard cataract surgery. Small incision + ultrasound emulsification + IOL insertion. Complication: posterior capsule opacification (PCO) → treated with Nd:YAG capsulotomy.

questions.stem: 45-YEAR-OLD MALE: high IOP. Optic disc photo shows cup-to-disc ratio of 0.8 (enlarged cup). Identify the FALSE statement. Identify the false statement:

choices.label: A
choices.text: OCT is a valuable imaging tool
choices.explanation: TRUE statement — OCT is very valuable in POAG for assessing RNFL (retinal nerve fibre layer) thickness.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: FALSE STATEMENT (the answer): Raised IOP with large optic cup IS a diagnosis of probable POAG → REQUIRES TREATMENT. NO TREATMENT = progressive optic nerve damage → permanent blindness. POAG management: topical prostaglandin analogues (latanoprost) first-line.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The anterior chamber angle could be open or closed
choices.explanation: TRUE — HIGH IOP does not determine whether angle is open or closed until gonioscopy is performed. POAG has an OPEN angle.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The other eye should be checked
choices.explanation: TRUE — POAG is bilateral (usually asymmetric). Fellow eye must always be examined.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perimetry is needed to assess the visual field
choices.explanation: TRUE — Perimetry (visual field testing) is essential for monitoring glaucoma progression and functional impact.

questions.keyPoint: POAG MANAGEMENT IS NECESSARY — no treatment leads to progressive optic neuropathy and blindness. First-line: PROSTAGLANDIN ANALOGUES (latanoprost). Monitoring: IOP + visual fields (perimetry) + OCT (RNFL). Gonioscopy confirms OPEN angle.

questions.topic: Glaucoma treatment: all of the above
questions.stem: Same POAG patient. How is glaucoma treated:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Medicine alone is correct — but not the best answer if "all of the above" is an option.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Laser trabeculoplasty alone — not complete.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Incorrect — all are used.

choices.label: D
