choices.explanation: GLAUCOMA TREATMENT includes ALL of: (1) MEDICINE (prostaglandin analogues, beta-blockers, CAIs), (2) LASER TRABECULOPLASTY (SLT or ALT — improves aqueous outflow), (3) SURGERY (trabeculectomy, aqueous shunts). Treatment depends on IOP, severity, patient compliance.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Surgery alone — incomplete answer.

questions.keyPoint: GLAUCOMA TREATMENT: Medicine (first-line) → Laser trabeculoplasty → Surgery (trabeculectomy). ALL three modalities are used depending on severity and response. Prostaglandin analogues = most effective first-line IOP-lowering drops.

questions.topic: Background DR: identify sign NOT present
questions.stem: 46-YEAR-OLD DIABETIC: deterioration of vision. Fundoscopy shows dots, plots, microaneurysms, hard exudates, cotton wool spots. Identify sign NOT present. Identify the sign that is NOT present: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 175 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: RETINAL DETACHMENT is NOT a feature of BACKGROUND (non-proliferative) diabetic retinopathy. Background DR features: microaneurysms, dot haemorrhages (intraretinal), hard exudates (lipid deposits), cotton wool spots (ischaemia). Retinal detachment occurs in PROLIFERATIVE DR (from fibrovascular membrane contraction), not in background DR.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: TRUE — this IS the diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hard exudates ARE present in background DR (yellow waxy deposits from lipoprotein leakage).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cotton wool spots ARE present (represent ischaemic areas/nerve fibre layer infarcts).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Dot haemorrhages ARE present in background DR (intraretinal microaneurysm haemorrhages).

questions.keyPoint: BACKGROUND DR features: microaneurysms, dot haemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton wool spots, retinal oedema. RETINAL DETACHMENT = PROLIFERATIVE DR complication (fibrovascular membrane traction). KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: background DR = non-proliferative (no new vessels = no tractional detachment).

questions.stem: 35-YEAR-OLD MAN WITH ANKYLOSING SPONDYLITIS: red eye + photophobia. Clinical photo shows inflammatory cells in anterior chamber. Correct statement about his eye condition:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The anterior chamber sign is called hypopyon
choices.explanation: HYPOPYON = layering of WHITE BLOOD CELLS (pus) in the INFERIOR ANTERIOR CHAMBER = the correct term for the inflammatory cellular exudate in severe anterior uveitis. In HLA-B27 associated uveitis (AS, reactive arthritis), hypopyon can occur.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The anterior chamber sign is called hyphema
choices.explanation: HYPHEMA = BLOOD (RBCs) in anterior chamber from trauma or rubeosis iridis. Not inflammatory cells.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The redness is due to a microbial infection
choices.explanation: Anterior uveitis in AS is AUTOIMMUNE/INFLAMMATORY, not primarily microbial. Page 176 | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pupil size and/or shape is never affected
choices.explanation: Synechiae (adhesions) in uveitis DISTORT the pupil shape (irregular pupil = pathognomonic finding).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TOPICAL STEROIDS are FIRST-LINE treatment for anterior uveitis (reduce inflammation). ATROPINE (mydriatic) is added to prevent posterior synechiae.

questions.keyPoint: ANTERIOR UVEITIS (iritis/iridocyclitis): pain + photophobia + SMALL IRREGULAR PUPIL (synechiae) + CILIARY FLUSH (perilimbal injection) + CELLS AND FLARE in AC. HYPOPYON (white cell layering) in severe cases. HLA-B27 associated: AS, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, IBD. Treatment: TOPICAL STEROIDS + CYCLOPLEGICS.

questions.stem: Diabetic retinopathy — all are TRUE EXCEPT: Diabetic Retinopathy. All are TRUE EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.text: DR is one of common causes of blindness among children of type I diabetes
choices.explanation: Type 1 DM → DR over time. However, DR is more common in adults with long-standing disease; rare in children.

choices.label: B
choices.text: VEGF stimulates new vessel formation and increases vascular permeability
choices.explanation: TRUE — VEGF is the key driver of proliferative DR and macular oedema.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pregnant women with proliferative DR do poorly without treatment, but those who had prior panretinal photocoagulatio n re
choices.explanation: FALSE STATEMENT (the answer): Prior PRP does provide SOME protection during pregnancy, but does NOT guarantee stability. PDR can STILL WORSEN during pregnancy despite prior PRP. This overstatement makes it the false answer. Page 177 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.text: In type I DM, no clinically significant retinopathy in first 5 years
choices.explanation: RUE — DR is rare in the first 5 years of type 1 DM (requires time to develop vascular hanges).

choices.label: E
choices.text: In type II DM, incidence of DR increases with disease duration
choices.explanation: RUE — longer duration → higher DR incidence.

questions.keyPoint: PDR in PREGNANCY: can worsen even with prior PRP. Pregnancy is a risk factor for DR progression (due to hormonal changes, increased blood flow). PRP before pregnancy reduces but does NOT eliminate this risk. The overstatement "remain stable" = the false answer.

questions.stem: In dry eye diseases, increased tear osmolarity — all correct EXCEPT: In dry eye diseases, increased tear osmolarity — all correct EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Increased palpebral fissure width → increased ocular surface exposure → increased evaporation → increased osmolarity. TRUE.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Meibomitis → deficient lipid layer → increased evaporation → increased osmolarity. TRUE.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: FALSE: Punctal stenosis → REDUCED DRAINAGE of tears → tears accumulate longer → DECREASED osmolarity (tears diluted by accumulation). Punctal stenosis causes EPIPHORA, not dry eye with increased osmolarity. In fact it can be used therapeutically (punctal plugs) to treat dry eye.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Decreased corneal sensation (diabetic neuropathy, herpetic) → reduced blink reflex → reduced tear secretion → increased osmolarity. TRUE.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: OCP (mucous membrane pemphigoid) → scar the conjunctiva → damage goblet cells → reduced mucin → aqueous deficient dry eye → increased osmolarity. TRUE.

questions.keyPoint: PUNCTAL STENOSIS → reduced drainage → tears accumulate → DECREASED tear osmolarity (diluted). In dry eye management: PUNCTAL PLUGS (block drainage to retain tears) are USED TO TREAT dry eye. All other options INCREASE tear osmolarity (increased evaporation or decreased secretion).

questions.stem: Treatment of patient with congenital esotropia. Treatment of patient with congenital esotropia:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Prism glasses can help some forms of strabismus but are not the primary treatment for congenital esotropia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lateral rectus recession reduces convergence — WRONG for congenital ESOTROPIA (which is an INWARD deviation). Lateral rectus RESECTION (strengthening) or MEDIAL RECTUS RECESSION is correct.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Orthoptic exercises useful for phorias and accommodative esotropia but not primary treatment for CONGENITAL esotropia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: As above — wrong muscle/direction.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CONGENITAL ESOTROPIA (infantile esotropia): large angle inward deviation → requires BILATERAL MEDIAL RECTUS RECESSION (weakening both medial recti) to straighten the eyes. Surgery performed early (ideally 6-18 months) to allow binocular vision development.

questions.keyPoint: CONGENITAL (INFANTILE) ESOTROPIA: large angle inward deviation (>40 prism dioptres) → BILATERAL MEDIAL RECTUS RECESSION (weakening converging muscles). Early surgery (before 18 months) for best binocular vision outcomes. Accommodative esotropia → glasses first.

questions.stem: Blow out fracture of orbit commonly produces: Blow out fracture of orbit commonly produces: AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 179 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Blowout fracture is specifically of the ORBITAL WALLS (floor/medial), not the nasal bones.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Frontal bone fracture occurs with direct frontal impact. Not typical blowout.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: BLOWOUT FRACTURE = FLOOR of orbit (most commonly). Sudden intraorbital pressure forces the thin floor to fracture into the maxillary sinus. Floor overlies maxillary sinus (roof of sinus = floor of orbit).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Can occur with severe ocular trauma but is NOT the typical result of a blowout fracture specifically.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Septal deviation = nasal bone trauma. Not a typical feature of orbital blowout fracture.

questions.keyPoint: BLOWOUT FRACTURE = ORBITAL FLOOR fracture (into maxillary sinus). Most common wall. Second = medial wall (into ethmoid). Clinical: vertical diplopia + restricted upgaze + enophthalmos + infraorbital numbness. CT orbit (coronal) = gold standard imaging.

questions.topic: Choroidal melanoma vs metastatic: FALSE statement
questions.stem: Choroidal melanomas and Metastatic Tumors vary in several ways. Which is FALSE? Choroidal melanomas and Metastatic Tumors — which statement is FALSE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Treatment for metastatic tumors and choroidal melanomas is the same
choices.explanation: Treatment differs: choroidal melanoma → local (plaque brachytherapy, proton beam, enucleation). Metastatic → systemic chemotherapy + targeted therapy for primary.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They differ in age of presentation
choices.explanation: TRUE — melanomas typically present at an older age (5th-6th decade). Metastases present at any age depending on the primary.

choices.label: C
choices.text: B-scan: Metastatic Tumor has HIGH internal reflectivity; choroidal melanoma has LOW
choices.explanation: TRUE — choroidal MELANOMA has LOW internal reflectivity on B-scan (characteristic). METASTATIC tumour has HIGH internal reflectivity (from tissue disorganisation). Page 180 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Metastatic tumors are usually multifocal and multilobular while choroidal melanomas are singular
choices.explanation: TRUE — melanomas usually UNILATERAL and SINGULAR; metastases tend to be BILATERAL and MULTIFOCAL.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: FALSE STATEMENT (the answer): Choroidal melanomas are UNILATERAL (arising from uveal melanocytes). Metastatic tumours are more commonly BILATERAL (haematogenous spread to both eyes). So they are NOT BOTH commonly bilateral.

questions.keyPoint: CHOROIDAL MELANOMA: UNILATERAL, singular, LOW internal reflectivity on B-scan, LOW internal reflectivity, treated locally. METASTATIC ocular tumour: BILATERAL + multifocal + HIGH reflectivity + breast/lung primary most common. The FALSE statement = "Both commonly bilateral."

questions.stem: Mastoiditis is: Mastoiditis is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Usually associated with subtotal perforation of TM
choices.explanation: ASOM → acute mastoiditis can occur WITHOUT TM perforation (the infection spreads through the mastoid cortex).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive fistula test is early sign
choices.explanation: Positive fistula test = perilymphatic fistula (in CHRONIC disease with labyrinthine fistula). NOT an early sign of mastoiditis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Most cases of acute mastoiditis resolve with IV antibiotics. Surgery (mastoidectomy/myringotomy) only if: no response to antibiotics, subperiosteal abscess, intracranial complications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Maximum area to elicit tenderness is over the tragus
choices.explanation: Mastoid tenderness = OVER THE MASTOID PROCESS (behind the ear). Tragus tenderness = otitis externa.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: ACUTE MASTOIDITIS: predominantly a disease of INFANTS AND CHILDREN (following ASOM). Peak age 2 years. Can also occur in adults but less commonly. Post-auricular swelling + auricle displaced anteroinferiorly. Page 181 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

questions.keyPoint: ACUTE MASTOIDITIS: predominantly in INFANTS AND CHILDREN (following ASOM). Post-auricular swelling + erythema + tenderness OVER MASTOID PROCESS + auricle displaced ANTEROINFERIORLY. Treatment: IV antibiotics ± myringotomy ± cortical mastoidectomy (if abscess/complications).

questions.stem: All are recognized causes of entropion EXCEPT: All are recognized causes for entropion EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Trachoma (chlamydial scarring of tarsal conjunctiva) = CICATRICIAL ENTROPION. Important cause globally.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Blepharospasm/orbicularis spasm = SPASTIC ENTROPION.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CONGENITAL ENTROPION exists (rare) from developmental deficiency.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Involutional (senile) entropion = most common type in elderly from horizontal lid laxity.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Facial nerve palsy causes ECTROPION (eyelid turns OUTWARD — orbicularis weakness → lid sags outward). NOT entropion (inward turning). ENTROPION causes: cicatricial (trachoma, OCP), involutional (senile), spastic, congenital. ECTROPION causes: CN VII palsy (paralytic), involutional, cicatricial.

questions.keyPoint: FACIAL NERVE PALSY → ECTROPION (lid turns OUT — weak orbicularis = no lid muscle support). ENTROPION (lid turns IN) causes: Involutional (most common), Cicatricial (trachoma, OCP, chemical burns), Spastic (blepharospasm), Congenital. Key: CN VII palsy = ECTROPION not ENTROpion.

questions.stem: Young child: fever + sore throat + follicles in lower palpebral conjunctiva + tender preauricular lymph nodes. Most probable diagnosis? Most probable diagnosis:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ADENOVIRAL CONJUNCTIVITIS: classic features = follicles in palpebral conjunctiva (lymphoid follicle reaction = URTI precursor) + PREAURICULAR LYMPHADENOPATHY (tender) + watery discharge + associated URTI (fever + sore throat). Highly contagious. Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC) = severe form with corneal involvement.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Trachoma: SUPERIOR TARSAL follicles (not inferior) + scarring + pannus + Herbert pits = CHRONIC chlamydial disease in endemic areas. No fever/sore throat in this way.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Allergic conjunctivitis: itching + chemosis + papillae (not follicles) + bilateral + no preauricular LN.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Gonococcal: profuse purulent HYPERACUTE discharge + chemosis + lid oedema. Not this presentation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Staphylococcal: mucopurulent discharge + bilateral blepharitis. No preauricular LN or follicles.

questions.keyPoint: ADENOVIRAL CONJUNCTIVITIS: follicles (palpebral conjunctiva) + PREAURICULAR LYMPHADENOPATHY + watery discharge + associated URTI. Highly contagious. NO antivirals effective. Self-limiting 2-3 weeks. Treat: artificial tears, cold compresses. ISOLATE to prevent spread.

questions.stem: Type of laser used in photorefractive keratectomy (PRK): Type of laser used in photorefractive keratectomy (PRK):

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Krypton laser = used for retinal photocoagulation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Argon laser = diabetic retinopathy (panretinal photocoagulation), trabeculoplasty. NOT for refractive surgery.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CO2 laser = dermatology (warts, skin resurfacing), ENT (removal of papillomata). NOT for corneal refractive surgery. Page 183 University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: EXCIMER LASER (argon fluoride, 193 nm UV) = used in both LASIK and PRK for corneal ablation (photoablation of stromal tissue to reshape the cornea and correct refractive errors). Cold laser — does not cause thermal damage. PRK = ablation of SURFACE epithelium + stroma. LASIK = ablation under a flap.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Nd:YAG = posterior capsulotomy (cataracts), iridotomy (AACG), vitreous strands. NOT for refractive surgery.

questions.keyPoint: EXCIMER LASER (193 nm) = used in PRK and LASIK (corneal refractive surgery). Nd:YAG = capsulotomy + iridotomy. Argon = retinal photocoagulation + trabeculoplasty. CO2 = ENT/dermatology. Krypton = retinal.

questions.stem: MOTHER brings her 9-YEAR-OLD SON with CONSTANTLY ABNORMAL-LOOKING EYES. Clinical photo shows RIGHT UPPER EYELID PTOSIS. What is the abnormality in his eyes?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pseudoptosis = apparent ptosis from reduced orbital volume (e.g., phthisis bulbi, microphthalmos) or contralateral lid retraction. Not consistent with the clinical photo showing true ptosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The photo shows RIGHT UPPER EYELID PTOSIS — the right eyelid droops and covers part of the pupil. The left eye appears normal. The term ptosis = true upper eyelid droop from levator/Mueller muscle dysfunction.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Esotropia = inward deviation of the eye. The photos shows eyelid ptosis, not an ocular misalignment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lid retraction = upper lid too elevated (thyroid eye disease). The LEFT lid appears normal here.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Placeholder.

questions.keyPoint: RIGHT PTOSIS in a child: eyelid droops from levator dysfunction. Most common cause in children = CONGENITAL (levator dysgenesis). Assessment: MRD1 (severity) + levator function (surgical planning). Urgent treatment if pupil covered (amblyopia risk).

questions.topic: Most common cause of right ptosis in 9-year-old
questions.stem: Same 9-year-old with right ptosis. What is the most common cause for this abnormality? (*Related to previous scenario)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CONGENITAL PTOSIS = most common cause in children. Due to maldevelopment (dysgenesis) of the levator palpebrae superioris muscle. Absent eyelid crease + present from birth/infancy. Family history may be present. Levator function varies.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CN III palsy causes ptosis WITH dilated pupil + ophthalmoplegia (eye in down and out position). In a 9-year-old, CN III palsy from trauma, intracranial tumour, or demyelination. Not the MOST COMMON cause.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diabetic CN III palsy = pupil-sparing ptosis in ADULTS (not children). Not the most common cause in a 9-year-old.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Post-traumatic ptosis can occur but the MOST COMMON cause of childhood ptosis is congenital.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Placeholder.

questions.keyPoint: Most common cause of PTOSIS IN CHILDREN = CONGENITAL (levator palpebrae superioris dysgenesis). Absent eyelid crease = pathognomonic. CN III palsy = ptosis + dilated pupil + ophthalmoplegia. Horner = ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis. Myasthenia = variable, worse with fatigue.

questions.stem: Same 9-year-old with congenital right ptosis. What treatment options are involved? (*Related to previous scenario) AMBOSS-Style | High-Yield Medical Education Page 185 ENT & Ophthalmology — Complete MCQ Study Guide | University of Kufa, College of Medicine

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Glasses do not correct ptosis (they correct refractive errors). Glasses may be needed for associated anisometropic amblyopia but do not lift the eyelid.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medication to improve levator palpebrae superioris strength
choices.explanation: No pharmacological agent reliably strengthens the levator in congenital ptosis. Phenylephrine eye drops can temporarily raise the lid (via Müller's muscle stimulation — used diagnostically to predict success of Müller muscle-conjunctival resection) but not for long-term treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LEVATOR RESECTION (levator advancement/resection) = treatment for congenital ptosis with ADEQUATE LEVATOR FUNCTION (>4 mm). At age 9, if LF is adequate, levator resection is the appropriate surgical treatment. If LF poor (<4 mm) → frontalis sling.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Medial rectus recession treats esotropia (inward squint). No role in ptosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Placeholder.

questions.keyPoint: TREATMENT: Levator resection (if LF >4 mm) or Frontalis sling (if LF <4 mm). At age 9, amblyopia risk is less critical than in infants, but surgery still important for visual function and cosmesis. Post-op: may need amblyopia therapy.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch.13 — Ophthalmology — Single Best Answer Questions. 1. Drug known to cause visual impairment as side effect:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CHLOROQUINE/hydroxychloroquine → RETINAL TOXICITY (bull's eye maculopathy). Dose-dependent. Monitor: annual OCT + Humphrey visual field (10-2). Gentamicin → SNHL (ototoxic not vision). Bendroflumethiazide, metformin, atorvastatin → not primarily visual impairment. Distractor.

questions.keyPoint: Chloroquine: CHLOROQUINE/hydroxychloroquine → RETINAL TOXICITY (bull's eye maculopathy). Dose-dependent. Monitor: annual OCT + Humphrey visual field (10-2). Gentamicin → SNHL (ototoxic not vision). Bendroflumethiazide, metformin, atorvastatin → not primarily visual impairment.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch.13 — Ophthalmology — Single Best Answer Questions. 2. Which extraocular muscle is correctly paired with cranial nerve?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: EOM + CN: SR/IR/MR/IO = CN III; SO = CN IV (mnemonic: SO4 = superior oblique =

questions.keyPoint: Superior rectus — CN III: EOM + CN: SR/IR/MR/IO = CN III; SO = CN IV (mnemonic: SO4 = superior

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch.13 — Ophthalmology — Single Best Answer Questions. 3. True statement about refractive errors:

choices.label: A
choices.text: e) Hypermetropia is a cause of childhood squint
choices.explanation: Hypermetropia → accommodative esotropia (most common childhood squint type). Myopia treated with CONCAVE (diverging) lenses. Astigmatism IS correctable with cylindrical lenses. Presbyopia = progressive LOSS of accommodation with age (not short-sightedness). Page 187 University of Kufa, College of Medicine istractor.

questions.keyPoint: Hypermetropia is a cause of childhood squint: Hypermetropia → accommodative esotropia (most common childhood squint type). Myopia treated with CONCAVE (diverging) lenses. Astigmatism IS correctable with cylindrical lenses. Presbyopia = progressive LOSS of accommodation with age (not short-sightedness).

flag.note: Answer not cleanly captured from the source table — verify the key.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch.13 — Ophthalmology — Single Best Answer Questions. 4. Risk factor for open-angle glaucoma:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: POAG risk factors: RAISED IOP (most important modifiable), age >40, family history, Afro-Caribbean ethnicity, myopia, DIABETES MELLITUS, thin cornea (central corneal thickness <555 µm). Hypermetropia = risk for ANGLE-CLOSURE glaucoma (not POAG). Caucasian origin = lower risk. Distractor.

questions.keyPoint: Diabetes mellitus: POAG risk factors: RAISED IOP (most important modifiable), age >40, family history, Afro-Caribbean ethnicity, myopia, DIABETES MELLITUS, thin cornea (central corneal thickness <555 µm). Hypermetropia = risk for ANGLE-CLOSURE glaucoma (not POAG). Caucasian origin = lower risk.

flag.note: Answer not cleanly captured from the source table — verify the key.

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch.13 — Ophthalmology — Single Best Answer Questions. 5. Causes of GRADUAL visual loss include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Gradual visual loss: cataract, ARMD, glaucoma, diabetic maculopathy, chronic retinal detachment. ACUTE visual loss: CRAO, CRVO, optic neuritis, GCA, vitreous haemorrhage, acute retinal detachment. Retinal artery embolism/GCA = ACUTE not gradual. Distractor.

questions.keyPoint: Macular degeneration: Gradual visual loss: cataract, ARMD, glaucoma, diabetic maculopathy, chronic retinal detachment. ACUTE visual loss: CRAO, CRVO, optic neuritis, GCA, vitreous haemorrhage, acute retinal detachment. Retinal artery embolism/GCA = ACUTE not gradual.

choices.label: A

questions.stem: Davidson's Ch.13 — Ophthalmology — Single Best Answer Questions. 17. Features of diabetic background retinopathy include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Background DR: MICROANEURYSMS (earliest) + DOT/BLOT haemorrhages (intraretinal) + HARD EXUDATES (yellow, waxy lipoprotein deposits) + COTTON WOOL SPOTS (ischaemic nerve fibre layer). Vitreous haemorrhage + new vessel formation = PROLIFERATIVE DR (not background). Retinal neovascularisation = PDR. Distractor.

Vitreous haemorrhage + new vessel formation = PROLIFERATIVE DR (not background). Retinal neovascularisation = PDR.

choices.explanation: EVISCERATION: removes the CORNEA and all INTRAOCULAR CONTENTS (lens, vitreous, uvea, retina) while leaving the SCLERAL SHELL and extraocular muscles intact. Implant placed inside the scleral shell. Advantages: maintains orbital volume, good prosthesis motility, preserves sympathetic ophthalmia risk (controversial). Differs from enucleation (entire globe removed).

questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 3. Red eye (2): 64yo man, 1-week erythematous vesicular rash on right side of face + forehead, painful red right eye. Slit lamp: discrete GREEN PATCH under blue light. Most likely cause?

HUTCHINSON SIGN (vesicles on nose tip) = CN V1 nasociliary branch involved → HIGH RISK of ocular involvement. Treatment: IV/oral aciclovir.

questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 5. Paediatric ophthalmology: 9yo boy, red BULGING non-moving right eye + headache + pain on eye movement + multiple episodes sinusitis + fever 39°C + PROPTOSIS + OPHTHALMOPLEGIA right + sinuses tender + eyelids erythematous. choices.text: D) Admit for high-dose intravenous antibiotics
choices.explanation: ORBITAL CELLULITIS (post-septal) from ethmoid sinusitis: proptosis + ophthalmoplegia + reduced VA + fever. Medical emergency. Admit + HIGH-DOSE IV ANTIBIOTICS (ceftriaxone + metronidazole). CT orbit to exclude subperiosteal/orbital abscess (if present → surgical drainage). Complications: cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, loss of vision.

questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 6. Nerve palsy and eye: 64yo woman, droopy LEFT eyelid + smoker 40 pack years + small reactive left pupil + ABSENCE OF SWEATING on left side of forehead (anhidrosis) + no neuromuscular disorder. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.explanation: HORNER'S SYNDROME: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis (± enophthalmos). Cause: interruption of OCULOSYMPATHETIC PATHWAY. In a HEAVY SMOKER = PANCOAST TUMOUR (apical lung tumour compressing the sympathetic chain at stellate ganglion level) until proven otherwise. Investigation: CXR + CT thorax. Other causes: carotid dissection, cervical cord lesion.

questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 10. Ocular trauma (1): 12yo boy, playing squash, ball hit left eye, double vision + reduced VA left + IRREGULAR LEFT PUPIL poorly reactive + restricted eye movements + reduced sensation below left eye + blood from ipsilateral nose + subconjunctival haemorrhage. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ORBITAL BLOWOUT FRACTURE: squash ball (fits orbit rim → transmits force to floor/medial wall) → floor fracture → inferior rectus entrapment (restricted movements + vertical diplopia) + infraorbital nerve numbness (below left eye = V2) + blood in nose (from sinus) + subconjunctival haemorrhage. Irregular pupil may suggest associated iris sphincter damage.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Distractor.

questions.topic: Chemical ocular injury: most accurate statement
questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 11. Ocular trauma (2): most accurate statement regarding chemical ocular injury?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ALKALI burns = MORE SEVERE than acid burns. Alkali → LIQUEFACTIVE NECROSIS (no coagulative barrier) → penetrates deep into eye → iritis, cataract, glaucoma, corneal perforation. Acid → COAGULATIVE NECROSIS (protein precipitate limits penetration). Both: IRRIGATE IMMEDIATELY with copious water/saline for 20-30 minutes. pH testing to confirm neutralisation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Distractor.

questions.keyPoint: Alkali are especially damaging (liquefactive necrosis): ALKALI burns = MORE SEVERE than acid burns. Alkali → LIQUEFACTIVE NECROSIS (no coagulative barrier) → penetrates deep into eye → iritis, cataract, glaucoma, corneal perforation. Acid → COAGULATIVE NECROSIS (protein precipitate limits penetration). Both: IRRIGATE IMMEDIATELY with copious water/saline for 20-30 minutes. pH testing to confirm neutralisation.

questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 12. Ocular trauma (3): 20yo man, RTA, GCS 4/15, deep horizontal laceration at INFERIOR ASPECT OF LOWER EYELID. Structure most likely damaged?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: INFERIOR ASPECT OF LOWER EYELID laceration: the INFERIOR OBLIQUE MUSCLE runs along the inferior orbital floor from the anteromedial orbit to the inferolateral globe. A deep horizontal laceration of the lower eyelid can damage the inferior oblique (causing impaired extorsion/elevation). Other structures at risk: lacrimal canaliculi (medial laceration).

questions.topic: Carotid duplex for amaurosis fugax + cherry-red spot
questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 15. Acute visual disturbance (3): 48yo man, sudden-onset vision loss left eye which RESOLVED (amaurosis fugax). Smoker + TIA + angina. Fundoscopy: retinal infarcts + CHERRY-RED SPOT at macula. Most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Distractor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: D) Carotid duplex and oral aspirin
choices.explanation: CENTRAL RETINAL ARTERY OCCLUSION (CRAO) + amaurosis fugax in a patient with vascular risk factors (smoking, TIA, angina) = CAROTID ATHEROSCLEROSIS as the SOURCE. Management: CAROTID DUPLEX (assess for carotid stenosis) + ORAL ASPIRIN (antiplatelet therapy). Urgent cardiology/neurology referral. Cherry-red spot = macula retains colour from choroidal supply while surrounding retina becomes oedematous/white.

Cherry-red spot = macula retains colour from choroidal supply while surrounding retina becomes oedematous/white.

questions.stem: Surgery ■■■■■ — Ophthalmology Clinical Cases. 18. Headache: 77yo woman, 1-week constant ache right side of head, VERY TENDER scalp, similar episode last year left side caused COMPLETE BLINDNESS in left eye. No right eye disturbance currently. Next recommendation?

choices.explanation: Distractor.

START HIGH-DOSE PREDNISOLONE (1g IV methylprednisolone × 3 days OR 60-80mg prednisolone oral) IMMEDIATELY to prevent blindness in the remaining right eye. Do NOT wait for biopsy result. choices.text: E) Laser trabeculoplasty is rarely indicated
choices.explanation: FALSE STATEMENT: Laser trabeculoplasty (SLT = selective laser trabeculoplasty) IS WIDELY USED in POAG management — either as first-line alternative to drops OR as adjunct when drops are insufficient.
